title: Digital Electronics
description: Digital electronics from scratch (beginner rework in progress): 14 sub-topics from number systems through HDL and FPGAs. Beginner-first readings, diagrams, tiered easy to hard.

lessons.title: What Is Binary?
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Flip a light switch. It is either ON or OFF — there is no "half on." Computers are built from billions of tiny switches just like this, and that is the whole reason they speak a language with only two symbols.

**Build.** That two-symbol language is called **binary** ("bi" means two). The only symbols allowed are **0** and **1**. Each single 0 or 1 is called a **bit**, short for *binary digit*. Think of 0 as "switch off" and 1 as "switch on."

Why only two? Because a circuit can tell "off" from "on" very reliably — much more reliably than it could tell ten different voltage levels apart. Two states are easy to build and hard to confuse, so engineers picked two.

Here is the surprising part: *everything* inside a computer is built from these bits. Numbers, the letters in this sentence, photos, music, video — all of it is stored as long strings of 0s and 1s. The number system you grew up with is called **decimal** ("deci" means ten) and uses ten symbols, 0 through 9, because humans have ten fingers. Computers simply count with two symbols instead of ten.

**Recap.** Binary is counting with just two symbols, 0 and 1, because a circuit is naturally off or on. One such symbol is a bit. Everything a computer stores is made of bits.

items.prompt: How many different symbols does the binary number system use?
items.explanation: Binary uses exactly two symbols: 0 and 1. The name 'binary' comes from 'bi', meaning two. Decimal (everyday numbers) is the system that uses ten symbols, 0 through 9.

items.prompt: What is a single 0 or 1 called?
choices: A byte
A bit
A digit
A nibble
items.explanation: A single binary 0 or 1 is a 'bit', short for 'binary digit'. A byte is a group of 8 bits, and a nibble is a group of 4, so those are collections of bits, not a single one.

items.prompt: Why do computers use just two states instead of ten?
choices: Two states are harder to build but cheaper
A circuit can tell 'off' from 'on' very reliably
Binary numbers are always shorter than decimal
It is the only system that can store text
items.explanation: A circuit reliably distinguishes two states (off/on) far better than ten separate voltage levels. Two clear states are easy to build and hard to confuse. Binary numbers are actually longer than decimal, and any system can store text.

items.prompt: In the light-switch analogy for a bit, what does the value 1 represent?
choices: The switch is off
The switch is on
The switch is broken
The switch is half-on
items.explanation: We treat 1 as 'on' and 0 as 'off', just like a light switch. There is no 'half-on' state; that is exactly why two clean states are so useful to a computer.

items.prompt: Which of these is stored inside a computer as bits (0s and 1s)?
choices: Only whole numbers
Only text
Numbers, text, images, and sound
Only images and sound
items.explanation: Everything in a computer is built from bits: numbers, text, images, sound, and video alike. There is no special separate storage for any one of them; it is all 0s and 1s underneath.

items.prompt: The everyday number system you learned in school uses ten symbols (0-9). What is it called?
choices: Binary
Decimal
Hexadecimal
Octal
items.explanation: The everyday ten-symbol system is 'decimal' ('deci' means ten). Binary uses two symbols, octal eight, and hexadecimal sixteen.

items.prompt: Which of these is a valid binary number?
items.explanation: A binary number may contain only 0s and 1s, so 1011 is valid. The others all contain digits (2 or 9) that are not allowed in binary.

items.prompt: The word 'bit' is short for what?
choices: Binary integer
Basic information token
Binary digit
Byte item
items.explanation: 'Bit' is short for 'binary digit'. It is the smallest unit of data, holding a single 0 or 1.

lessons.title: Place Value & Counting in Binary
lessons.content:
**Hook.** In the number 327, the 3 is worth far more than the 7. Position matters: the 3 sits in the "hundreds" spot. Binary works the same way — only the spots are worth different amounts.

**Build.** In decimal, each spot to the left is worth **ten times** more: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands. That is why we call it base-10. In binary, each spot to the left is worth **two times** more: ones, twos, fours, eights, sixteens. That is why it is called base-2.

So the binary places, reading from the right, are: $1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, \\dots$ — each one double the last. To read a binary number, you add up the place values wherever there is a 1 (a switch that is on) and ignore the 0s.

Example: read $1011$. The places are $8, 4, 2, 1$. The bits are on, off, on, on. So add $8 + 2 + 1 = 11$.

**Counting** in binary is just like a car odometer with only two symbols. Start at $0$, then $1$. Now you are out of symbols, so the next number rolls over to $10$ (that is binary for "two"), then $11$ (three), then $100$ (four). Each time a column runs past 1, it resets to 0 and bumps the column to its left.

**Recap.** Each binary place is worth double the one to its right ($1, 2, 4, 8, \\dots$). Read a number by adding the place values where there is a 1. Counting rolls a column over to the next once it passes 1.

items.prompt: In binary, each place value to the left is worth how much compared to the one on its right?
choices: The same
Twice as much
Ten times as much
Half as much
items.explanation: Binary is base-2, so each place to the left is worth twice (double) the place to its right: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16. Decimal is the system where each place is worth ten times more.

items.prompt: Reading from the right, what are the first four binary place values?
items.explanation: Each binary place doubles, so from the right they are 1, 2, 4, 8. The list 1, 10, 100, 1000 is decimal places; 1, 3, 9, 27 doubles by three (base-3); and 2, 4, 6, 8 just adds two each time, which is not how place value works.

items.prompt: What is the decimal value of the binary number 0110?
items.explanation: The places are 8, 4, 2, 1. The 1s sit in the 4 and 2 columns, so add 4 + 2 = 6. The 8 and 1 columns hold 0 and contribute nothing.

items.prompt: What is the decimal value of the binary number 1001?
items.explanation: Places are 8, 4, 2, 1. The 1s are in the 8 and 1 columns, so 8 + 1 = 9. A common mistake is reading it as ten by treating it like decimal 'one-thousand-and-one'.

items.prompt: When counting in binary, what comes immediately after 1?
items.explanation: Binary has no symbol '2'. After 1 you run out of symbols, so the column rolls over to the next place: the next number is 10 (which means two). This is just like 9 rolling over to 10 in decimal.

items.prompt: How is the number three written in binary?
items.explanation: Counting goes 0, 1, 10 (two), 11 (three). In 11 the 2 and 1 columns are both on, and 2 + 1 = 3. There is no symbol '3' in binary, and 100 is four.

items.prompt: What is the largest number you can write with 4 bits, and what is it in decimal?
items.explanation: All four bits on is 1111. Adding the places 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 15. People often guess 16, but that would need a fifth bit (10000); with 4 bits the values run 0 through 15, which is 16 different values.

items.prompt: Which binary number is larger: 0101 or 0011?
choices: 0101 (it equals 5)
0011 (it equals 5)
They are equal
0011 (it equals 6)
items.explanation: 0101 = 4 + 1 = 5, and 0011 = 2 + 1 = 3, so 0101 is larger. As in decimal, a 1 sitting in a higher place value makes a number bigger.

lessons.title: Converting Between Binary and Decimal
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You already turned binary into decimal in the last lesson by adding up place values. Now let us make both directions a reliable habit, and name the groups of bits we use every day.

**Build.** **Binary to decimal** is the easy direction: write the place values ($\\dots 16, 8, 4, 2, 1$) above the bits and add the ones where a bit is on. For $1101$: places $8, 4, 2, 1$, bits on-on-off-on, so $8 + 4 + 1 = 13$.

**Decimal to binary** uses a simple trick: keep dividing by 2 and write down the remainder each time (always 0 or 1). Then read the remainders *bottom to top*. Convert $13$:
- $13 \\div 2 = 6$ remainder **1**
- $6 \\div 2 = 3$ remainder **0**
- $3 \\div 2 = 1$ remainder **1**
- $1 \\div 2 = 0$ remainder **1**

Reading bottom to top gives $1101$. (A second method: subtract the largest place value you can, and repeat.)

**Naming groups of bits.** A single bit is one 0 or 1. Four bits together make a **nibble**. Eight bits make a **byte** — the everyday unit of computer storage, and enough to hold one keyboard character. A **word** is whatever group size a particular chip works with most naturally, often 16, 32, or 64 bits.

**Recap.** Binary to decimal: add the on place values. Decimal to binary: divide by 2 and read remainders bottom-up. Bits group into nibbles (4), bytes (8), and words (the chip's natural size).

items.prompt: Convert binary 1101 to decimal.
items.explanation: Places 8, 4, 2, 1 with bits on-on-off-on: 8 + 4 + 1 = 13. The 2 column is off so it adds nothing.

items.prompt: How many bits are in a byte?
items.explanation: A byte is 8 bits. Four bits is a nibble (half a byte), and 16 or more bits is usually called a word, depending on the chip.

items.prompt: How many bits are in a nibble?
items.explanation: A nibble is 4 bits, which is exactly half of a byte. The playful name comes from a 'nibble' being a small bite of a 'byte'.

items.prompt: To convert a decimal number to binary by the division method, what do you do?
choices: Multiply by 2 and keep the whole part
Divide by 2 repeatedly and read the remainders bottom to top
Divide by 10 and read remainders
Add 2 until you reach the number
items.explanation: Repeatedly divide by 2, recording each remainder (0 or 1), then read the remainders from bottom to top. Dividing by 10 would relate to decimal, not binary.

items.prompt: What is decimal 6 written in binary?
items.explanation: 6 = 4 + 2, so the 4 and 2 columns are on and the 1 column is off: 110. By division, 6/2=3 r0, 3/2=1 r1, 1/2=0 r1, read bottom-up = 110.

items.prompt: What is decimal 10 written in binary?
items.explanation: 10 = 8 + 2, so the 8 and 2 columns are on: 1010. The choice 1001 would be 8 + 1 = 9, and 1100 would be 8 + 4 = 12.

items.prompt: How many different values can a single byte (8 bits) represent?
items.explanation: Eight bits give 2 to the 8th power = 256 different combinations, representing values 0 through 255. People often say 255 (the largest value) instead of 256 (the count of values); the count is one more because 0 is included.

items.prompt: What does the term 'word' mean in computing?
choices: Always exactly 8 bits
A group of letters stored as text
The bit-group size a particular chip works with naturally
Always exactly 16 bits
items.explanation: A word is the natural bit-group size of a given processor, commonly 16, 32, or 64 bits. It is not a fixed number across all machines, and it is unrelated to text words.

lessons.title: Hexadecimal: A Shorthand for Bits
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Long strings of 0s and 1s are exhausting to read and easy to miscopy. Is $11010110$ the same as $11011010$? Hard to tell at a glance. Humans invented a shorthand to make bits easier to handle: **hexadecimal**.

**Build.** Hexadecimal ("hex" for short) is base-16: it uses sixteen symbols. The first ten are the familiar $0$ through $9$, and then we borrow letters for ten through fifteen: **A = 10, B = 11, C = 12, D = 13, E = 14, F = 15.**

Here is why hex is so handy: $16 = 2 \\times 2 \\times 2 \\times 2$, which is four 2s. That means **one hex symbol stands for exactly four bits** (one nibble). So you can read binary in tidy four-bit chunks instead of one long blur.

To turn binary into hex, split the bits into groups of four from the right, then translate each group:
$$1011\\,0111 \\rightarrow 1011 = \\text{B}, \\quad 0111 = 7 \\rightarrow \\text{B7}$$
To go back, expand each hex symbol into its four bits. Hex values are often written with a "0x" in front, like $\\texttt{0xB7}$, so you know it is hex and not decimal.

**Recap.** Hex is base-16 (0-9 then A-F). Because $16 = 2^4$, each hex symbol equals exactly four bits, making it a compact, less error-prone way for humans to write long bit strings.

items.prompt: Hexadecimal is which base (how many symbols)?
choices: Base-2
Base-8
Base-10
Base-16
items.explanation: Hexadecimal is base-16, using sixteen symbols: 0-9 plus A, B, C, D, E, F. Base-2 is binary, base-8 is octal, and base-10 is decimal.

items.prompt: What decimal value does the hex symbol A represent?
items.explanation: In hex, letters continue counting past 9: A = 10, B = 11, and so on up to F = 15. So A is ten.

items.prompt: How many bits does a single hexadecimal symbol stand for?
items.explanation: Because 16 = 2 to the 4th power, one hex symbol maps to exactly 4 bits (one nibble). This is the whole reason hex is a convenient shorthand for binary.

items.prompt: What is the largest single hex symbol, and what decimal value is it?
items.explanation: The symbols stop at F, which equals 15 (the largest value four bits can hold, 1111). There is no symbol G, and F is 15 rather than 16.

items.prompt: Convert the binary number 1011 0111 to hexadecimal.
choices: 0xB7
0x7B
0xA7
0xB8
items.explanation: Split into nibbles: 1011 = B (eleven) and 0111 = 7. Read left to right that is B7. Swapping the order would give 7B, a different value.

items.prompt: Expand the hex digit C into 4 bits.
items.explanation: C equals 12 in decimal, which is 8 + 4 = 1100 in binary. 1010 is 10 (A) and 1110 is 14 (E).

items.prompt: Why is hexadecimal a popular shorthand for humans?
choices: It uses fewer total digits than binary for the same value and groups bits in fours
It is the only base a computer can store
It uses just two symbols like binary
Computers store numbers directly in hex
items.explanation: Hex packs four bits into one symbol, so long bit strings become short and easy to read without miscopying. Computers themselves still store everything as binary; hex is just a human-friendly view of it.

items.prompt: The byte 1111 1111 is written in hex as what?
choices: 0xFF
0xF0
items.explanation: Each nibble 1111 is F (fifteen), so eight ones become FF, written 0xFF. That equals 255 in decimal, the largest value one byte can hold.

lessons.title: Binary Addition & Subtraction
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You already know how to add by hand in decimal, carrying a 1 when a column overflows. Binary addition works exactly the same way — it is actually *simpler*, because there are only a few rules to remember.

**Build.** Add binary numbers column by column from the right, just like decimal. The rules are:
- $0 + 0 = 0$
- $0 + 1 = 1$
- $1 + 1 = 10$ (write **0**, carry **1** to the next column)
- $1 + 1 + 1 = 11$ (write **1**, carry **1**)

The only new idea is that $1 + 1$ already fills the column and rolls over, because binary runs out of symbols after 1. Example, $0111 + 0011$ (that is $7 + 3$):
$$0111 + 0011 = 1010$$
Reading $1010$ back as decimal: $8 + 2 = 10$. Correct!

**Subtraction** also mirrors decimal: when the top digit is smaller, you **borrow** from the column to the left. In binary, borrowing turns a $0$ into a $10$ (worth two) in that column. For example $100 - 1$: borrow across to get $011$, which is $3$ — exactly $4 - 1$.

(In practice, computers usually subtract by *adding a negative number* instead of borrowing. That clever trick is the next lesson.)

**Recap.** Binary addition carries whenever a column reaches two ($1 + 1 = 10$). Subtraction borrows from the left, where a borrow is worth two. The process is the same column-by-column method you already use in decimal.

items.prompt: In binary, what is 1 + 1?
items.explanation: Binary has no symbol '2', so 1 + 1 rolls over: you write 0 and carry 1, giving 10 (which means two). Writing it as 2 would be using a decimal symbol that binary does not have.

items.prompt: When adding a binary column gives 1 + 1, what happens?
choices: Write 1, carry 0
Write 0, carry 1
Write 2, carry nothing
Write 1, carry 1
items.explanation: 1 + 1 = 10 in binary, so you write the 0 in that column and carry the 1 into the next column to the left, just like carrying in decimal addition.

items.prompt: What is 0111 + 0011 in binary?
items.explanation: This is 7 + 3 = 10. Adding column by column with carries gives 1010, and 1010 reads back as 8 + 2 = 10. Correct.

items.prompt: If a column has 1 + 1 plus an incoming carry of 1, what do you write and carry?
choices: Write 1, carry 1
Write 0, carry 1
Write 1, carry 0
Write 0, carry 0
items.explanation: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3, which is 11 in binary: you write 1 in the column and carry 1 to the next. This happens whenever three 1s meet in a column.

items.prompt: What is 101 + 011 in binary?
items.explanation: This is 5 + 3 = 8. Working the columns: 1+1=10 (write 0 carry 1), 0+1+1=10 (write 0 carry 1), 1+0+1=10 (write 0 carry 1), final carry gives 1000 = 8.

items.prompt: When subtracting in binary and the top bit is 0 but you need to subtract 1, what do you do?
choices: Skip the column
Borrow from the column to the left, making this column worth two
Add a 1 to the answer
Carry instead of borrow
items.explanation: Just like decimal subtraction, you borrow from the next column to the left. In binary that borrow is worth two in the current column, letting you complete the subtraction.

items.prompt: What is 100 - 1 in binary?
items.explanation: 100 is 4 and subtracting 1 gives 3, which is 011 in binary. You borrow across the two 0s to reach the rightmost column.

items.prompt: How does binary addition compare to decimal addition?
choices: It uses a completely different method
It is the same column-by-column carry method, just carrying at two instead of ten
It never needs carrying
It works from left to right instead of right to left
items.explanation: The method is identical: add columns from the right and carry when a column overflows. The only difference is binary carries once a column reaches two, while decimal carries at ten.

lessons.title: Negative Numbers & Two's Complement
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Bits are just 0s and 1s, so how does a computer write a *negative* number? There is no minus sign available — only switches. Engineers had to invent a clever code, and the one that won is called **two's complement**.

**Build.** People tried three ideas:

1. **Sign-magnitude.** Use the leftmost bit purely as a sign (0 = positive, 1 = negative) and the rest as the size. Simple to read, but it has *two* zeros (a "+0" and a "-0"), which is awkward, and it needs special circuitry to add.

2. **One's complement.** Make a negative by flipping every bit. It also suffers from two zeros and an extra fix-up step when adding.

3. **Two's complement (the winner).** Make a negative by flipping every bit **and then adding 1**. To get $-5$: start with $+5$ = $00000101$, flip to $11111010$, add 1 to get $11111011$.

Two's complement won for two reasons: there is exactly **one zero**, and ordinary binary addition "just works" for negatives with no special handling — the same adder circuit does both. A leading 1 still signals a negative number, but you decode it by the flip-and-add-1 process rather than reading it as a plain sign.

**Overflow vs carry.** With a fixed number of bits, a result can run off the end. A **carry** is a 1 that falls off the left during addition. **Overflow** is different: it means the *signed* answer came out wrong — for example, adding two positives and getting a negative because the true answer was too big to fit. Carry and overflow are separate warnings, and mixing them up is a classic mistake.

**Recap.** Two's complement encodes negatives by flipping all bits and adding 1. It won because it has a single zero and reuses the normal adder. Watch for overflow, which is a wrong-signed result, distinct from a carry off the end.

items.prompt: In two's complement, how do you make the negative of a number?
choices: Flip every bit
Flip every bit, then add 1
Add 1, then flip every bit
Set the leftmost bit to 1 and leave the rest
items.explanation: Two's complement: invert (flip) all the bits, then add 1. Flipping alone is one's complement, and just setting the top bit is sign-magnitude; both of those have problems two's complement avoids.

items.prompt: Why did two's complement win over the other schemes?
choices: It is the easiest for humans to read
It has one zero and normal addition just works for negatives
It uses fewer bits than the others
It is the only one that can store positives
items.explanation: Two's complement has a single representation of zero and lets the ordinary adder handle negative numbers correctly with no extra steps. Sign-magnitude and one's complement both have two zeros and need special handling.

items.prompt: What awkward problem do sign-magnitude and one's complement share?
choices: They cannot store negatives
They have two different representations of zero (+0 and -0)
They use three symbols
They cannot be added at all
items.explanation: Both schemes end up with a separate +0 and -0, which complicates comparisons and arithmetic. Two's complement collapses these into a single zero.

items.prompt: In 8-bit two's complement, +5 is 00000101. What is -5?
items.explanation: Flip 00000101 to get 11111010, then add 1 to get 11111011. The choice 11111010 forgot the '+1' step, and 10000101 is the sign-magnitude form, not two's complement.

items.prompt: In two's complement, what does a leading (leftmost) 1 bit tell you?
choices: The number is positive
The number is negative
The number is zero
Nothing at all
items.explanation: A leading 1 indicates a negative value in two's complement. To find its size you flip the bits and add 1, rather than just reading the remaining bits as a plain magnitude.

items.prompt: In 8-bit two's complement, what is -1?
items.explanation: Start with +1 = 00000001, flip to 11111110, add 1 to get 11111111. So -1 is all ones, which is a handy pattern to recognize.

items.prompt: What does signed 'overflow' mean?
choices: A 1 fell off the left end during addition
The signed answer came out wrong, e.g. two positives summing to a negative
The number ran out of hex symbols
The result was exactly zero
items.explanation: Overflow means the true signed result was too big to fit, so the sign came out wrong (like positive + positive giving a negative). A 1 falling off the left is a carry, which is a separate thing.

items.prompt: How are 'carry' and 'overflow' different?
choices: They are the same thing
Carry is a bit off the left end; overflow is a wrong-signed result
Carry only happens in subtraction
Overflow only happens in hex
items.explanation: A carry is a bit that drops off the most-significant end during addition. Overflow specifically means the signed answer is wrong. They are independent signals, and confusing them is a common error.

lessons.title: Fractions: Fixed-Point & Floating-Point
lessons.content:
**Hook.** So far every binary number has been a whole number. But computers handle $3.14$ and $0.5$ too. There are two common ways to store fractions, and one of them explains a famous surprise: why $0.1 + 0.2$ does not quite equal $0.3$ on a computer.

**Build.** **Fixed-point** is the simple idea. You take ordinary binary and *agree* on where the "binary point" sits, just like a fixed dollars-and-cents format always keeps two decimal places. Bits to the right of the point are worth $\\tfrac{1}{2}, \\tfrac{1}{4}, \\tfrac{1}{8}, \\dots$. The point never moves, which keeps the math fast and predictable — but the range of sizes you can store is limited.

**Floating-point** lets the point *move* so you can store both tiny and huge numbers. It stores numbers like scientific notation ($1.5 \\times 10^{3}$), but in binary. The standard format, **IEEE 754**, splits the bits into three parts:
- **Sign** (1 bit): positive or negative.
- **Exponent**: how far to shift the point (how big or small).
- **Mantissa** (also called the fraction): the actual digits of the number.

A single-precision float uses $1 + 8 + 23 = 32$ bits for those three parts.

**Why $0.1$ is not exact.** In binary, $0.1$ is a *repeating* fraction — its digits go on forever, the same way $\\tfrac{1}{3} = 0.3333\\dots$ never ends in decimal. The computer has to cut it off and round, so the stored value is a hair off. Tiny errors like that add up, which is why $0.1 + 0.2$ comes out as $0.30000\\dots04$ instead of exactly $0.3$.

**Recap.** Fixed-point pins the binary point in place: simple and fast, limited range. Floating-point (IEEE 754) uses a sign, an exponent, and a mantissa to move the point for a huge range. Because some decimals repeat forever in binary, floats round them, so $0.1$ is never exactly $0.1$.

items.prompt: What is the core idea of fixed-point representation?
choices: The binary point can move anywhere
The binary point stays in an agreed, fixed spot
It can only store whole numbers
It uses three separate fields
items.explanation: Fixed-point keeps the binary point in one agreed position, like always showing two decimal places for money. This keeps arithmetic simple and fast but limits the range. The movable point belongs to floating-point.

items.prompt: Just right of the binary point, what is the first place value worth?
items.explanation: Place values keep halving across the point, so the first fractional place is worth 1/2, then 1/4, then 1/8. (1/10 would apply to decimal, not binary.)

items.prompt: What advantage does floating-point have over fixed-point?
choices: It is always exact
It can represent a much wider range of sizes, from tiny to huge
It uses fewer bits
It cannot store negative numbers
items.explanation: Letting the point 'float' lets the same format hold both very small and very large numbers, a huge dynamic range. It is not more exact, though; in fact it rounds many values.

items.prompt: What are the three parts of an IEEE 754 floating-point number?
choices: Sign, exponent, mantissa
Whole, fraction, remainder
Sign, carry, overflow
Byte, nibble, word
items.explanation: IEEE 754 splits the bits into a sign (positive/negative), an exponent (how far to shift the point), and a mantissa or fraction (the digits). The other lists mix up unrelated terms.

items.prompt: In a floating-point number, what does the sign bit do?
choices: Sets how big the number is
Tells whether the number is positive or negative
Holds the digits of the number
Moves the binary point
items.explanation: The single sign bit marks the number positive or negative. The exponent controls size (moving the point), and the mantissa holds the actual digits.

items.prompt: What does the exponent field of a float control?
choices: Whether the number is negative
How far to shift the binary point (the scale)
The exact digits of the number
How many bytes are used
items.explanation: The exponent says how far to move the binary point, setting the overall scale (how big or small). The mantissa holds the digits, and the sign bit handles negativity.

items.prompt: Why is 0.1 not stored exactly in binary floating-point?
choices: Computers cannot store any fractions
0.1 is a repeating fraction in binary, so it gets rounded
0.1 is too large to fit
The sign bit is missing
items.explanation: In binary, 0.1 repeats forever (like 1/3 = 0.333... in decimal), so the computer must round it off. The stored value is slightly inexact, which is why 0.1 + 0.2 is not exactly 0.3.

items.prompt: Given the rounding in floats, what is good advice when comparing two floating-point results?
choices: Always test them for exact equality
Avoid exact-equality tests; check if they are close enough
Convert them to hex first
Never use floating-point at all
items.explanation: Because floats round, two values that should be equal may differ by a tiny amount, so exact-equality tests can fail unexpectedly. The usual practice is to check that they are within a small tolerance of each other.

lessons.title: Two Special Codes: BCD & Gray Code
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Plain binary is great for math, but a couple of jobs call for a different way of arranging bits. Two handy codes show up often enough to know by name: **BCD** and **Gray code**.

**Build.** **Binary-coded decimal (BCD)** stores each *decimal digit* in its own group of four bits, instead of converting the whole number to binary. Take the number $27$: in BCD you store the 2 and the 7 separately as $0010\\,0111$. (Plain binary for 27 would be $11011$ — a different pattern.) BCD wastes a little space, but it makes driving a digital clock or a calculator display easy, because each digit is already isolated and ready to show.

**Gray code** is an ordering of binary values where **only one bit changes** from each value to the next. Normal counting can flip several bits at once: going from $0111$ to $1000$ changes all four. If a sensor is read at the exact instant several bits are mid-flip, you could catch a garbage value. Gray code avoids this by changing just one bit per step, so a reading is never more than one bit off. That is why it is used in position sensors and rotary dials, where a brief misread could matter.

**Recap.** BCD keeps each decimal digit in its own 4-bit group, which is convenient for displays and clocks. Gray code orders values so only one bit changes at a time, preventing glitches when reading sensors.

items.prompt: How does binary-coded decimal (BCD) store a number?
choices: It converts the whole number to plain binary
It stores each decimal digit in its own group of 4 bits
It uses base-16
It stores only the sign and exponent
items.explanation: BCD keeps each decimal digit separate, encoding it in its own 4-bit nibble. That is different from converting the entire value to one binary number.

items.prompt: How is the number 27 stored in BCD?
items.explanation: Each digit gets its own nibble: 2 = 0010 and 7 = 0111, so 27 is 0010 0111. The value 11011 is plain binary for 27, which is a different representation.

items.prompt: Where is BCD especially convenient?
choices: High-speed math
Driving digital displays like clocks and calculators
Compressing files to save space
Storing negative numbers
items.explanation: Because each decimal digit is already isolated, BCD makes it easy to light up the correct digit on a 7-segment display or clock. It is not chosen for speed or compactness; it actually wastes some space.

items.prompt: What decimal number does the BCD pattern 1001 0011 represent?
items.explanation: Read each nibble as a decimal digit: 1001 = 9 and 0011 = 3, giving 93. Reading it as one plain binary number would wrongly give 147.

items.prompt: What is the defining property of Gray code?
choices: It uses the fewest possible bits
Only one bit changes between each value and the next
It has no zero
Each value doubles the previous one
items.explanation: In Gray code, moving from one value to the next flips exactly one bit. This single-bit change is what makes it safe against misreads during a transition.

items.prompt: Why does changing only one bit at a time help?
choices: It makes numbers larger
It prevents catching a garbage value when several bits flip at once
It saves memory
It allows negative numbers
items.explanation: If many bits flip simultaneously (like 0111 to 1000), reading at the wrong instant could capture a meaningless in-between value. With one bit changing, a misread is at most one step off, never garbage.

items.prompt: Where is Gray code commonly used?
choices: Position sensors and rotary encoders
Storing text
Floating-point math
Driving a calculator display
items.explanation: Gray code suits position sensors and rotary encoders, where a brief multi-bit misread could give a wildly wrong position. Displays are where BCD shines instead.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly contrasts BCD and Gray code?
choices: Both store decimal digits separately
BCD isolates decimal digits for display; Gray code changes one bit at a time for safe sensing
Both change one bit at a time
Gray code is for displays and BCD is for sensors
items.explanation: BCD keeps each decimal digit in its own nibble (handy for displays), while Gray code orders values so only one bit changes at a time (handy for sensors). The other options mix up their purposes.

lessons.title: TRUE/FALSE Logic: AND, OR, NOT & Truth Tables
lessons.content:
Think about how you make everyday decisions. "I'll go outside **if** it's sunny **and** I'm free." "I'll grab a snack **if** there are chips **or** there's fruit." You are combining true/false facts to reach a yes/no answer. **Boolean algebra** is simply the math of these true/false decisions, and it is the logic behind every choice a computer makes.

In the previous sub-topic you met **binary**, where everything is a $0$ or a $1$. Boolean algebra uses the same two values, and we read them as **TRUE = 1** and **FALSE = 0**. A **Boolean variable** (like $A$ or $B$) is just a box that holds either $0$ or $1$.

There are three basic operations:

- **AND** is true only when *both* inputs are true. "Sunny AND free" needs both. We write it $A \cdot B$ (a dot) or just $AB$.
- **OR** is true when *at least one* input is true. "Chips OR fruit" — either one is enough. We write it $A + B$ (a plus). Careful: this plus does not mean ordinary addition.
- **NOT** flips a value: it turns $1$ into $0$ and $0$ into $1$. We write it with a bar on top, $\overline{A}$, read "NOT A".

A **truth table** lists the output for *every* possible combination of inputs. With two inputs there are four combinations ($00, 01, 10, 11$). For AND, the output is $1$ only in the last row ($A{=}1, B{=}1$). For OR, the output is $1$ in every row except the first. The truth table is the complete, no-surprises description of what an operation does.

**Recap:** Boolean algebra is the math of TRUE (1) and FALSE (0). AND needs both, OR needs at least one, NOT flips. A truth table spells out the answer for every input.

items.prompt: In Boolean algebra, what do the two values usually represent?
choices: TRUE and FALSE (written 1 and 0)
Positive and negative numbers
High and low temperatures
Letters A and B
items.explanation: Boolean algebra has exactly two values. We read 1 as TRUE and 0 as FALSE. It is the math of true/false (yes/no) decisions, which is why it uses only two values rather than the full range of ordinary numbers.

items.prompt: An AND operation outputs 1 (TRUE) in which case?
choices: Only when both inputs are 1
When at least one input is 1
Only when both inputs are 0
When the inputs are different
items.explanation: AND means "both." Like "sunny AND free," it is true only when every input is true. If even one input is 0, the AND output is 0. "At least one" describes OR, not AND.

items.prompt: An OR operation outputs 1 (TRUE) in which case?
choices: When at least one input is 1
Only when both inputs are 1
Only when both inputs are 0
Only when the inputs are equal
items.explanation: OR means "either one is enough," like "chips OR fruit." It is true if any input is 1. It is false only when every input is 0. Requiring both inputs to be 1 describes AND.

items.prompt: What does the NOT operation do to its input?
choices: Flips it: 1 becomes 0 and 0 becomes 1
Doubles it
Leaves it unchanged
Adds the two inputs together
items.explanation: NOT (the complement) reverses the value: TRUE becomes FALSE and FALSE becomes TRUE. We write it with an overbar, so $\overline{A}$ is read "NOT A." NOT takes just one input, so there is nothing to add or double.

items.prompt: Evaluate this expression: with $A=1$ and $B=0$, what is $A \cdot B$ (A AND B)?
choices: Both 0 and 1
It is undefined
items.explanation: AND needs both inputs to be 1. Here $B=0$, so the AND output is 0. A single 0 input is enough to pull an AND result down to 0.

items.prompt: Evaluate this expression: with $A=1$ and $B=0$, what is $A + B$ (A OR B)?
choices: It is undefined
items.explanation: OR is true when at least one input is 1. Here $A=1$, so $A + B = 1$. Note the plus sign here is the OR operation, not ordinary addition, so the answer is 1 (not 2).

items.prompt: Read the truth table for OR. In which single row is the output 0?
choices: When both inputs are 0
When both inputs are 1
When the first input is 1
OR is never 0
items.explanation: OR is true if any input is 1, so it is false only when there are no 1s at all, that is, when both inputs are 0. In all three other rows at least one input is 1, giving output 1.

items.prompt: What is a truth table?
choices: A list of the output for every possible combination of inputs
A list of only the inputs that give a TRUE output
A circuit drawing of gates and wires
A way to write numbers in binary
items.explanation: A truth table is the complete description of an operation: it shows the output for each and every input combination, leaving nothing out. Listing only the TRUE rows would be incomplete, and it is a table of values, not a circuit drawing.

items.prompt: How many rows does the truth table for an operation with 2 inputs have?
items.explanation: Each input can be 0 or 1, so 2 inputs give $2 \times 2 = 4$ combinations (00, 01, 10, 11). In general $n$ inputs give $2^n$ rows, so the count doubles for each extra input.

items.prompt: A light turns on only when switch X AND switch Y are both pressed. Which operation describes the light?
choices: AND
OR
NOT
XOR
items.explanation: "Both pressed" is exactly the AND condition: the output (light on) is 1 only when every input is 1. If it turned on with either switch alone, that would be OR.

lessons.title: More Useful Operations: NAND, NOR, XOR & XNOR
lessons.content:
Once you have AND, OR, and NOT, you can mix them to make a few new operations that come up so often they get their own names. Think of them as handy shortcuts.

The first two are easy: just add a NOT on the end.

- **NAND** means "NOT AND." Do an AND, then flip the result. So NAND is $1$ everywhere AND was $0$, and $0$ only when both inputs are $1$. We write it $\overline{A \cdot B}$.
- **NOR** means "NOT OR." Do an OR, then flip it. NOR is $1$ only when both inputs are $0$. We write it $\overline{A + B}$.

The next two are about *comparing* two inputs:

- **XOR** (exclusive-OR) is true when the two inputs are **different**. Think of a search filter like "cats AND NOT dogs" — you want one thing but not the other. We write it $A \oplus B$ (a plus inside a circle). XOR is the building block of adding binary numbers, because $1 + 1$ produces a $0$ with a carry, just like XOR gives $0$ when both inputs match.
- **XNOR** means "NOT XOR." It is true when the two inputs are the **same** (both 0 or both 1). It is the "are these equal?" detector.

A quick way to remember XOR vs XNOR: **XOR = different, XNOR = equal**. And the "N" in front of any name (NAND, NOR, XNOR) always means "add a NOT," so the output is flipped.

**Recap:** NAND and NOR are just AND and OR with a NOT stuck on the end. XOR is true when inputs differ; XNOR is true when they match.

items.prompt: What does NAND mean?
choices: NOT AND: do an AND, then flip the result
AND done twice
NOT OR
AND with three inputs
items.explanation: The "N" prefix means "add a NOT." So NAND is AND followed by a NOT, written $\overline{A \cdot B}$. NOT OR is a different operation called NOR.

items.prompt: Evaluate this expression: with $A=1$ and $B=1$, what is NAND ($\overline{A \cdot B}$)?
choices: Undefined
items.explanation: First do the AND: $1 \cdot 1 = 1$. Then NOT flips it to 0. NAND is 0 only in this one case (both inputs 1) and 1 in every other case.

items.prompt: NOR outputs 1 (TRUE) in which case?
choices: Only when both inputs are 0
Only when both inputs are 1
When at least one input is 1
When the inputs differ
items.explanation: NOR is NOT OR. OR is 0 only when both inputs are 0, so flipping that, NOR is 1 only when both inputs are 0. In every other row OR was 1, so NOR is 0.

items.prompt: XOR ($A \oplus B$) outputs 1 when...
choices: the two inputs are different
the two inputs are the same
both inputs are 1
both inputs are 0
items.explanation: XOR (exclusive-OR) is the "difference" detector: it is 1 when exactly one input is 1 (the inputs differ) and 0 when they match. "Same inputs" describes XNOR instead.

items.prompt: Evaluate this expression: with $A=1$ and $B=0$, what is $A \oplus B$ (XOR)?
choices: Undefined
items.explanation: The inputs are different (one is 1, the other 0), and XOR is 1 exactly when the inputs differ. So $A \oplus B = 1$ here.

items.prompt: What does XNOR detect?
choices: Whether the two inputs are equal
Whether the two inputs are different
Whether both inputs are 0
Whether both inputs are 1
items.explanation: XNOR is NOT XOR. Since XOR is true when inputs differ, flipping it gives true when the inputs are equal (both 0 or both 1). That makes XNOR the equality detector.

items.prompt: Read the truth table: for inputs $A=0$, $B=0$, which of these outputs is 1?
choices: NAND and NOR and XNOR
Only XOR
Only AND
NOR only
items.explanation: With both inputs 0: AND=0 so NAND=1; OR=0 so NOR=1; the inputs are equal so XOR=0 and XNOR=1. So NAND, NOR, and XNOR are all 1, while AND, OR, and XOR are 0.

items.prompt: What does the letter "N" at the front of NAND, NOR, or XNOR always tell you?
choices: The output of the base operation is flipped (a NOT is added)
There are exactly N inputs
The inputs are numbers, not true/false
The operation is negative-only
items.explanation: The leading "N" stands for NOT. NAND = NOT AND, NOR = NOT OR, XNOR = NOT XOR. In each case you take the base operation and flip its output. It says nothing about how many inputs there are.

items.prompt: A circuit lights up only when two bits are NOT the same. Which operation is this?
choices: XOR
XNOR
AND
NOR
items.explanation: "Not the same" means the inputs differ, which is exactly XOR. XNOR would light up when they ARE the same, so it is the opposite of what is described.

lessons.title: The Rules of the Game: Boolean Laws & De Morgan's
lessons.content:
Just like ordinary arithmetic has rules (such as $a + 0 = a$), Boolean algebra has its own rules, called **laws** or **theorems**. They let you rewrite an expression into a simpler one that means exactly the same thing. You do not need to memorize them all at once; the idea is that each rule is a safe "move" you can make.

Start with the simplest, which you can check by hand:

- **Identity:** $A + 0 = A$ and $A \cdot 1 = A$ (ORing with 0 or ANDing with 1 changes nothing).
- **Null:** $A + 1 = 1$ and $A \cdot 0 = 0$ (a 1 forces an OR true; a 0 forces an AND false).
- **Complement:** $A + \overline{A} = 1$ and $A \cdot \overline{A} = 0$ (a thing OR-ed with its opposite is always true; AND-ed with its opposite is always false).

Then the "shape-shifting" laws: **commutative** ($A + B = B + A$ — order does not matter), **associative** (grouping does not matter), and **distributive** ($A(B + C) = AB + AC$, just like in normal algebra).

The single most useful rule is **De Morgan's theorem**. It tells you how to push a NOT across a whole expression:

$$\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$$
$$\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$$

In plain words: when you flip the NOT bar over a group, **break the bar and swap AND with OR**. "NOT (sunny AND warm)" is the same as "(NOT sunny) OR (NOT warm)" — if either condition fails, the whole thing fails. There is also a neat symmetry called **duality**: swap every AND with OR and every 0 with 1 in a valid law, and you get another valid law for free. That is why these rules come in pairs.

**Recap:** Boolean laws are safe rewrite moves. De Morgan's is the star: break the bar, swap AND and OR.

items.prompt: Apply De Morgan's theorem to $\overline{A + B}$. What does it equal?
choices: $\overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$
$\overline{A} + \overline{B}$
$A \cdot B$
$A + B$
items.explanation: Break the bar and swap the operation: the OR becomes an AND, and each variable gets its own bar. So $\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$. A common mistake is to leave it as an OR; De Morgan's always swaps AND and OR.

items.prompt: Apply De Morgan's theorem to $\overline{A \cdot B}$. What does it equal?
choices: $\overline{A} + \overline{B}$
$\overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$
$A + B$
$\overline{A \cdot B}$ cannot be simplified
items.explanation: Break the bar and swap AND for OR: $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$. This is why a NAND gate can be redrawn as an OR with both inputs inverted.

items.prompt: "It is NOT true that it is sunny AND warm." Which everyday sentence means the same thing?
choices: It is not sunny, OR it is not warm
It is not sunny, AND it is not warm
It is sunny, OR it is warm
It is sunny AND warm
items.explanation: This is De Morgan's in words: $\overline{S \cdot W} = \overline{S} + \overline{W}$. The whole statement fails if EITHER condition fails, so it becomes an OR of the two negatives. Choosing AND would wrongly require both to fail.

items.prompt: Using the identity law, what is $A \cdot 1$?
choices: $\overline{A}$
items.explanation: ANDing with 1 leaves the value unchanged: if $A=1$ the result is 1, and if $A=0$ the result is 0, so the output always just equals $A$. (ANDing with 0, by contrast, would force the result to 0.)

items.prompt: Using the null law, what is $A + 1$?
choices: $\overline{A}$
items.explanation: OR is true if any input is 1. Since one input is already 1, the result is 1 no matter what $A$ is. Do not confuse this with $A + 0 = A$, where the 0 leaves $A$ unchanged.

items.prompt: Using the complement law, what is $A \cdot \overline{A}$ (A AND NOT-A)?
choices: $\overline{A}$
items.explanation: A value and its opposite can never both be 1, so their AND is always 0. (The matching rule is $A + \overline{A} = 1$, since one of the two is always true.)

items.prompt: The commutative law says order does not matter. Which equation shows it?
choices: $A + B = B + A$
$A \cdot \overline{A} = 0$
$\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$
items.explanation: Commutative means you can swap the order of the inputs without changing the result: $A + B = B + A$ (and likewise $AB = BA$). The other choices are the identity, complement, and De Morgan laws.

items.prompt: The duality principle lets you create a new valid law from an existing one by...
choices: swapping every AND with OR and every 0 with 1
replacing each variable with its complement
removing all the NOT bars
doubling every variable
items.explanation: Duality swaps the operations ($\cdot \leftrightarrow +$) and the constants ($0 \leftrightarrow 1$), leaving the variables alone. This is why laws come in pairs. Note this is different from De Morgan's, which also complements the variables.

items.prompt: The distributive law $A(B + C) = AB + AC$ is most like which familiar rule?
choices: Multiplying out a bracket in ordinary algebra
Rounding a number
Adding fractions
Counting in binary
items.explanation: It works just like expanding $a(b+c) = ab + ac$ in regular algebra, treating AND like multiply and OR like add. This makes it the easiest Boolean law to remember.

lessons.title: Tidying Up: Simplifying & Building From Truth Tables
lessons.content:
There are usually many ways to write the same logic, just as $2 + 2$ and $1 + 3$ both equal $4$. Some are long and clumsy; some are short. **Simplifying** a Boolean expression means using the laws from the last lesson to find a shorter version that gives the exact same truth table. Shorter expressions mean fewer parts in the real circuit, which saves cost, power, and time.

A tiny example using the **absorption** rule: $A + A \cdot B = A$. Why? If $A$ is already true, the OR is true and the second term does not matter; if $A$ is false, then $A \cdot B$ is also false. So the whole thing just follows $A$. We removed a term without changing the meaning.

Now the reverse skill: building an expression *from* a truth table. Suppose someone hands you a truth table and asks for a formula. The most direct recipe is the **sum of products (SOP)**, also called the canonical SOP form:

1. Look only at the rows where the output is $1$.
2. For each such row, write a **product** (an AND) of all the inputs, putting a NOT bar on each input that is $0$ in that row. This one product is called a **minterm**, and it is true for exactly that one row.
3. **OR** all those products together.

For example, if the output is 1 only in the rows $A{=}0,B{=}0,C{=}1$ and $A{=}1,B{=}1,C{=}1$, the SOP is $\overline{A}\,\overline{B}\,C + A B C$. There is a mirror-image recipe called **product of sums (POS)** that instead looks at the rows where the output is $0$. Both describe the same function; SOP is usually the friendlier place to start.

Minterms are often numbered by reading the row's input bits as a binary number, so the row $A{=}1,B{=}0,C{=}1$ ($101$ in binary) is minterm number $5$.

**Recap:** Simplifying shrinks an expression using the laws. To build one from a truth table, OR together a product (minterm) for each row where the output is 1 — that is the canonical SOP form.

items.prompt: What does it mean to "simplify" a Boolean expression?
choices: Rewrite it as a shorter expression with the same truth table
Change it so it gives different answers
Convert it to a decimal number
Add more terms so it is easier to read
items.explanation: Simplifying finds an equivalent but shorter form, meaning the truth table (the output for every input) is unchanged. The point is fewer gates in hardware, so adding terms or changing the answers would defeat the purpose.

items.prompt: Using the absorption rule, simplify $A + A \cdot B$.
choices: $A \cdot B$
$A + B$
items.explanation: If $A$ is 1 the OR is already 1; if $A$ is 0 then $A \cdot B$ is 0 too. Either way the result just follows $A$, so $A + AB = A$. The extra term is "absorbed."

items.prompt: In the canonical sum-of-products (SOP) method, which truth-table rows do you use?
choices: The rows where the output is 1
The rows where the output is 0
Only the first and last rows
All rows, regardless of output
items.explanation: SOP collects the rows that make the function TRUE: write a product (minterm) for each output-1 row and OR them together. The output-0 rows are what the mirror-image POS method uses instead.

items.prompt: What is a minterm?
choices: An AND of all the input variables, true for exactly one row
An OR of all the inputs
The smallest number in a truth table
Any term with a NOT bar
items.explanation: A minterm is a single product (AND) covering every input variable, arranged so it is 1 for exactly one input combination. ORing the minterms of the output-1 rows reproduces the function. An OR of all inputs is instead a maxterm.

items.prompt: When you write the minterm for a row, what do you do to an input that is 0 in that row?
choices: Write it with a NOT bar (complemented)
Leave it out of the product entirely
Write it as a plain 0
Replace the whole product with 0
items.explanation: A minterm must be TRUE for that exact row. Since an input of 0 is only "true" when complemented, you bar it ($\overline{A}$). Inputs that are 1 stay plain. That way the product is 1 for just that one combination.

items.prompt: Read the truth table for $f(A,B,C)$ that is 1 only at the row $A{=}0, B{=}0, C{=}1$. What is the minterm for that row?
choices: $\overline{A}\,\overline{B}\,C$
$A\,B\,\overline{C}$
$A + B + \overline{C}$
items.explanation: Bar the inputs that are 0 and leave the 1s plain: $A{=}0 \to \overline{A}$, $B{=}0 \to \overline{B}$, $C{=}1 \to C$. ANDing gives $\overline{A}\,\overline{B}\,C$. Using an OR would make it a maxterm, not a minterm.

items.prompt: After writing one minterm for each output-1 row, how do you combine them into the full SOP expression?
choices: OR all the minterms together
AND all the minterms together
Subtract them
Pick only the longest one
items.explanation: Each minterm covers one TRUE row, and you want the function to be 1 if it matches ANY of those rows, which is an OR. ANDing them instead would be 1 only when all rows happened at once, which is impossible.

items.prompt: How does the product-of-sums (POS) method differ from SOP?
choices: It builds terms from the rows where the output is 0
It uses only the rows where the output is 1
It produces a different truth table
It can only handle two inputs
items.explanation: POS is the mirror image of SOP: it focuses on the output-0 rows and ANDs together sum (OR) terms. It describes the very same function, just approached from the FALSE rows instead of the TRUE ones.

items.prompt: Minterms are often numbered by reading the row's input bits as binary. For $f(A,B,C)$, which row is minterm number 5?
items.explanation: The number 5 in binary is $101$, so $A{=}1, B{=}0, C{=}1$. Reading the input bits as a binary number is how each row gets its minterm index.

items.prompt: Why do engineers bother simplifying an expression before building the circuit?
choices: Fewer terms means fewer gates, saving cost, power, and delay
Longer expressions are always wrong
Simplifying changes the function to a better one
It is required to draw a truth table
items.explanation: A shorter equivalent expression maps to fewer physical gates, which lowers cost, power use, and signal delay, all without changing what the circuit does. The function itself stays exactly the same.

lessons.title: Two Big Ideas: NAND Builds Anything & Don't-Cares
lessons.content:
This lesson covers two ideas that feel surprising at first but are very practical.

**Idea 1: One gate can build everything.** A set of operations is called **functionally complete** (or universal) if you can build *any* logic function out of it. The trio AND, OR, NOT is obviously complete. The amazing part: **NAND all by itself is complete.** With nothing but NAND gates, you can make NOT, AND, OR, and therefore anything at all. Here is how:

- **NOT from NAND:** feed the same signal into both inputs. $\overline{A \cdot A} = \overline{A}$. Both inputs equal, so it just flips $A$.
- **AND from NAND:** a NAND gives $\overline{A \cdot B}$, so follow it with a NAND-as-NOT to flip it back to $A \cdot B$.
- **OR from NAND:** by De Morgan's, $A + B = \overline{\overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}}$, which is three NANDs (invert each input, then NAND the results).

NOR alone is universal in the same way. This is why real computer chips are built mostly from huge numbers of identical NAND (or NOR) cells: making one simple cell perfectly and stamping out millions of copies is far cheaper than designing many different gates.

**Idea 2: Don't-cares.** Sometimes certain input combinations simply *cannot happen*, or nobody ever looks at the output for them. We mark that output with an $X$ (or $d$) and call it a **don't-care**. Because we genuinely do not care whether it is $0$ or $1$, we are free to pick whichever choice makes the circuit *simpler*. A classic example: a display that shows decimal digits $0$ through $9$ uses 4 input bits, but $4$ bits can also represent $10$ through $15$. Those six extra combinations never occur, so they are don't-cares we can use to shrink the logic.

**Recap:** NAND (or NOR) alone can build any logic, so chips are seas of one repeated gate. Don't-care inputs (impossible or unused) can be set to 0 or 1 — whatever simplifies the circuit.

items.prompt: What does it mean for a set of operations to be "functionally complete" (universal)?
choices: You can build any possible logic function using only that set
It contains exactly three operations
It can only build AND and OR
It works only for two inputs
items.explanation: Functionally complete means the set is powerful enough to construct every Boolean function. AND/OR/NOT qualifies, and remarkably so does NAND all by itself. The number of operations in the set is not what matters.

items.prompt: How do you make a NOT gate using a single NAND gate?
choices: Connect the same signal to both of its inputs
Connect a 0 to one input
Use two NANDs in a row
It is impossible with NAND
items.explanation: Tie both inputs together to feed in the same signal $A$. Then $\overline{A \cdot A} = \overline{A}$, which is exactly NOT. This trick is the first step in showing NAND is universal.

items.prompt: A plain NAND gate gives $\overline{A \cdot B}$. How do you turn that into a plain AND ($A \cdot B$)?
choices: Add a NAND-as-NOT on the output to flip it back
Add another input
Swap the two inputs
Connect the output to ground
items.explanation: AND is just NAND with its output flipped. Since a NAND with tied inputs acts as a NOT, feeding the NAND's output through one more NAND-as-NOT gives $A \cdot B$. That is two NAND gates total.

items.prompt: Building OR from NANDs uses De Morgan's: $A + B = \overline{\overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}}$. How many NAND gates does this take?
items.explanation: Two NANDs act as inverters to make $\overline{A}$ and $\overline{B}$, then a third NAND combines them. So three NAND gates produce an OR.

items.prompt: Which statement is true about universal gates?
choices: NAND alone, and NOR alone, can each build any logic function
Only AND can build any logic function
XOR alone can build any logic function
No single gate can be universal
items.explanation: Both NAND and NOR are universal: each can produce NOT, AND, and OR, and therefore anything. XOR alone is not universal (it cannot make a constant or a simple inverter by itself), and AND alone certainly cannot.

items.prompt: Why are real computer chips built mostly from one repeated gate like NAND?
choices: A single universal cell, copied millions of times, can build anything and is cheap to make well
NAND is the only gate that exists physically
It makes the chip run on less than one volt
Chips are not allowed to mix gate types
items.explanation: Because NAND is universal, manufacturers perfect one simple cell and stamp out huge numbers of identical copies, which is cheaper and more reliable than designing many distinct gates. It is an economics-and-manufacturing win, not a physical limitation.

items.prompt: What is a "don't-care" condition?
choices: An input combination that cannot occur or whose output is never used
An input that is always 1
A broken gate in the circuit
The very first row of a truth table
items.explanation: A don't-care is a row whose output we are free to define however we like, because that input either never happens or its output is never read. We mark it with an X. It is not a fault or a fixed value.

items.prompt: How should you treat a don't-care output when trying to simplify a circuit?
choices: Set it to 0 or 1, whichever makes the logic simpler
Always set it to 0
Always set it to 1
Ignore the whole truth table
items.explanation: Since the value genuinely does not matter, you pick 0 or 1 freely to get the simplest circuit. Forcing it always to 0 (or always to 1) throws away that flexibility and usually leaves the logic larger than it needs to be.

items.prompt: A circuit reads a 4-bit code meant to show decimal digits 0 through 9. Why are the codes for 10 through 15 don't-cares?
choices: Those codes represent no valid digit, so they never occur as inputs
They are the same as the codes for 0 through 5
They always output 1
They are the only codes that matter
items.explanation: Four bits can count 0 to 15, but only 0 to 9 are valid digits. The six leftover codes (10 to 15) never appear, so their outputs are don't-cares the designer can exploit to simplify the logic.

lessons.title: Gates Are Tiny Circuits of Transistor Switches
lessons.content:
**Hook:** You already know logic gates as ideas (AND, OR, NOT). But a real gate is not a magic box - it is a tiny electrical circuit. Inside, the work is done by **transistors**, which act as voltage-controlled **switches** (you met the transistor as a switch in analog). This lesson connects the logic you know to the real hardware.

**Build:** A transistor switch has a control terminal (the *gate*) and two terminals it connects or disconnects (think of it as the two ends of a light switch). When the control is held one way, the switch is *closed* (it conducts, like a wire). Held the other way, it is *open* (no current flows).

A logic gate wires a few of these switches between a **supply** (the higher voltage, which we read as logic **1**) and **ground** (0 volts, which we read as logic **0**). The pattern of which switches are open or closed decides whether the output gets connected to the supply or to ground.

Take the simplest gate, the **NOT gate** (also called an **inverter**). It uses two switches stacked between supply and ground, both controlled by the same input. When the input is 0, the top switch closes and connects the output to the supply, so the output is 1. When the input is 1, the bottom switch closes and connects the output to ground, so the output is 0. The output is always the opposite of the input - exactly what NOT means.

The key trick: in a well-built gate, *exactly one* path is connected at a time. The output is never left dangling; it is firmly tied to either the supply or ground. That is what makes a clean, reliable 1 or 0.

**Recap:** Logic gates are small circuits of transistor switches. The switches steer the output to the supply (a 1) or to ground (a 0). Everything else in digital electronics is built on this idea.

items.prompt: Inside a real logic gate, what actually does the work of producing a 1 or a 0?
choices: Tiny magnets that store the bit
Transistors acting as voltage-controlled switches
A small battery in each gate
Software running on the chip
items.explanation: A gate is an electrical circuit. Transistors behave like switches that open or close depending on their control voltage, steering the output to the supply or to ground. There are no magnets, per-gate batteries, or software inside a basic gate.

items.prompt: In digital logic, what does a logic '1' physically correspond to in the circuit?
choices: The voltage near the supply (the higher voltage)
Exactly 1 volt, always
The voltage at ground (0 V)
A current of 1 amp
items.explanation: A '1' means the output is connected to the supply, so its voltage sits near the supply rail. It is not a fixed 1 volt and it is not a current. Ground (0 V) is read as logic '0'.

items.prompt: A transistor used as a switch is 'closed' (conducting). What does that mean for the circuit?
choices: It blocks all current, like an open gap
It connects its two terminals, behaving like a wire
It turns the whole chip off
It stores a charge for later
items.explanation: A closed switch conducts, so it acts like a wire joining its two terminals. An open switch is the one that blocks current. This on/off behavior is what lets transistors steer the output.

items.prompt: In the two-switch NOT gate (inverter), when the input is 0, what happens?
choices: The bottom switch closes, so the output is 0
The top switch closes, connecting the output to the supply, so the output is 1
Both switches close at once
Both switches open, leaving the output floating
items.explanation: With input 0, the top switch closes and ties the output to the supply, giving a 1 - the opposite of the input. Closing both at once, or opening both, would be a broken or unreliable gate.

items.prompt: Why is it important that exactly one switch path is connected at a time in a gate?
choices: So the output is firmly tied to a 1 or 0 and never left dangling
To save the manufacturer money on transistors
Because two paths would make the gate faster
So the gate can store two bits at once
items.explanation: If the output were left disconnected (dangling), its voltage would be undefined and unreliable. Connecting exactly one path - to the supply or to ground - gives a clean, definite 1 or 0.

items.prompt: The output of a NOT gate is the opposite of its input. If you feed in a 1, the output is:
choices: Both 1 and 0
Undefined
items.explanation: NOT inverts: input 1 gives output 0, input 0 gives output 1. With input 1 the bottom switch closes and ties the output to ground (a 0).

items.prompt: What two electrical references does a gate connect its output between?
choices: Two different ground wires
The supply (read as 1) and ground (read as 0)
An input and an output only
A clock and a reset line
items.explanation: A gate sits between the supply voltage (logic 1) and ground (logic 0). The switches decide which of these the output is tied to.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the big idea of this lesson?
choices: Logic gates are abstract math with no physical form
Logic gates are small circuits of transistor switches that steer the output to a 1 or a 0
Every gate needs its own microprocessor
Gates store bits using spinning disks
items.explanation: The whole point is that the logic you know is implemented physically: gates are circuits of transistor switches steering an output between supply and ground. The other options misdescribe how gates work.

lessons.title: Logic Levels: 1 and 0 Are Voltage Ranges
lessons.content:
**Hook:** It is tempting to think a logic 1 is exactly some voltage and a 0 is exactly 0 V. In real circuits that is never true. Wires pick up tiny voltage wobbles (electrical **noise**), and no chip is perfect. So instead of single values, a 1 and a 0 are each a whole **range** of voltages. Understanding these ranges is what keeps digital systems reliable.

**Build:** Picture the voltage from 0 V up to the supply as a tall bar. The top band counts as logic **1**, the bottom band counts as logic **0**, and in the middle there is a **forbidden zone** - a band where the chip is not sure which it is. A good signal spends almost no time there; it jumps quickly through.

Four key voltages mark the bands, and they come in two pairs. The chip that *sends* a signal (the **driver**) promises its outputs land in safe bands: $V_{OH}$ (output-high) is the lowest voltage it promises for a 1, and $V_{OL}$ (output-low) is the highest voltage it promises for a 0. The chip that *receives* the signal makes its own demands: $V_{IH}$ (input-high) is the lowest voltage it will accept as a 1, and $V_{IL}$ (input-low) is the highest voltage it will accept as a 0. The 'O' stands for output (the driver), the 'I' for input (the receiver).

Now the payoff. The driver sends a 1 at least as high as $V_{OH}$. The receiver only needs $V_{IH}$. The gap between them is spare room - called the **noise margin**:
$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}, \qquad NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}.$$
The high-side noise margin $NM_H$ is how much the 1 can sag (from noise) before the receiver might mistake it for not-a-1. The low-side margin $NM_L$ is the same idea for a 0. Bigger margins mean a tougher, more noise-proof signal. A negative margin means the signal can be misread even with no noise at all - a broken design.

**Recap:** Logic 1 and 0 are voltage ranges with a forbidden zone between. $V_{OH}, V_{OL}, V_{IH}, V_{IL}$ define the bands, and the leftover slack - the noise margin - is how much electrical noise the signal can shrug off.

items.prompt: Why are logic 1 and logic 0 defined as voltage ranges rather than single exact voltages?
choices: Because chips are too cheap to be precise
Because real signals pick up noise and no chip is perfect, so a band of voltages must count as each level
Because voltage cannot be measured exactly
Because the supply changes randomly every second
items.explanation: Noise wobbles the voltage and chips have tolerances, so a 1 (or 0) is a whole band of acceptable voltages. This is what makes digital reliable despite imperfect signals.

items.prompt: What is the 'forbidden zone' between the 1-band and the 0-band?
choices: A voltage range where the receiver cannot reliably tell 1 from 0
A region where the chip is destroyed
The exact midpoint voltage, which is always a 1
The voltage used only by the power supply
items.explanation: The forbidden zone is the middle band where the level is undefined - the receiver might read it either way. A good signal passes through it quickly and never parks there. It does not destroy the chip.

items.prompt: Which two voltages are promised by the DRIVER (the chip sending the signal)?
choices: V_IH and V_IL
V_OH and V_OL
V_OH and V_IH
V_IL and V_OL
items.explanation: The 'O' means output, which belongs to the driver. V_OH is its guaranteed lowest 1, V_OL its guaranteed highest 0. The 'I' (input) values belong to the receiver.

items.prompt: V_IH is best described as:
choices: The highest voltage the receiver will still call a 0
The lowest voltage the receiver will accept as a 1
The supply voltage of the driver
The output voltage when the driver sends a 1
items.explanation: V_IH (input-high) is the receiver's demand: any input at or above V_IH is read as a 1. The highest voltage still read as 0 is V_IL; output voltages are the V_O values.

items.prompt: The high-side noise margin is NM_H = V_OH - V_IH. What does it physically mean?
choices: How much the supply voltage can rise
How much a logic 1 can sag (from noise) before the receiver might fail to read it as a 1
How fast the signal switches
The total power the chip uses
items.explanation: NM_H is the spare room between what the driver guarantees for a 1 and what the receiver minimally needs. Noise smaller than this margin cannot flip the 1 into a misread. It is not about supply rise, speed, or power.

items.prompt: A driver guarantees V_OH = 2.4 V. A receiver requires V_IH = 2.0 V. What is the high-side noise margin?
items.explanation: NM_H = V_OH - V_IH = 2.4 - 2.0 = 0.4 V. It is positive, so a 1 can pick up up to 0.4 V of disturbance and still be read correctly.

items.prompt: If a calculated noise margin comes out NEGATIVE, what does that tell you?
choices: The signal has extra protection against noise
The signal can be misread even with no noise at all - the design is broken for that direction
The chip will run cooler
Nothing; negative margins are normal
items.explanation: A negative margin means the driver does not even reach the level the receiver demands, so the bit can be wrong before any noise is added. That connection needs fixing (e.g., a level shifter).

items.prompt: Larger noise margins are desirable because they:
choices: Make the chip use more power
Let the signal tolerate more electrical noise before a bit is misread
Shrink the forbidden zone to zero
Increase the clock frequency automatically
items.explanation: More margin means more spare room between what is sent and what must be received, so more noise can be absorbed safely. It does not change power, the forbidden zone width, or the clock by itself.

items.prompt: Why should a signal spend as little time as possible in the forbidden zone?
choices: Because the receiver cannot reliably interpret a voltage parked there
Because the forbidden zone uses up the battery faster than any other level
Because it is illegal to measure those voltages
Because the forbidden zone is where data is stored
items.explanation: In the forbidden zone the level is undefined, so the receiver may read it inconsistently. Signals should jump quickly through it between a clean 0 and a clean 1.

lessons.title: How CMOS Builds Gates, and Where the Power Goes
lessons.content:
**Hook:** Almost every chip you own uses one recipe for its switches, called **CMOS** (complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor). Knowing how CMOS arranges its switches explains two everyday facts: why a phone in standby barely sips power, and why a busy processor gets hot.

**Build:** CMOS uses two flavors of transistor switch. One flavor (called **NMOS**) is good at connecting a wire down to ground - it makes a clean 0. The other (called **PMOS**) is good at connecting a wire up to the supply - it makes a clean 1. A CMOS gate puts a group of PMOS switches on top (the **pull-up network**, which can pull the output up to the supply) and a group of NMOS switches on the bottom (the **pull-down network**, which can pull the output down to ground). They are wired as opposites: for any input, one network conducts and the other is off. So the output is always driven firmly to a 1 or a 0, and - crucially - there is no straight-through path from supply to ground in a settled state.

That last fact is why **static power** (the power used while nothing is switching) is tiny: with no through-path, almost no current leaks from supply to ground. This is why a CMOS gadget sitting idle barely drains its battery.

The power that *does* matter shows up when the gate **switches**. Every wire and input it drives behaves like a small bucket for charge - a **capacitance** $C$. Each time the output flips, that capacitance is filled to the supply voltage $V$ and then emptied, and that costs energy. Do it $f$ times per second and the **dynamic power** is
$$P = C\,V^2\,f.$$
Notice the shape: power grows straight-line with how fast you switch ($f$), but with the *square* of the voltage ($V^2$). That squared term is the headline. Halving the voltage cuts switching power to one quarter. This single fact is why chip supply voltages keep dropping (you will see the 5 V to 3.3 V to 1.8 V trend next lesson) and why a flat-out processor running fast at higher voltage runs hot.

**Recap:** CMOS pairs a PMOS pull-up with an NMOS pull-down so exactly one conducts - giving clean outputs and near-zero idle (static) power. The real cost is dynamic power, $P = CV^2 f$, dominated by the voltage-squared term.

items.prompt: In a CMOS gate, what is the pull-up network's job?
choices: To connect the output down to ground (a 0)
To connect the output up to the supply (a 1)
To store the previous output
To generate the clock signal
items.explanation: The pull-up network (made of PMOS switches) can pull the output up to the supply, producing a 1. The pull-down network handles connecting to ground (a 0).

items.prompt: Why does the pull-down network use NMOS and the pull-up use PMOS?
choices: NMOS makes a clean connection to ground (a clean 0); PMOS makes a clean connection to the supply (a clean 1)
It is purely random which one is used where
NMOS is cheaper so it is always on the bottom
PMOS cannot switch at all
items.explanation: Each switch type is good at one job: NMOS pulls down to a solid 0, PMOS pulls up to a solid 1. Using them this way gives a full, clean swing between the rails.

items.prompt: In a settled (not switching) CMOS gate, why is there almost no current flowing from supply to ground?
choices: A resistor blocks it
One of the two networks is always off, so there is no straight-through path
A capacitor stores all the current
The clock turns the gate off
items.explanation: The pull-up and pull-down are opposites, so in any settled state one is off and breaks the path between supply and ground. That is why static (idle) power is tiny.

items.prompt: Why does a CMOS gadget sitting idle barely drain its battery?
choices: It switches off its supply completely
Static power is tiny because no through-path exists when nothing is switching
It runs on solar power
Idle chips reverse the current back into the battery
items.explanation: With nothing switching, there is no charging/discharging and no through-path, so only tiny leakage flows. That low static power is a defining CMOS advantage.

items.prompt: In the dynamic power equation $P = C V^2 f$, what does C represent?
choices: The clock count
The capacitance (the 'bucket' of charge) on the wires and inputs being driven
The current in amps
The number of gates
items.explanation: C is the capacitance that must be charged and discharged each time the output switches - the wires and the inputs it drives act like a small charge bucket. f is the switching rate and V the supply voltage.

items.prompt: A CMOS block uses 40 mW of dynamic power. If you halve the supply voltage (same C and frequency), the dynamic power becomes about:
choices: 20 mW
10 mW
40 mW
5 mW
items.explanation: Power goes as voltage squared. Halving V multiplies power by (1/2)^2 = 1/4, so 40 mW becomes 10 mW. Halving would only give 20 mW if power were linear in V, which it is not.

items.prompt: Doubling only the switching frequency f (same V and C) changes dynamic power by a factor of:
choices: 1 (no change)
items.explanation: Power is linear in frequency in $P = C V^2 f$, so doubling f doubles the power. The squared relationship applies to voltage, not frequency.

items.prompt: In $P = C V^2 f$, which term is squared, and why does that matter?
choices: The frequency f - so speed dominates power
The voltage V - so lowering the supply is the most powerful way to cut power
The capacitance C - so fewer wires matters most
Nothing is squared; all terms are equal
items.explanation: Voltage is squared, so a small drop in V gives a large drop in power. This is exactly why chip designers keep lowering the supply voltage to save power and heat.

items.prompt: Why does a processor running at full speed get noticeably hot?
choices: Because static (idle) power is huge in CMOS
Because high switching frequency (and often higher voltage) raises dynamic power, which turns into heat
Because the pull-up network melts
Because it stops using CMOS when busy
items.explanation: Heat comes from dynamic power, which rises with switching frequency and with the square of voltage. A busy, fast chip switches its capacitances rapidly, dissipating that energy as heat. Static power stays small.

lessons.title: Logic Families, Supply Rails, Fan-Out & Drive
lessons.content:
**Hook:** Not all chips speak the same electrical 'dialect.' An old chip from the 1980s and a modern one may both do AND and OR, but at different voltages and with different abilities. A group of chips that share the same electrical recipe is called a **logic family**. Knowing the families is how you tell, at a glance, whether two parts will get along.

**Build:** The old family is **TTL** (transistor-transistor logic, the classic 74xx parts). It runs at 5 volts, but its idea of a '1' is weak - it may only guarantee about 2.4 V for a high output. The modern families are all **CMOS** (the recipe from the last lesson). You will hear names like **HC** (a common 5 V CMOS family) and **LVC** (a fast low-voltage CMOS family). They differ in voltage and speed, but all share the clean, low-power CMOS style.

A big trend ties this together: **supply voltages have steadily dropped** - 5 V, then 3.3 V, then 1.8 V, and lower. The reason is the lesson you just learned: power grows with voltage squared, so lower voltage means far less power and heat. The catch is that a 5 V gadget and a 1.8 V gadget no longer share the same idea of '1', so mixing them can fail unless you check the levels (you will handle the fix - level shifting - next lesson).

Two more practical traits separate families. **Drive strength** is how much current an output can push or pull; stronger drive can charge wires faster and feed more loads. **Fan-out** is how many inputs a single output can safely drive. For old TTL, fan-out is a hard count, because each input it feeds draws real current. For CMOS, inputs draw almost no steady current, so the limit is different: each extra input adds capacitance, which slows the signal's edges and raises dynamic power ($P = CV^2 f$ again). Either way, overloading an output degrades the voltage levels and the timing.

**Recap:** A logic family is a shared electrical recipe. TTL is the old 5 V family; modern CMOS families like HC and LVC run at ever-lower voltages (5 V to 3.3 V to 1.8 V) to save power. Drive strength sets how hard an output pushes, and fan-out sets how many inputs it can feed before levels or timing suffer.

items.prompt: What is a 'logic family'?
choices: A group of engineers who design chips
