A group of chips that share the same electrical recipe (voltages, drive, switching style)
A single chip with many gates inside
The set of all AND gates ever made
items.explanation: A logic family is a set of parts built to a common electrical standard - same supply expectations, level definitions, and drive behavior - so parts in a family interoperate cleanly.

items.prompt: Which describes the older TTL family compared with modern CMOS?
choices: It runs at 5 V and its guaranteed '1' output can be fairly weak (around 2.4 V)
It runs at 1.8 V with very strong outputs
It uses no supply voltage at all
It is the newest, lowest-power family available
items.explanation: Classic TTL is a 5 V family whose high output is only guaranteed to about 2.4 V - a weaker '1' than full CMOS. Modern CMOS families came later and run at lower voltages.

items.prompt: Supply voltages have trended 5 V to 3.3 V to 1.8 V and lower. What is the main reason?
choices: Lower voltage looks better on datasheets
Power grows with the square of voltage, so lower voltage greatly reduces power and heat
Lower voltage makes chips physically larger
It increases the noise margin automatically
items.explanation: From $P = C V^2 f$, dropping the voltage slashes power because of the squared term. Saving power and heat is the driving reason for the falling supply rails. It actually tends to shrink noise margins, not grow them.

items.prompt: A 5 V gadget and a 1.8 V gadget both do logic. Why can connecting them directly still fail?
choices: They use different programming languages
They no longer share the same voltage idea of '1' and '0', so a signal from one may land in the other's forbidden zone
1.8 V chips cannot do AND
5 V chips are always broken
items.explanation: Different supply voltages mean different level thresholds. A 1.8 V output may be too low to register as a '1' on a 5 V input (or a 5 V output too high for a 1.8 V input). You must check the levels or add a level shifter.

items.prompt: What does an output's 'drive strength' describe?
choices: How many different chips it was made by
How much current the output can push or pull, which sets how fast it charges wires and how many loads it can feed
The color code printed on the chip
The clock frequency of the whole board
items.explanation: Drive strength is the output's current-pushing ability. Stronger drive charges capacitances faster (sharper edges) and can supply more inputs.

items.prompt: What does 'fan-out' mean?
choices: How fast the chip cooling fan spins
How many inputs a single output can safely drive
How many outputs feed one input
The number of pins on the chip
items.explanation: Fan-out is the number of downstream inputs one output can reliably drive. Exceed it and levels and timing degrade.

items.prompt: Why is the fan-out limit for CMOS mostly about capacitance rather than steady current?
choices: CMOS inputs draw heavy steady current
CMOS inputs draw almost no steady current, so the real cost of each added input is the capacitance it adds, slowing edges and raising dynamic power
CMOS outputs cannot drive any inputs
Capacitance has no effect in CMOS
items.explanation: A CMOS input sips essentially no DC current, so you can feed many in terms of current. The limit is that each input adds capacitance, slowing rise/fall times and raising $C V^2 f$ power - a timing and power limit, not a current count.

items.prompt: HC and LVC are examples of what kind of family?
choices: Old bipolar TTL families
Modern CMOS families (HC is a common 5 V CMOS family; LVC is a fast low-voltage CMOS family)
Mechanical relay families
Analog amplifier families
items.explanation: Both HC and LVC are CMOS families. HC is a widely used 5 V CMOS line; LVC is a fast low-voltage CMOS line. They share the clean, low-static-power CMOS style.

items.prompt: What happens if you connect far more inputs to one output than its fan-out allows?
choices: Nothing changes; outputs drive infinite loads
The voltage levels and the signal timing degrade, risking misreads and slow, sloppy edges
The output voltage doubles
The chip switches families automatically
items.explanation: Overloading an output weakens its ability to reach clean levels and slows its edges, hurting both noise margins and timing. That is exactly why fan-out limits exist.

lessons.title: Floating Inputs, Open-Drain, Level Shifting & Decoupling Caps
lessons.content:
**Hook:** This lesson covers the everyday rules that keep real boards working: what to do with unused pins, how chips share one wire, how to connect parts that run at different voltages, and the one tiny part that goes next to every chip.

**Build:** First, **never leave a CMOS input unconnected (floating)**. With nothing driving it, its voltage drifts toward the forbidden zone, where the gate is half-on. It can pick up noise, flicker between 1 and 0, and waste power. The fix is a **pull resistor**: a resistor (often around 10 k-ohm) tying the pin to the supply (a **pull-up**, parks it at 1) or to ground (a **pull-down**, parks it at 0). This gives the input a definite, quiet level.

Next, sharing a wire. A normal output forces both 1 and 0, so two outputs on one wire would fight. An **open-drain** output is gentler: it can only pull the wire LOW or let go (release it). To make a 1, you add a single **pull-up resistor** that gently raises the released line to the supply. Now many chips can share one line safely: the line reads 1 only if *every* chip releases it, and any single chip pulling LOW wins. This 'all must agree to be high' behavior is called **wired-AND**, and it is exactly how shared buses (such as the common two-wire I2C bus) and shared alarm lines work.

Third, **level shifting**. When two parts run at different voltages and a noise margin would go negative (recall: that means a guaranteed misread), you cannot wire them directly. A **level shifter** sits between them and translates a 1 in one voltage world into a 1 in the other. The open-drain trick helps here too: the pull-up resistor can go to whichever supply the receiving side uses, so the released line rises to that side's idea of a 1.

Finally, the **decoupling capacitor** (also called a bypass cap). Every time a chip switches, it suddenly demands a gulp of charge (that $C V^2 f$ energy has to come from somewhere). The distant power supply cannot react fast enough, so its voltage would dip and create noise. A small capacitor - classically **0.1 microfarad (uF)** - placed right at the chip's power pins acts as a tiny local reservoir, supplying that instant gulp and keeping the chip's supply steady. That is why every chip gets its own 0.1 uF cap.

**Recap:** Tie unused inputs with pull resistors so they never float. Open-drain outputs with one pull-up let many chips share a wire (wired-AND), as on I2C. Use a level shifter when voltage worlds differ and margins go negative. And give every chip a ~0.1 uF decoupling cap to feed its switching gulps and keep the supply quiet.

items.prompt: Why is leaving a CMOS input floating (unconnected) a bad idea?
choices: It permanently damages the chip instantly
Its voltage drifts into the forbidden zone, so it can flicker, pick up noise, and waste power
It makes the input run faster
Floating inputs are required for normal operation
items.explanation: With nothing driving it, a floating input drifts near the undefined middle, where the gate is partly on. It can oscillate, respond to noise, and dissipate extra power. Tie it to a definite level instead.

items.prompt: What is the fix for an unused input that must not float?
choices: A pull resistor to the supply (pull-up) or to ground (pull-down) to park it at a definite level
A second power supply
A large inductor in series
Nothing - just leave it open
items.explanation: A pull resistor (often around 10 k-ohm) ties the pin to a known level: pull-up to the supply parks it at 1, pull-down to ground parks it at 0. This keeps the input quiet and defined.

items.prompt: An open-drain output differs from a normal output because it can:
choices: Force both a 1 and a 0 by itself
Only pull the line LOW or release it; an external pull-up resistor provides the 1
Generate its own clock
Supply unlimited current
items.explanation: Open-drain can actively pull low or let go (release). It cannot drive high on its own - an external pull-up resistor raises the released line to a 1. That is what makes safe wire-sharing possible.

items.prompt: On a shared open-drain bus with one pull-up, the line reads 1 only when:
choices: At least one chip pulls it low
Every chip on the line has released it (none is pulling low)
The pull-up resistor is removed
Exactly one chip is active
items.explanation: This is wired-AND: any single chip pulling LOW forces the whole line low, so the line is HIGH only when all chips release it and the pull-up takes over.

items.prompt: The 'all must agree to be high, any one can pull low' behavior of an open-drain bus is the basis of which common interface?
choices: The I2C two-wire bus
A power supply rail
An audio amplifier
A crystal oscillator
items.explanation: I2C uses open-drain lines with pull-ups precisely so multiple devices can share the two wires safely (wired-AND). It is also used for shared interrupt and handshake lines.

items.prompt: A 1.8 V chip's output drives a 5 V chip's input. You compute the high-side noise margin and it is negative. The two parts will NOT interoperate directly. What should you do?
choices: Connect them anyway; a negative margin is fine
Insert a level shifter so the 1.8 V '1' is translated into a valid 5 V '1'
Increase the clock frequency
Remove all pull resistors
items.explanation: A negative noise margin means the 1.8 V output never reaches the 5 V input's required high level - a guaranteed misread. A level shifter translates between the two voltage worlds so margins become positive. Wiring them directly would fail.

items.prompt: How can the open-drain trick also help bridge two different supply voltages?
choices: By removing the pull-up entirely
By tying the pull-up resistor to whichever supply the receiving side uses, so the released line rises to that side's '1'
By running both chips at 0 V
Open-drain cannot help with different voltages
items.explanation: Since the high level on an open-drain line is set by the external pull-up, connecting that pull-up to the receiver's supply makes the line reach the receiver's idea of a 1 - a simple form of level translation.

items.prompt: Why does every chip get its own small (~0.1 uF) decoupling capacitor right at its power pins?
choices: To store data when the power is off
To act as a tiny local charge reservoir that supplies the sudden gulp of current each switching event needs, keeping the chip's supply steady
To slow the chip down on purpose
To replace the need for a real power supply
items.explanation: Switching draws charge suddenly (the $C V^2 f$ energy). The distant supply reacts too slowly, so a local cap right at the pins delivers that instant gulp and stops the local voltage from dipping and creating noise. It does not store data or replace the supply.

items.prompt: A pull-up resistor and a pull-down resistor differ in that:
choices: A pull-up ties the pin to ground; a pull-down ties it to the supply
A pull-up ties the pin to the supply (parks it at 1); a pull-down ties it to ground (parks it at 0)
Both tie the pin to the supply
Neither affects the input's level
items.explanation: A pull-up connects to the supply and parks an idle input at 1; a pull-down connects to ground and parks it at 0. Choosing one gives the floating-prone input a definite resting level.

items.prompt: On the open-drain bus, two chips share the line. Chip A pulls LOW and chip B releases. What does the line read, and why?
choices: 1, because chip B released it
0, because any chip pulling low wins (wired-AND)
Forbidden zone, because the chips disagree
It oscillates between 0 and 1
items.explanation: In wired-AND, a single chip pulling LOW dominates the shared line, so it reads 0. The line is high only when every chip releases. There is no disagreement state - low always wins.

lessons.title: From Spec to Circuit: How We Build Logic
lessons.content:
## Hook

You already know logic gates (AND, OR, NOT) and a little Boolean algebra. But how do engineers turn a real-world need into an actual circuit? This lesson shows the simple recipe they follow every time.

## Build

First, a key word: **combinational** logic means the output depends *only* on the inputs you give it *right now*. There is **no memory** of the past. Press the same buttons and you always get the same answer, instantly. (Later you will meet *sequential* logic, which does remember.)

Think of a vending machine's coin checker: given the coins in the slot right now, it decides yes or no. It does not care what happened yesterday. That is combinational behavior.

The design recipe has three steps:

1. **Spec** - write down, in plain words, what the circuit must do. Example: "Turn on the alarm if a door is open AND the system is armed."
2. **Truth table** - list every possible combination of inputs and the output you want for each. A circuit with $n$ inputs has $2^n$ rows.
3. **Logic / gates** - read the table and build gates that produce those outputs. Often we simplify first so we use fewer gates.

A **truth table** is just a complete list: every input pattern on the left, the desired output on the right. Because it covers *all* cases, it is the honest, complete description of any combinational circuit.

From these gates we build reusable **building blocks** that do useful jobs: a *multiplexer* (a selectable switch), a *decoder* (turns a code into one active line), an *adder* (adds numbers), and an *ALU* (the calculator inside a CPU). The rest of this sub-topic builds them up one at a time.

## Recap

Combinational logic has no memory: output depends only on present inputs. The flow is always spec, then truth table, then gates. Master this recipe and every block ahead is just a bigger example of it.

items.prompt: A circuit has 3 inputs. How many rows does its complete truth table have?
choices: 3 rows
6 rows
8 rows
9 rows
items.explanation: A truth table lists every combination of inputs. With n inputs there are 2^n combinations, and 2^3 = 8. It is 8, not 3 (that is just the input count) and not 9 (that would be 3 squared, which is not how combinations grow).

items.prompt: What does it mean that a circuit is 'combinational'?
choices: Its output depends only on the inputs present right now, with no memory of the past
It combines several clock signals together
Its output depends on past inputs as well as present ones
It can only use AND and OR gates
items.explanation: Combinational means memoryless: the same inputs always give the same output, immediately. A circuit that remembers past inputs is sequential, not combinational. The word has nothing to do with clocks or with which gates are allowed.

items.prompt: What is the correct order of the standard design flow?
choices: Gates, then truth table, then spec
Spec, then truth table, then logic/gates
Truth table, then spec, then gates
Spec, then gates, then truth table
items.explanation: You first state what the circuit must do (spec), then list the desired output for every input pattern (truth table), then build the gates. Starting from gates would be guessing; the spec must come first.

items.prompt: Why is a truth table considered a complete description of a combinational circuit?
choices: It uses the fewest possible gates
It lists the output for every possible input combination, leaving nothing out
It shows how the circuit changes over time
It only lists the cases where the output is 1
items.explanation: Because it covers all 2^n input patterns, the truth table specifies the output in every case, so nothing is undefined. It says nothing about gate count, and it is not about time (that would be sequential logic).

items.prompt: A coin checker outputs 'accept' based only on the coins inserted right now. Which kind of logic is this?
choices: Sequential, because money is involved
Combinational, because the output depends only on the present inputs
Neither - it is analog
Sequential, because it must remember previous coins
items.explanation: If the decision uses only the inputs present at this moment and nothing remembered, it is combinational. A checker that summed coins across multiple insertions would need memory, making that part sequential.

items.prompt: Why do engineers usually simplify the logic before building the gates?
choices: To use fewer gates, saving space, cost, and delay
To change what the circuit does
Because simplified logic is always sequential
To add more inputs to the circuit
items.explanation: Simplification keeps the same truth table (same behavior) but reaches it with fewer gates, which costs less and is often faster. It must never change what the circuit does; if it did, it would be wrong.

items.prompt: Spec: 'Sound the alarm only when the door is open AND the system is armed.' Which single gate matches this?
choices: An OR gate fed by 'door open' and 'armed'
An AND gate fed by 'door open' and 'armed'
A NOT gate on 'armed'
An XOR gate fed by 'door open' and 'armed'
items.explanation: 'Both conditions true' is exactly the job of an AND gate. OR would also fire on just one condition; XOR would fire only when the two conditions differ, neither of which matches the spec.

items.prompt: In this sub-topic, what is a 'building block' like a multiplexer or adder?
choices: A type of memory cell that stores one bit
A reusable circuit, made from gates, that performs a useful job
A single transistor
A clock generator
items.explanation: Building blocks are reusable combinational circuits assembled from basic gates to do a common task (select an input, add numbers, etc.). They are not memory cells, single transistors, or clocks.

items.prompt: How many input combinations must you check for a circuit with 4 inputs?
items.explanation: Every input can be 0 or 1, so n inputs give 2^n combinations. For 4 inputs that is 2^4 = 16. The count doubles each time you add an input.

lessons.title: Selectors: Multiplexers, Demuxes, Decoders & Encoders
lessons.content:
## Hook

Imagine a TV with several inputs - HDMI 1, HDMI 2, an old game console - and one screen. A little switch inside picks which source reaches the screen. That switch is a **multiplexer**, one of the most-used building blocks in all of digital design.

## Build

A **multiplexer** (mux) is a selectable switch. It has several **data inputs**, a single **output**, and some **select lines** that choose which input gets connected to the output. With $n$ select lines you can choose among $2^n$ inputs. A 4:1 mux has 2 select lines and picks one of 4 inputs; the 2-bit select code (like a channel number) names the chosen input.

Everyday hooks: a mux is a TV input selector, or a railroad switch that routes one train onto one track. The simplest mux is the 2:1: when the select is 0 the output follows input 0; when select is 1 it follows input 1.

There is a neat bonus: a mux can act as a **universal logic element**. If you put your variables on the select lines and wire each data input to the 0 or 1 you want for that row of the truth table, the mux reproduces *any* function you like. Each select code simply looks up one row.

A **demultiplexer** (demux) is the mux run backwards: one input, many outputs, and the select lines decide which single output the input is routed to. Think of one delivery truck (the input) and a switch that sends it down one of several streets.

A **decoder** turns a binary code into exactly one active output line. A 2-to-4 decoder takes a 2-bit code and raises exactly one of its 4 outputs (the rest stay 0). Everyday hook: a room number chooses which one room's light turns on. Because each output marks one specific input code, a decoder is a handy way to detect any pattern.

An **encoder** does the reverse: given one active input line, it outputs the binary code for that line. But a plain encoder breaks if two inputs are active at once. A **priority encoder** fixes this: it outputs the code of the *highest-priority* active input and ignores the lower ones, so there is always a clear answer.

## Recap

Mux = selectable switch (many in, one out). Demux = one in, many out. Decoder = code in, one line out. Encoder = one line in, code out; the priority version handles ties.

items.prompt: A 4:1 multiplexer has its select lines set to S1S0 = 10 (binary for 2). Which input appears at the output?
choices: I0
I1
I2
I3
items.explanation: The select code is a number naming the chosen input. Binary 10 equals 2, so input I2 is connected to the output. Reading the code as anything other than its binary value is the common mistake here.

items.prompt: In a 2:1 multiplexer, what appears at output Y when the select line S = 0?
choices: Input I0
Input I1
Always 0
Always 1
items.explanation: When S = 0 the mux connects data input I0 to the output; when S = 1 it connects I1. The output is not a fixed constant - it follows whichever input the select line chooses.

items.prompt: How many select lines does an 8:1 multiplexer need?
items.explanation: To choose among 8 inputs you need a code that can count to 8, and 2^3 = 8, so 3 select lines. Two lines would only pick among 4 inputs; eight lines is far more than needed.

items.prompt: In one phrase, what does a multiplexer do?
choices: Adds several numbers together
Picks one of several inputs and sends it to a single output
Stores a bit until the next clock
Splits one input across many outputs
items.explanation: A mux is a selectable switch: many inputs in, one chosen input out, controlled by the select lines. Splitting one input to many outputs is the opposite device, the demultiplexer.

items.prompt: How does a demultiplexer differ from a multiplexer?
choices: It has one input and routes it to one of many outputs
It has no select lines
It adds binary numbers
It is exactly the same circuit
items.explanation: A demux reverses the mux: a single data input is steered to one chosen output. Both use select lines; the difference is direction (one-to-many versus many-to-one).

items.prompt: A 2-to-4 decoder receives the input code A1A0 = 10. What do its four outputs Y0..Y3 look like?
choices: All four outputs are 1
Only Y2 is 1; Y0, Y1, Y3 are 0
Only Y0 is 1; the rest are 0
All four outputs are 0
items.explanation: A decoder raises exactly one output - the one matching the input code. Code 10 is binary 2, so Y2 goes high and the others stay low. A decoder never raises more than one line at a time.

items.prompt: How many outputs does a 3-to-8 decoder have, and how many are active at once?
choices: 3 outputs, all active
8 outputs, exactly one active
8 outputs, all active
3 outputs, exactly one active
items.explanation: An n-to-2^n decoder turns an n-bit code into 2^n output lines and activates exactly the one matching the code. For n = 3 that is 8 outputs with a single one high.

items.prompt: How can a multiplexer act as a 'universal' logic element that builds any function?
choices: By putting the variables on the select lines and wiring each data input to the 0 or 1 wanted for that truth-table row
By removing all of its select lines
Only if every data input is tied to 1
It cannot - a mux can only copy one input
items.explanation: Drive the select lines with your input variables; each select code then picks one data input. Set each data input to the output value that row of the truth table needs, and the mux reproduces any function. Tying all inputs to 1 would just output a constant 1.

items.prompt: What does an encoder do, and what problem does a priority encoder solve?
choices: It activates one output from a code; priority versions run faster
It outputs the binary code of an active input line; the priority version resolves the case where two inputs are active by choosing the highest-priority one
It adds two numbers; the priority version handles carries
It stores data; the priority version stores more
items.explanation: An encoder is the reverse of a decoder: given an active input line it outputs that line's binary code. If two inputs are active a plain encoder gives a wrong answer; a priority encoder picks the highest-priority active input so the output is always well defined.

items.prompt: In an 8-to-3 priority encoder (higher index = higher priority), inputs D5 and D2 are both active. What 3-bit code does it output?
choices: 010 (for D2)
101 (for D5)
items.explanation: The highest-priority active input wins. D5 outranks D2, so the output is the binary code for 5, which is 101. A plain encoder would garble the result; the priority rule is what makes the answer clean.

lessons.title: Comparators, Parity & Code Converters
lessons.content:
## Hook

Not every circuit moves data around - some *judge* it. Is this number equal to that one? Is it bigger? Did this message arrive without a typo? These everyday questions map onto small combinational blocks.

## Build

A **comparator** compares two binary numbers. The simplest is an **equality** comparator: it answers 'are A and B the same?' The trick is to compare bit by bit. An **XNOR** gate outputs 1 only when its two inputs match, so XNOR each pair of bits and AND all the results together. If every pair matches, the AND outputs 1 (equal); a single mismatch pulls it to 0.

A **magnitude** comparator answers 'is A greater than, equal to, or less than B?' It works the way you compare multi-digit decimal numbers: start at the **most significant bit** (the leftmost, highest-value bit). Whoever has a 1 where the other has a 0 at the highest differing position is larger. You only look at lower bits when the higher bits tie.

**Parity** is a cheap way to catch errors when data is sent over a wire. A **parity bit** is one extra bit added so the total number of 1s comes out even (for 'even parity'). To make it, XOR all the data bits together. At the far end, a checker XORs everything again; if the count of 1s is no longer even, a bit got flipped. The catch: simple parity only catches an **odd** number of flipped bits. If *two* bits flip, the count stays even and the error slips through. It also cannot fix errors, only flag them.

**Code converters** translate one binary code into another - just a function you tabulate and build. A common one is **binary to Gray code**: Gray code is arranged so that neighbors differ by exactly one bit. That single-bit-change property prevents glitches in position sensors and is why Gray code shows up in encoders. Another familiar converter is the **7-segment decoder**, which takes a 4-bit digit and lights the right segments on a numeric display - a pure truth-table-to-gates job.

## Recap

Comparators judge equality (XNOR + AND) or magnitude (most-significant bit first). Parity is a one-bit error *detector* (XOR everything) that misses even-count errors. Code converters re-map one code to another, like binary-to-Gray or the 7-segment display decoder.

items.prompt: Which gate outputs 1 exactly when its two inputs are equal, making it the heart of an equality comparator?
choices: XOR
XNOR
NAND
OR
items.explanation: XNOR outputs 1 when both inputs match (0,0 or 1,1) and 0 when they differ - exactly 'are these bits equal?'. XOR is the opposite (1 when they differ), so it is the wrong choice for detecting equality.

items.prompt: To check whether two 4-bit numbers are equal, you XNOR each bit pair. What do you do with the four XNOR outputs?
choices: OR them together
AND them together
XOR them together
Add them as numbers
items.explanation: The numbers are equal only if every bit pair matches, i.e. all four XNORs are 1. AND requires all inputs to be 1, so it gives 1 only on full equality. OR would report 'equal' even if just one pair happened to match.

items.prompt: When comparing the magnitude of two binary numbers, where do you start?
choices: At the least significant (rightmost) bit
At the most significant (leftmost) bit
At a randomly chosen bit
In the middle and work outward
items.explanation: The most significant bit carries the most weight, just like the leftmost digit of a decimal number. The first position where the numbers differ, read from the top, decides which is larger. Starting from the right can give the wrong answer.

items.prompt: How do you compute an even-parity bit for a set of data bits?
choices: AND all the data bits
XOR all the data bits together
Count the bits and divide by two
Take the most significant bit
items.explanation: XORing all the bits gives 1 when there is an odd number of 1s. Appending that XOR result makes the overall count of 1s even - that is the even-parity bit. AND or a single bit would not track the count of 1s.

items.prompt: A byte protected by even parity arrives with TWO bits flipped. What does the parity checker report?
choices: It detects and corrects both errors
It detects the error and names the bad bits
It reports no error - the two flips cancel out in the parity count
It always reports a hardware fault
items.explanation: Two flips change the count of 1s by an even amount, so the parity stays correct and the checker sees nothing wrong. Simple parity only catches an odd number of flips and can never correct errors - that is its core weakness.

items.prompt: What can single-bit parity actually do for you?
choices: Correct any single-bit error automatically
Detect that an odd number of bits flipped, but not fix it
Detect and correct any number of errors
Encrypt the data
items.explanation: Parity is detection only, and only for an odd number of flipped bits. It tells you something is wrong but cannot say which bit or repair it. Correction needs stronger schemes like Hamming codes.

items.prompt: What is special about Gray code that makes it useful in position sensors?
choices: Each value uses fewer bits than binary
Consecutive values differ by exactly one bit, avoiding glitches during transitions
It can store negative numbers
It is faster to add than binary
items.explanation: Because neighbors differ in only one bit, moving from one value to the next never has several bits changing at slightly different times, so no false in-between codes appear. It uses the same number of bits as binary and is not about arithmetic speed.

items.prompt: A 7-segment decoder is best described as which kind of circuit?
choices: A combinational circuit that maps a 4-bit digit to 7 segment-on/off outputs
A memory that stores the displayed digit
A clocked counter
A priority encoder
items.explanation: It is pure combinational logic: 4 input bits (a digit) map through a fixed truth table to 7 outputs that turn the display's segments on or off. There is no stored state and no clock involved.

items.prompt: At its core, what is any code converter (like binary-to-Gray)?
choices: A circuit that needs a clock to step through codes
A combinational function you can write as a truth table and build from gates
A type of memory chip
An analog amplifier
items.explanation: A converter just maps each input code to a fixed output code, which is exactly a truth table turned into gates - combinational logic. No clock or memory is required.

lessons.title: Adders: Half, Full, Ripple-Carry & a Faster Idea
lessons.content:
## Hook

Adding numbers is the most basic math a computer does, and it is built from the gates you already know. Once you can add two bits, you can add numbers of any size by chaining the same little block.

## Build

Start with the smallest job: add two single bits, A and B. The result can be 0, 1, or 2, so you need two output bits - a **sum** and a **carry** (the bit you carry to the next column, just like adding decimal digits). This is a **half-adder**: the sum is $A \oplus B$ (XOR) and the carry is $A \cdot B$ (AND). It is called 'half' because it cannot accept a carry coming *in* from a lower column, so it only works for the very first column.

To add real multi-bit numbers, each column also receives a **carry-in** from the column to its right. A block that adds three bits - A, B, and carry-in - is a **full-adder**. Its outputs: $\text{Sum} = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}$, and the carry-out is 1 whenever **two or more** of the three inputs are 1 (a 'majority' rule). Worked example: A=1, B=1, Cin=0 gives Sum=0 and Cout=1 (that is 1+1 = binary 10).

Now chain them. Put one full-adder per bit position and feed each carry-out into the next adder's carry-in. That is a **ripple-carry adder** - simple and regular. The downside is in the name: the carry has to *ripple* through every stage one after another. The answer is not final until the carry has traveled all the way from the lowest bit to the highest, so a wider adder is a slower adder. This carry path is the circuit's slowest route.

Engineers speed this up with **carry-lookahead**: instead of waiting for the carry to crawl through each stage, extra logic figures out, in advance and in parallel, whether each column will produce a carry. It looks at whether a column will *generate* a carry on its own or merely *pass along* an incoming one, then computes all the carries at once. The result is much faster addition, at the cost of more gates.

One more bonus: **subtraction reuses the adder**. In two's complement, $A - B$ equals $A$ plus the bit-flip of $B$ plus 1. So the same hardware adds or subtracts, with a control line that flips B and forces an extra carry-in.

## Recap

Half-adder: two bits in, sum + carry out, no carry-in. Full-adder: three bits in (adds a carry-in), sum out, carry-out by majority. Chain full-adders for a ripple-carry adder (simple but slow because the carry ripples). Carry-lookahead computes carries in parallel for speed. Subtraction is addition with B inverted plus 1.

items.prompt: Using a full-adder, add the bits A=1, B=1, Cin=0. What are Sum and Cout?
choices: Sum=1, Cout=1
Sum=0, Cout=1
Sum=0, Cout=0
Sum=1, Cout=0
items.explanation: 1 + 1 + 0 = 2, which in binary is 10: a sum bit of 0 and a carry-out of 1. The carry is 1 because two of the three inputs are 1 (the majority rule). Forgetting the carry and reporting Sum=0, Cout=0 loses the overflow into the next column.

items.prompt: What are the sum and carry outputs of a half-adder for inputs A and B?
choices: Sum = A AND B, Carry = A XOR B
Sum = A XOR B, Carry = A AND B
Sum = A OR B, Carry = A AND B
Sum = A XOR B, Carry = A OR B
items.explanation: The sum bit is 1 when exactly one input is 1, which is XOR. The carry is 1 only when both inputs are 1, which is AND. Swapping them (AND for sum) would give the wrong result for 1+0.

items.prompt: Why can a half-adder only be used for the lowest bit position of a larger adder?
choices: It is too slow for higher bits
It has no carry-in, so it cannot accept the carry coming from a lower column
It produces no carry-out
It can only output 0
items.explanation: A half-adder adds only two bits and has no input for an incoming carry. Every column above the first must accept a carry-in from the column below, which requires a full-adder. The half-adder does produce a carry-out; it just can't take one in.

items.prompt: How many bits does a full-adder add together, and what extra input does it have compared to a half-adder?
choices: Two bits; it has an extra output
Three bits; the extra input is a carry-in from the previous column
Four bits; the extra input is a clock
Three bits; the extra input is a reset
items.explanation: A full-adder sums three bits: A, B, and a carry-in. That carry-in is the key addition over a half-adder, letting full-adders chain to handle multi-bit numbers. It has nothing to do with clocks or resets - it is combinational.

items.prompt: A full-adder's carry-out is 1 in which situation?
choices: When all three inputs are 0
When exactly one input is 1
When two or more of the three inputs are 1
Only when all three inputs are 1
items.explanation: Carry-out follows a majority rule: it is 1 whenever at least two of A, B, Cin are 1. 'Only when all three' is too strict (it misses 1+1+0), and 'exactly one' would never produce a carry.

items.prompt: How is a ripple-carry adder built?
choices: By chaining full-adders, each carry-out feeding the next adder's carry-in
By using a single half-adder for all bits
By placing all adders in parallel with no connection
By using only XOR gates
items.explanation: One full-adder per bit position, with carry-out wired to the next stage's carry-in, forms a ripple-carry adder. The stages must connect through the carry - that connection is exactly what makes the carry 'ripple'.

items.prompt: Why does a ripple-carry adder get slower as the numbers get wider?
choices: Wider numbers need more clock cycles
The carry must travel through every stage in turn before the answer is final
XOR gates slow down with more inputs
It runs out of memory
items.explanation: Each stage's correct output waits on the carry arriving from the stage below, so the carry ripples serially from the lowest bit up to the highest. More bits means a longer carry trip and more delay. It is combinational, so there are no clock cycles or memory involved.

items.prompt: What is the main idea behind a carry-lookahead adder?
choices: It removes the need for any addition
It computes the carries in parallel ahead of time instead of waiting for them to ripple
It uses a faster clock
It replaces full-adders with memory cells
items.explanation: Carry-lookahead uses extra logic to work out every column's carry at once, based on which columns generate or pass along a carry, so the answer does not wait for a slow ripple. The trade-off is more gates. There is no clock in this combinational circuit.

items.prompt: How does an adder perform subtraction A - B using two's complement?
choices: It adds A and B, then divides by two
It computes A plus the inverted bits of B plus 1
It removes the carry chain
It cannot subtract at all
items.explanation: In two's complement, negating B means inverting its bits and adding 1. So A - B = A + (NOT B) + 1, which the same adder does by flipping B and forcing a carry-in of 1. A control line lets one circuit both add and subtract.

lessons.title: The ALU, Flags & Glitches
lessons.content:
## Hook

Every time a computer adds, subtracts, or compares, the work happens in one block: the **ALU** (Arithmetic Logic Unit). It is the calculator at the heart of a CPU. This lesson assembles the pieces you have built into that calculator, and then looks at a subtle real-world wrinkle: glitches.

## Build

An **ALU** combines several operations into one unit and uses a **function-select** input to pick which one to perform: add, subtract, AND, OR, XOR, or shift. Think of it as a calculator whose buttons are chosen by the select code. Inside, an adder handles add/subtract, simple gates handle AND/OR/XOR, and a multiplexer at the output picks the chosen result. Stack one-bit slices together and you get an ALU for whole numbers.

The ALU also reports **flags** - one-bit status signals that describe the result:

- **Z (Zero):** 1 when the whole result is zero. Useful for 'are these equal?' (subtract and check Z).
- **C (Carry):** the carry out of the top bit. It signals overflow for **unsigned** numbers.
- **N (Negative):** the sign bit of the result; 1 means negative in two's complement.
- **V (Overflow):** **signed** overflow - the true answer was too big or too small to fit, so the sign came out wrong.

A classic trap: **C and V are different things.** C is about unsigned math; V is about signed math. Example: adding two positive signed numbers and getting a negative-looking result sets V (signed overflow) even though C might be 0.

Now the wrinkle. In an ideal world a circuit's output jumps cleanly to its new value. In reality, signals race through different gate paths at slightly different speeds, so an output can briefly show a wrong value before settling. This brief wrong value is a **glitch** (or **hazard**). A **static hazard** is a momentary blip - an output that should hold steady at 1 (or 0) dips the other way for an instant. A **dynamic hazard** is when an output that should change just once flickers several times before settling.

Glitches are usually harmless: if the output feeds a clocked circuit that only looks at the value once it has settled, the blip is ignored. They become dangerous when they feed sensitive lines like a clock or a reset, where a single false pulse can cause real misbehavior. A common cure is to add a small bit of 'redundant' logic that keeps the output covered during the risky transition.

## Recap

An ALU is a select-driven calculator (add/sub/AND/OR/XOR/shift) built from the blocks you already know, plus a result-selecting mux. It reports flags: Z (zero), C (unsigned carry), N (negative sign), V (signed overflow) - and C is not the same as V. Glitches are brief wrong outputs from unequal path delays; harmless into clocked logic, risky on clocks or resets.

items.prompt: What is the job of the function-select input on an ALU?
choices: It sets the clock speed
It chooses which operation (add, subtract, AND, OR, etc.) the ALU performs
It selects which flag to output
It picks the supply voltage
items.explanation: The function-select code tells the ALU which operation to apply to its inputs, like choosing a button on a calculator. An output mux then routes the chosen operation's result out. It does not set clocks, voltages, or which flag appears.

items.prompt: What is an ALU?
choices: A memory chip that stores results
A combinational block that performs arithmetic and logic operations chosen by a select input
A clock generator
A single AND gate
items.explanation: An ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) bundles add, subtract, AND, OR, XOR, shift, and more, with a select input choosing the operation - the calculator at the core of a CPU. It computes; it does not store data like memory does.

items.prompt: Inside an ALU, what role does a multiplexer typically play?
choices: It generates the clock
It selects which operation's result becomes the ALU's output, based on the function-select code
It stores the flags
It inverts every input
items.explanation: The ALU computes several operations and a mux, driven by the function-select code, picks the one to send out. This reuses the selectable-switch idea from the mux lesson. The mux does not make clocks or store anything.

items.prompt: When is the Zero (Z) flag set to 1?
choices: When the result's sign bit is 1
When every bit of the result is 0
When there is a carry out of the top bit
When the inputs are equal in sign
items.explanation: Z is 1 only when the entire result equals zero (all result bits are 0). The sign bit being 1 is the N flag, and a carry out of the top bit is the C flag - different signals.

items.prompt: What does the Negative (N) flag report?
choices: Whether the result is odd
The sign bit of the result (1 means negative in two's complement)
Whether a carry occurred
Whether the result is zero
items.explanation: N simply copies the most significant bit of the result, which is the sign bit in two's complement: 1 means negative. It says nothing about oddness, carries, or zero.

items.prompt: What is the key difference between the Carry (C) flag and the Overflow (V) flag?
choices: C is for signed math; V is for unsigned math
C signals overflow for unsigned numbers; V signals overflow for signed numbers
They are two names for the same flag
C is for addition only; V is for subtraction only
items.explanation: C (the carry out of the top bit) indicates unsigned overflow, while V indicates signed (two's complement) overflow. They are independent and a classic source of bugs when confused - use C for unsigned, V for signed.

items.prompt: Adding two POSITIVE signed numbers gives a result whose sign bit is 1 (looks negative). Which flag does this set?
choices: The Zero flag
The Overflow (V) flag, because the signed result is wrong
No flag - this is normal
Only the Carry flag, never V
items.explanation: Two positives can never legitimately sum to a negative, so the answer overflowed the signed range: V is set. This is exactly the signed-overflow case that C alone would not flag, illustrating why C and V differ.

items.prompt: What causes a combinational glitch (hazard)?
choices: A missing clock signal
Signals arriving through different gate paths at slightly different times, so the output briefly shows a wrong value
The power supply being too high
Using too few inputs
items.explanation: When one input change reaches the output along paths of unequal delay, the output can momentarily settle on a wrong value before correcting itself - that brief wrong value is the glitch. The steady-state logic is still correct; the issue is timing of the paths.

items.prompt: When are glitches genuinely dangerous rather than harmless?
choices: Always - every glitch corrupts the result
Never - they can be ignored entirely
When they feed sensitive lines like a clock or a reset, where a false pulse causes a real event
Only when the numbers are unsigned
items.explanation: A glitch feeding a clocked circuit is sampled only after it settles, so it is harmless. But on a clock, reset, or similar trigger line, a momentary false pulse can fire a real, unwanted event - that is when hazards must be designed out.

lessons.title: Why Simplify, and What a K-Map Is
lessons.content:
Imagine you have a logic circuit built from gates (the tiny parts that compute AND, OR, and NOT). Often the first version you write is bigger than it needs to be. **Minimizing** means finding a simpler way to compute the *exact same* output using **fewer and smaller gates**. Fewer gates means a circuit that is cheaper to build, faster to react, and uses less power. So simplifying logic is not just tidiness, it saves real money and energy.

You already know about **truth tables** (a list of every input combination and the output it should give) and **Boolean algebra** (the rules for AND, OR, NOT). You *can* simplify by juggling algebra rules, but that is fiddly and easy to get wrong.

A **Karnaugh map** (K-map for short, named after Maurice Karnaugh) is a smarter trick: it is just a truth table re-drawn as a **grid**. The grid is arranged so that simplifying becomes **visual**. You look for groups of adjacent 1s, draw a loop around each group, and each loop turns into one simpler term. It is a bit like spotting a pattern by grouping similar things together on a board.

Recap: a K-map is a truth table laid out as a grid so you can simplify logic by *eye* instead of by algebra. The payoff is smaller, cheaper, faster circuits. The next lessons show how the grid is laid out and how the grouping works.

items.prompt: What does it mean to 'minimize' a logic circuit?
choices: Find a simpler circuit that produces the exact same output using fewer and smaller gates
Make the circuit produce a different, easier output
Shrink the physical size of each individual transistor
Reduce the number of inputs the circuit is allowed to have
items.explanation: Minimizing keeps the behaviour (the truth table) identical while using fewer/smaller gates. It does not change what the circuit computes, and it is not about transistor size or removing inputs.

items.prompt: Why is using fewer gates a good thing?
choices: Fewer gates generally means a cheaper, faster, lower-power circuit
Fewer gates make the circuit look more complicated
Fewer gates always change the output for the better
Fewer gates require a larger power supply
items.explanation: Each gate costs money, adds delay, and draws power. Trimming gates saves all three. The output stays the same; that is the whole point of minimizing.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is a Karnaugh map?
choices: A truth table re-drawn as a grid so that logic can be simplified visually
A list of every gate available in a chip
A graph of voltage over time
A circuit that automatically rewires itself
items.explanation: A K-map holds exactly the same information as a truth table, just arranged as a grid. The grid layout is what makes simplification something you can see by eye.

items.prompt: On a K-map, how do you actually do the simplifying?
choices: Circle groups of adjacent 1s; each group becomes one simpler term
Erase every 1 and keep only the 0s
Add up the decimal values of all the cells
Rotate the grid until the 1s line up diagonally
items.explanation: Grouping adjacent 1s and reading each loop as a single term is the core K-map technique. The remaining lessons build up the rules for doing this correctly.

items.prompt: What background does a K-map assume you already have?
choices: Truth tables and basic Boolean algebra (AND, OR, NOT)
Calculus and differential equations
How to program a microcontroller in C
How to solder a circuit board
items.explanation: A K-map is just a re-arranged truth table simplified with Boolean ideas, so truth tables and AND/OR/NOT are the only prerequisites. No higher math or hardware skills are needed.

items.prompt: Compared with simplifying by Boolean algebra alone, the K-map's main advantage is that it is:
choices: Visual, so it is easier and less error-prone for small problems
The only method that gives a correct answer
Able to handle thousands of variables by hand
Faster only because it ignores some of the inputs
items.explanation: Algebra works but is fiddly; the K-map turns the same job into spotting visual groups, which is easier to get right for small circuits. It does not ignore inputs, and it does not scale to huge problems (later lessons cover that limit).

items.prompt: A K-map and the matching truth table contain:
choices: The same information, just arranged differently
Completely different information
Only the inputs, never the outputs
Only the rows where the output is 0
items.explanation: The K-map is simply a re-layout of the truth table; no information is added or removed. Both list the output for every input combination.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best captures what K-map grouping feels like?
choices: Spotting a pattern by grouping similar items together on a grid
Sorting a list of numbers from smallest to largest
Measuring the length of a wire with a ruler
Counting how many times a light blinks
items.explanation: Grouping adjacent 1s on a grid is a pattern-spotting activity, much like clustering similar things on a board. That intuition is exactly what the K-map turns into a method.

lessons.title: The Grid Layout: Gray-Code Adjacency
lessons.content:
Here is the clever part of a K-map: the cells are arranged so that any two cells **right next to each other differ in exactly one input variable**. That single-variable difference is what lets two cells merge into one simpler term, so the whole grid is built to make 'next to each other' mean 'almost identical'.

To get that, we do **not** label the axes in plain counting order ($00, 01, 10, 11$). Notice that going from $01$ to $10$ flips *two* bits at once. Instead we use **Gray code**: $00, 01, 11, 10$. Read across that sequence and only **one bit changes at each step** (00 to 01 flips the last bit, 01 to 11 flips the first, 11 to 10 flips the last). Gray code is just an ordering where neighbours differ by a single bit.

The smallest map has 2 variables and is a **2 x 2 grid (4 cells)**. A 3-variable map is a **2 x 4 grid (8 cells)**: one variable picks the row, and two Gray-coded variables pick the column. In general an $n$-variable map has $2^n$ cells, one per row of the truth table. Each cell holds the output (a 0 or a 1) for that input combination.

One more rule that surprises people: the edges **wrap around**. The leftmost column is treated as next to the rightmost column, and the top row as next to the bottom row, because in Gray code those also differ by one bit.

Recap: Gray-code labels make neighbours differ by one variable, and the edges wrap. Get this layout solid; every later step rides on it.

items.prompt: What is special about two cells that sit right next to each other on a K-map?
choices: They differ in exactly one input variable
They always hold the same output value
Their decimal minterm numbers are always consecutive
They differ in exactly two input variables
items.explanation: The grid is built so adjacency means a one-variable difference. That single-bit change is precisely what lets two neighbouring cells combine into a simpler term.

items.prompt: Which sequence is Gray code, used to label a 4-position K-map axis?
items.explanation: Gray code is 00, 01, 11, 10: each step flips just one bit, including the wrap from 10 back to 00. Plain binary (00,01,10,11) flips two bits between 01 and 10, which would break adjacency.

items.prompt: Why are the axes NOT labelled in plain binary counting order (00, 01, 10, 11)?
choices: Because the 01-to-10 step flips two bits, so neighbours would differ in two variables and could not be merged
Because plain binary cannot represent the number 2
Because it would need twice as many cells
Because the output values would change
items.explanation: Going 01 to 10 changes both bits at once. If neighbours differed in two variables, looping them would not match the rule that lets cells merge. Gray code keeps every neighbour one bit apart.

items.prompt: How many cells does an n-variable K-map have, and what does each cell hold?
choices: 2^n cells, each holding the output for one input combination
n cells, each holding one input variable
2n cells, each holding a gate
n^2 cells, each holding a Gray-code label
items.explanation: There is one cell per truth-table row, and a truth table for n variables has 2^n rows. Each cell stores that row's output (a 0 or 1).

items.prompt: What shape is a standard 2-variable K-map?
choices: A 2 x 2 grid (4 cells)
A 2 x 4 grid (8 cells)
A 4 x 4 grid (16 cells)
A single row of 4 cells
items.explanation: Two variables give 2^2 = 4 minterms, drawn as a 2-by-2 grid. The 2x4 (8 cells) is the 3-variable map and 4x4 (16 cells) is the 4-variable map.

items.prompt: A 3-variable K-map is usually drawn as which grid?
choices: A 2 x 4 grid (8 cells): one variable on the row, two Gray-coded on the columns
A 3 x 3 grid (9 cells)
A 4 x 4 grid (16 cells)
A 1 x 8 single row
items.explanation: Three variables give 2^3 = 8 cells. The usual layout puts one variable on two rows and two Gray-coded variables across four columns.

items.prompt: What does 'wrap-around' adjacency mean on a K-map?
choices: The left edge is treated as next to the right edge, and the top as next to the bottom
You may only group cells that touch in the middle of the grid
The grid must be folded into a physical tube before use
Corner cells can never be grouped with anything
items.explanation: In Gray code the first and last labels also differ by one bit, so the outer edges are neighbours. The leftmost and rightmost columns wrap together, as do the top and bottom rows.

items.prompt: You fill a K-map from a function written as a list of minterms (the input combinations where the output is 1). Where do the 1s go?
choices: In the cells whose minterm numbers appear in that list; all other cells get 0
In every cell, then you erase the wrong ones at random
Only in the four corner cells
In the cells whose numbers are even
items.explanation: A minterm list names exactly the input combinations that give output 1. Place a 1 in each listed cell and 0 everywhere else; that is the K-map of the function.

items.prompt: On a 3-variable map with columns labelled 00, 01, 11, 10, which two columns are neighbours by wrap-around?
choices: The 00 column and the 10 column (the far-left and far-right)
The 01 column and the 11 column
No columns wrap on a 3-variable map
Every column is a neighbour of every other column
items.explanation: 00 and 10 differ in just one bit, so the far-left and far-right columns are adjacent even though they sit at opposite ends of the grid. That is wrap-around adjacency.

lessons.title: Grouping Rules and 4-Variable Maps
lessons.content:
Now we put the layout to work. The 4-variable map is a **4 x 4 grid (16 cells)**, with two Gray-coded variables on the rows and two on the columns. It is big enough to show every grouping rule.

To simplify, you cover all the 1s with loops, following these rules:

- A loop (a **group**) must contain only 1s.
- A group's size must be a **power of two**: 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16 cells. Never 3, 5, 6, and so on.
- A group must be a rectangle (a straight block), and it may **wrap around** the edges.
- **Make every group as big as legally possible**, and use as few groups as you can.

Why do bigger groups win? Each time a group **doubles** in size, it removes one more variable from that term. Concretely, in an $n$-variable map a group of $2^k$ cells eliminates $k$ variables, leaving a product term with $n-k$ letters. A group of 1 keeps all $n$ letters; a group of 16 covers the whole map and the term disappears entirely (the output is always 1). So a bigger loop equals a shorter, cheaper term. That is why the guiding habit is **largest group first**.

A single isolated 1 (no neighbouring 1s) still must be covered, as a group of size 1.

Recap: cover all the 1s with the fewest, largest power-of-two rectangles, wrapping allowed. Bigger groups drop more variables, giving simpler terms.

items.prompt: Which group size is NOT allowed on a K-map?
choices: A group of 3 cells
A group of 2 cells
A group of 4 cells
A group of 8 cells
items.explanation: Group sizes must be powers of two: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16. Three is not a power of two, so a group of 3 is illegal even if three 1s sit in a row.

items.prompt: On a 4-variable map, a legal group of 4 cells leaves a product term with how many letters?
choices: 2 letters
4 letters
1 letter
0 letters
items.explanation: A group of 4 = 2^2 cells eliminates k=2 variables. In a 4-variable map that leaves 4 - 2 = 2 letters in the term.

items.prompt: Why do we always try to make each group as large as possible?
choices: A larger group removes more variables, giving a shorter and cheaper term
A larger group is easier to draw neatly
A larger group changes the output to all 1s
Smaller groups are illegal in every K-map
items.explanation: Each doubling of a group drops one more variable. Bigger loops therefore yield terms with fewer letters, which means fewer/simpler gates. That is the reason for the 'largest group first' habit.

items.prompt: A group of 8 cells in a 4-variable map leaves a term with how many letters?
choices: 1 letter
3 letters
0 letters
8 letters
items.explanation: Eight cells = 2^3, so k = 3 variables are eliminated. In a 4-variable map that leaves 4 - 3 = 1 letter. Bigger group, shorter term.

items.prompt: A lone 1 sits with 0s on all four sides. What must you do with it?
choices: Cover it as a group of size 1, which keeps all four variables in that term
Ignore it to save gates
Erase it because it cannot be grouped
Merge it diagonally with the nearest 1
items.explanation: Every 1 must be covered. With no neighbouring 1s the largest legal group is just itself (size 1), which removes zero variables and keeps the full 4-letter term. Diagonal grouping is not allowed.

items.prompt: Can a group legally wrap around from the right edge of the map to the left edge?
choices: Yes, because the left and right edges are adjacent in Gray code
No, groups must stay inside the visible borders
Only on a 2-variable map
Only if the group has exactly 3 cells
items.explanation: Wrap-around adjacency means the outer edges are neighbours, so a group may span from one edge across to the opposite edge. The four corners of a 4-variable map, for instance, can form one group of 4.

items.prompt: Two 1s sit side by side. The group of 2 covering them eliminates how many variables?
choices: 1 variable
2 variables
0 variables
4 variables
items.explanation: A group of 2 = 2^1 cells eliminates k = 1 variable, because the two cells differ in exactly that one variable. The variable that changes across the pair drops out of the term.

items.prompt: A group covers all 16 cells of a 4-variable map. What does the term become?
choices: It vanishes; the output is simply 1 for every input
It keeps all four variables
It becomes a single-letter term
It is illegal because 16 is too big
items.explanation: A group of 16 = 2^4 eliminates all 4 variables (4 - 4 = 0 letters). The function is constantly 1. Sixteen is a power of two, so the group is perfectly legal.

items.prompt: Which is the best grouping strategy?
choices: Cover every 1 using the fewest groups, each as large as legally possible
Use as many tiny groups as you can
Cover only half the 1s and leave the rest
Cover the 0s and ignore the 1s
items.explanation: Fewest groups means fewer OR-ed terms; largest groups means fewer letters per term. Both shrink the circuit. Every 1 must be covered, and for a sum-of-products result you group the 1s, not the 0s.

lessons.title: Reading Off the Simplified Expression
lessons.content:
Once your 1s are grouped, you turn the picture back into a formula. The result is in **sum-of-products (SOP)** form: each group becomes one **product term** (variables ANDed together), and then you OR all the product terms together. 'Sum' is the OR, 'product' is the AND.

To read a single group, ask: **which variables stay constant across every cell in the loop?**

- A variable that is **1** for the whole group appears as itself (for example $A$).
- A variable that is **0** for the whole group appears **complemented**, written $\\overline{A}$ (read 'not A' or 'A-bar').
- A variable that **changes** within the group has been eliminated, so you drop it.

Example: suppose a group of four cells all share $C=1$ and $D=1$, while $A$ and $B$ each take both values. Then $C$ and $D$ stay constant (so they appear) and $A,B$ vary (so they drop). The term is $C\\cdot D$. If another group of two all share $A=1$, $B=0$, $D=0$, that term is $A\\cdot\\overline{B}\\cdot\\overline{D}$. The whole simplified function is the OR of the groups:
$$F = C\\cdot D + A\\cdot\\overline{B}\\cdot\\overline{D}.$$

Recap: each loop gives one AND term (constant-1 variables plain, constant-0 variables barred, changing variables dropped), and you OR the loops together. That OR-of-ANDs is the minimized SOP expression.

items.prompt: In a sum-of-products (SOP) expression, what do 'sum' and 'product' mean?
choices: Sum = OR of the terms; product = AND of the variables inside a term
Sum = ordinary addition; product = ordinary multiplication
Sum = AND; product = OR
Sum = NOT; product = XOR
items.explanation: In Boolean form 'product' is AND (variables ANDed within a term) and 'sum' is OR (terms ORed together). So SOP is an OR of AND-terms.

items.prompt: To read a group, you check each variable. A variable that stays 1 across the whole group appears in the term as:
choices: Itself, for example A
Its complement, A-bar
Dropped entirely
A constant 0
items.explanation: A variable held at 1 throughout the group appears uncomplemented (just A). A variable held at 0 would appear barred; a variable that changes is dropped.

items.prompt: A variable that stays 0 across the whole group appears as:
choices: Its complement, written A-bar
Itself, A
A 1
It is removed from the term
items.explanation: Held at 0 means the term is true when that variable is 0, i.e. when its complement is 1, so you write the barred form A-bar. Only variables that CHANGE get removed.

items.prompt: A variable that changes value within the group (1 in some cells, 0 in others) is:
choices: Dropped from that term
Written as itself
Written as its complement
Written twice
items.explanation: A variable that is not constant cannot help define the group, so it is eliminated. This is exactly why bigger groups (more varying variables) give shorter terms.

items.prompt: A group of 4 cells all have C = 1 and D = 1, while A and B take every combination. Circle the right group: what is its term?
choices: C AND D
A AND B
C AND D, both complemented
A AND B AND C AND D
items.explanation: C and D stay constant at 1, so they appear plain; A and B vary, so they drop. The simplified term is C AND D. Because two variables were eliminated, the group must have been size 4.

items.prompt: A group of 2 cells all have A = 1, B = 0, and D = 0, while C changes. What is the simplified term?
choices: A AND B-bar AND D-bar
A AND B AND D
A AND C
A-bar AND B AND D
items.explanation: A stays 1 (plain A); B stays 0 (B-bar); D stays 0 (D-bar); C varies and drops. The term is A AND B-bar AND D-bar. Three letters remain, matching a group of 2 in a 4-variable map.

items.prompt: You found two groups giving terms C AND D and A AND B-bar AND D-bar. How do you combine them into the final expression?
choices: OR them together: F = (C AND D) OR (A AND B-bar AND D-bar)
AND them together
Subtract the smaller term from the larger
Keep only the term with fewer letters
items.explanation: In SOP form every group is one product term and they are joined with OR. The full minimized function is the OR of all the group terms.

items.prompt: A group of two cells differs only in variable B (B is 1 in one cell, 0 in the other); both cells have A = 0. What term results?
choices: A-bar (just the single letter A-bar)
A-bar AND B
A-bar AND B-bar
B
items.explanation: A stays 0, giving A-bar; B changes, so it drops. With only those two variables involved the whole term collapses to A-bar. The single varying variable was eliminated.

items.prompt: If a group's term comes out with zero letters (everything varied), what does that tell you?
choices: The group covers the entire map, so the function is always 1
You made an error and should erase the group
The function is always 0
You must add the missing variables back in
items.explanation: Every variable varying means the group spans all 2^n cells, eliminating all variables. The output is constantly 1. (Grouping 1s can never produce a constant 0 result.)

lessons.title: Prime Implicants, Don't-Cares and Beyond
lessons.content:
A few ideas polish the method. First, vocabulary for picking groups well. A **prime implicant** is a group that is already as large as it can legally be (no bigger legal loop contains it). An **essential prime implicant** is a prime implicant that is the *only* group able to cover some particular 1. Because nothing else can cover that 1, an essential prime implicant **must** appear in the final answer. The practical recipe: pick all the essential prime implicants first, then add the fewest extra prime implicants needed to cover any leftover 1s.

Second, **don't-care cells** (written $X$). These are input combinations that can never happen, or whose output truly does not matter (for example the unused codes $1010$ to $1111$ in a 4-bit decimal digit). An $X$ is a wildcard: treat it as a $1$ **only when doing so makes a group bigger**, and as a $0$ otherwise. Used well, don't-cares often shrink the result noticeably. Never build a group whose only purpose is to cover an $X$; they are optional, never required.

Third, the limits and the tools. K-maps get unwieldy past 4 or 5 variables. The **Quine-McCluskey** method is a step-by-step table that finds the same prime implicants without a picture, which is what software can automate. Real design tools use fast minimizers (such as Espresso) on huge functions.

Finally, a hazard idea: a minimized circuit can briefly **glitch** when an input changes between two groups that merely touch. Adding one extra **redundant term** that bridges the two groups smooths the transition. It costs a gate but removes the glitch.

Recap: choose essential prime implicants first, exploit don't-cares as free wildcards, lean on Quine-McCluskey/software past a handful of variables, and add a redundant bridging term when a glitch must be prevented.

items.prompt: What is a prime implicant?
choices: A group that is already as large as it can legally be
Any single cell containing a 1
A group made only of 0s
A group of exactly three cells
items.explanation: A prime implicant is a maximal legal group: no larger legal loop contains it. Making groups as large as possible is exactly the search for prime implicants.

items.prompt: What makes a prime implicant 'essential'?
choices: It is the only group that can cover some particular 1
It is the smallest group on the map
It covers only don't-care cells
It contains the most 0s
items.explanation: If a certain 1 can be covered by just one prime implicant, that prime implicant is essential and must be in the final answer, because nothing else reaches that 1.

items.prompt: What is the recommended order for building the minimal answer?
choices: Pick all essential prime implicants first, then add the fewest extra prime implicants to cover leftover 1s
Pick the smallest groups first
Use every prime implicant on the map
Cover the don't-cares before anything else
items.explanation: Essentials are forced, so take them first; then cover whatever 1s remain with as few additional prime implicants as possible. Using all prime implicants would be wasteful and not minimal.

items.prompt: How should a don't-care (X) cell be treated when grouping?
choices: As a 1 only when it lets you form a bigger group; otherwise as a 0
Always as a 1, no matter what
Always as a 0, no matter what
Removed from the grid entirely
items.explanation: A don't-care is a wildcard. Count it as 1 when it enlarges a useful group (removing more variables); leave it as 0 otherwise. Forcing all to 1 can create needless terms.

items.prompt: Should you ever make a group whose only job is to cover a don't-care cell?
choices: No, don't-cares are optional and never need covering on their own
Yes, every don't-care must end up inside some group
Yes, but only on a 2-variable map
Only if the don't-care is in a corner
items.explanation: Don't-cares help only when they enlarge a group that is covering real 1s. A group existing solely to cover an X buys nothing and just adds a term, so it is never required.

items.prompt: Why do K-maps stop being practical past about 4 or 5 variables?
choices: The grid and its adjacencies get too large and error-prone to track by eye
Gray code only works for 4 variables
Boolean algebra forbids more than 5 variables
The output values become undefined
items.explanation: More variables mean exponentially more cells and harder-to-see adjacencies (a 5-variable map is two stacked 4-variable maps). Beyond that, people switch to tabular or automated methods.

items.prompt: What is the Quine-McCluskey method, in one line?
choices: A step-by-step table that finds the same prime implicants without drawing a map, easy for software to automate
A faster way to draw the K-map grid by hand
A method that only works for 2-variable functions
A way to add more variables to a truth table
items.explanation: Quine-McCluskey reaches the same prime implicants as a K-map using a systematic table instead of a picture, which is why it scales and why software tools use that style of approach.

items.prompt: A minimized circuit briefly glitches when an input change moves between two groups that only touch. The usual fix is to:
choices: Add one redundant term that bridges the two groups, smoothing the transition
Delete one of the two groups
Slow the whole circuit down permanently
Re-label the axes in plain binary
items.explanation: An extra (logically redundant) term covering the overlap keeps the output steady during the change, removing the glitch. It costs one gate but does not alter the truth table.

items.prompt: Does adding that redundant bridging term change what the circuit computes?
choices: No, the steady output is identical; it only affects timing during a transition
Yes, it flips several outputs from 1 to 0
Yes, it converts the circuit to product-of-sums
Yes, it removes an essential prime implicant
items.explanation: The redundant term is already implied by the others, so the final truth table is unchanged. Its only effect is to keep coverage continuous during an input change, killing the glitch.

items.prompt: A particular 1 can be covered by three different prime implicants. Is that 1's coverage forcing any essential prime implicant?
choices: No, because more than one prime implicant covers it, none of them is forced by that 1 alone
Yes, all three become essential
Yes, the largest of the three is essential
Yes, the smallest of the three is essential
items.explanation: An essential prime implicant is the ONLY one covering some 1. If three can cover this 1, you have a choice, so this 1 does not make any of them essential. Essentials come from 1s covered by exactly one prime implicant.

lessons.title: Memory from Feedback: The SR Latch
lessons.content:
Think of a light with two buttons: one marked ON, one marked OFF. Press ON and the light stays on, even after you let go. Press OFF and it stays off. The light *remembers* the last button you pressed. That memory is the whole idea behind this sub-topic.

So far you have studied **combinational logic** (the AND, OR, and other gates from earlier sub-topics): its output depends only on the inputs *right now*. Take the inputs away and it instantly forgets. **Sequential logic** is different: its output depends on what happened in the *past*. It has memory.

The trick that creates memory is **feedback** — wiring a circuit's output back into its own input so it can hold onto a value. The simplest memory circuit built this way is the **SR latch** (SR stands for **Set–Reset**). It is two simple gates wired so each one feeds the other, forming a loop that locks in a value.

It has two inputs and behaves just like our light:

- **S (Set) = 1** → forces the output **Q = 1** (turn it on).
- **R (Reset) = 1** → forces the output **Q = 0** (turn it off).
- **S = 0, R = 0** → **Hold**: the latch keeps whatever it had. *This holding is the memory.*

There is one combination to avoid. Pressing both buttons at once — **S = 1 and R = 1** — is the **forbidden state**: it tells the latch to turn on and off at the same time, which makes no sense and leaves the output unpredictable. Good designs simply never do this.

**Recap:** combinational logic forgets instantly; sequential logic remembers. Feedback is what gives it memory, and the SR latch is the first, simplest example — set it, reset it, or leave it alone to hold.

items.prompt: What is the key difference between combinational and sequential logic?
choices: Combinational logic is faster than sequential logic
Sequential logic has memory — its output depends on past inputs, not just present ones
Sequential logic can only use NAND gates
Combinational logic needs a clock and sequential logic does not
items.explanation: Combinational logic forgets instantly: its output depends only on the inputs right now. Sequential logic remembers — its output depends on its history. Speed and gate choice are not the defining difference.

items.prompt: What single trick lets a circuit store a value (have memory)?
choices: Feedback — wiring an output back to an input so the circuit holds its state
Using more inputs than outputs
Adding a resistor to slow the signal down
Connecting two outputs together
items.explanation: Feedback creates the memory loop: when an output is routed back to an input, the circuit can hold onto a value instead of forgetting it instantly. That is the leap into sequential logic.

items.prompt: In an SR latch, what does asserting S (Set) = 1 do?
choices: Forces the output Q to 0
Forces the output Q to 1
Makes the latch forget everything
Has no effect unless R is also 1
items.explanation: Set means 'turn it on': S=1 drives Q=1. Think of the ON button on the remembering light. R=1 is the OFF button that drives Q=0.

items.prompt: An SR latch currently holds Q = 1. You set S = 0 and R = 0. What is Q now?
choices: It drops to 0
It stays at 1 (hold)
It oscillates back and forth
It becomes unknown
items.explanation: S=0, R=0 is the Hold condition: the latch keeps whatever value it had. Holding the last value is exactly what makes the latch a memory cell.

items.prompt: Which input combination is the 'forbidden state' of an SR latch?
items.explanation: S=1 and R=1 together tell the latch to set and reset at the same time — a contradiction. The output becomes unpredictable, so good designs never apply this combination.

items.prompt: Why is the S = R = 1 combination a problem rather than just 'another setting'?
choices: It physically burns out the gates
It asks the latch to be both ON and OFF at once, so the result is unpredictable
It always forces Q to 1
It turns the latch into a combinational gate
items.explanation: Set says 'go to 1' and Reset says 'go to 0' — demanding both at once is a contradiction. The latch cannot do both, and the settled value can't be relied on. It does not damage the hardware.

items.prompt: The everyday 'light with separate ON and OFF buttons that stays put' is a good picture of which circuit?
choices: An AND gate
An SR latch
A plain wire
A resistor
items.explanation: ON = Set, OFF = Reset, and 'no button pressed' = Hold. The light remembering its last state is exactly how an SR latch stores one bit. An AND gate has no memory.

items.prompt: How many bits of information can one SR latch store?
choices: Zero — it has no memory
One bit (a single 0 or 1)
Eight bits (one byte)
As many as you have inputs
items.explanation: An SR latch holds a single value on its output Q — either 0 or 1. That is one bit. To store more bits, you use several latches (or flip-flops) together.

lessons.title: The Clock: D Latches vs D Flip-Flops
lessons.content:
The plain SR latch reacts the instant its inputs change. In a real system with thousands of memory cells, we want them all to update *together*, in step, so nothing gets out of sync. The solution is a **clock**: a signal that ticks steadily between 0 and 1, like a heartbeat or a metronome keeping the band in time. Every memory cell listens to the same clock and acts on its beat.

First, a tidier memory cell. A **D latch** ('D' for **Data**) has just one data input, D, plus an **enable** input. There is no forbidden state to worry about. Its rule is simple:

- While **enable = 1**, the output **Q follows D** continuously — whatever D does, Q copies. We say the latch is **transparent**, as if you could see straight through it.
- When **enable = 0**, Q **holds** the last value D had.

Because the D latch reacts to the *level* of the enable (the whole time it is high), we call it **level-sensitive**.

Now the most important device in this sub-topic: the **D flip-flop**. It also copies D to Q, but only at one precise moment — the instant the clock *rises* from 0 to 1, called the **rising edge**. It samples D right then, copies it to Q, and ignores D for the entire rest of the cycle. Because it reacts to the *edge* (the moment of change), we call it **edge-triggered**.

This is the single most important distinction in the whole topic:

- A **latch is level-sensitive**: transparent the whole time it is enabled.
- A **flip-flop is edge-triggered**: it samples once, on the clock edge.

The timing diagram makes it visible: the latch output wiggles along with D while enabled, but the flip-flop output only steps at each rising edge.

There is also a way to force a flip-flop's value: a **set** (force to 1) or **reset/clear** (force to 0) input. If it acts the instant it is asserted, ignoring the clock, it is **asynchronous**. If it waits for the next clock edge like everything else, it is **synchronous**. Synchronous is the well-behaved default; asynchronous reset is mainly for getting everything to a known state at power-on.

**Recap:** the clock is the shared heartbeat. A D latch is transparent while enabled (level); a D flip-flop captures D once per clock edge (edge). Learn that one difference cold.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is the difference between a latch and a flip-flop?
choices: A latch stores two bits; a flip-flop stores one
