A latch is level-sensitive (transparent while enabled); a flip-flop is edge-triggered (samples once per clock edge)
A latch needs a clock; a flip-flop does not
A flip-flop is always slower than a latch
items.explanation: This is THE distinction to memorize. A latch follows its input the whole time it is enabled (a level). A flip-flop captures its input only at the clock edge (a moment). Both store one bit.

items.prompt: What role does the clock play in a sequential system?
choices: It supplies power to the gates
It is a steady ticking signal that keeps every memory cell updating in step (a shared heartbeat)
It stores the data
It converts analog signals to digital
items.explanation: The clock is a regular tick between 0 and 1 — like a metronome — that all the flip-flops listen to so they update together. It carries timing, not power or data.

items.prompt: A D latch has its enable held at 1 while D toggles several times. What does Q do?
choices: Q ignores D until the next clock edge
Q follows D continuously (the latch is transparent)
Q captures D just once and freezes
Q goes to an unknown state
items.explanation: A latch is level-sensitive: while enabled it is transparent, so Q copies D in real time. Capturing once and freezing is flip-flop behavior, not latch behavior.

items.prompt: A positive-edge-triggered D flip-flop samples its input D at which moment?
choices: Continuously while the clock is high
At the instant the clock rises from 0 to 1 (the rising edge)
Whenever D changes
Once when power is applied
items.explanation: A positive-edge flip-flop captures D exactly at the rising edge and then holds it for the rest of the cycle. Following D the whole time the clock is high would be latch (level-sensitive) behavior.

items.prompt: On a timing diagram, the output Q changes value ONLY at the instants the clock rises. What kind of device is this?
choices: A transparent (level-sensitive) latch
A positive-edge-triggered flip-flop
A plain AND gate
A resistor
items.explanation: Changes confined to the rising edges are the signature of an edge-triggered flip-flop. A latch would show Q wiggling along with D for the whole time it is enabled.

items.prompt: What does 'transparent' mean for a D latch?
choices: It is physically see-through
While enabled, its output simply passes the input through in real time
It works without any input
It only updates at a clock edge
items.explanation: Transparent means you can 'see through' it: while the enable is high, Q just follows D. When the enable drops, the latch closes and holds the last value.

items.prompt: What is the difference between a synchronous reset and an asynchronous reset on a flip-flop?
choices: A synchronous reset acts only at the next clock edge; an asynchronous reset acts immediately, ignoring the clock
A synchronous reset is slower to wire up
An asynchronous reset only works while the clock is high
There is no real difference
items.explanation: Synchronous reset waits for the clock edge, staying in step with everything else. Asynchronous reset fires the moment it is asserted, regardless of the clock — handy for power-on, riskier mid-operation.

items.prompt: A D flip-flop has D = 1 just before a rising clock edge, then D drops to 0 right after the edge. What is Q after the edge?
choices: 0, because D ended at 0
1, because it captured D = 1 at the edge and holds it
Unknown
It keeps toggling
items.explanation: The flip-flop samples D at the rising edge (D=1 there) and holds that value until the next edge. Changes to D after the edge are ignored for the rest of the cycle.

lessons.title: More Flip-Flops: JK and T
lessons.content:
The D flip-flop is the everyday workhorse, but two close relatives are worth meeting because they show up inside counters.

First, the **JK flip-flop**. It is like the SR latch (J acts like Set, K acts like Reset), but cleverly *fixes the forbidden state*. Recall that S = R = 1 was illegal. With a JK flip-flop, J = K = 1 is perfectly legal and does something useful: it **toggles** — the output flips to the opposite of whatever it was (0 becomes 1, 1 becomes 0) on each clock edge. So a JK flip-flop has four modes on each clock tick:

- J = 0, K = 0 → **hold** (no change)
- J = 1, K = 0 → **set** Q to 1
- J = 0, K = 1 → **reset** Q to 0
- J = 1, K = 1 → **toggle** (flip Q)

Second, the **T flip-flop** ('T' for **Toggle**). It has a single input T:

- **T = 0** → hold (keep the current value)
- **T = 1** → toggle on each clock edge

A T flip-flop is just a JK with J and K tied together. Here is why it matters: when T is held at 1, the output flips on every clock edge, so it changes half as often as the clock. In other words, one T flip-flop **divides the clock frequency by two**. Chain several together and each halves the rate again — which, as the next lessons show, is exactly how you build a counter.

**Recap:** the JK flip-flop is an SR with the illegal corner turned into a useful 'toggle'. The T flip-flop toggles when T = 1, dividing the clock by two — the seed of counting.

items.prompt: What does a JK flip-flop do when J = 1 and K = 1?
choices: Nothing happens (it is forbidden)
It toggles — the output flips to the opposite value on each clock edge
It holds its current value
It always sets Q to 1
items.explanation: The JK flip-flop turns SR's old illegal corner into something useful: J=K=1 toggles. There is no forbidden state. Holding is J=K=0; forcing to 1 is J=1, K=0.

items.prompt: How does the JK flip-flop improve on the SR latch?
choices: It is faster
It removes the forbidden state by giving the 'both inputs high' case a useful meaning (toggle)
It needs no clock
It can store two bits
items.explanation: SR's S=R=1 was unpredictable. JK assigns that case a clean job — toggle — so every input combination is well defined. Speed and bit count are unchanged.

items.prompt: A T flip-flop has T held at 1. On each clock edge, the output:
choices: Stays constant
Toggles (flips to the opposite value)
Copies the clock exactly
Goes to an unknown state
items.explanation: T=1 means toggle: the output flips every clock edge. Because it flips once per edge, it changes half as often as the clock.

items.prompt: A T flip-flop with T = 1 produces an output whose frequency is:
choices: The same as the clock
Twice the clock frequency
Half the clock frequency (a divide-by-2)
Zero
items.explanation: Toggling once per edge means a full output cycle takes two clock cycles, so the output runs at half the clock frequency. This divide-by-2 behavior is the basis of counters.

items.prompt: A T flip-flop is most simply described as:
choices: A JK flip-flop with J and K tied together
Two D latches in series
An AND gate with feedback
A flip-flop that has no clock
items.explanation: Tie J and K together and you get the T flip-flop: T=0 holds, T=1 toggles. It is just a one-input view of the JK.

items.prompt: A JK flip-flop currently has Q = 0. The inputs are J = 0, K = 1 at the clock edge. What does Q become?
choices: 1 (set)
0 (reset, and it was already 0)
It toggles to 1
Unknown
items.explanation: J=0, K=1 is the reset case, forcing Q to 0. Since it was already 0, it stays 0. Toggle only happens when both J and K are 1.

items.prompt: A T flip-flop has Q = 1. The input is T = 0 at the clock edge. What is Q after the edge?
choices: 1 (hold — T = 0 means no change)
It toggles each edge regardless
Unknown
items.explanation: T=0 is the hold mode, so Q keeps its value (1). Only T=1 causes a toggle.

items.prompt: Why are T flip-flops the natural building block for counters?
choices: They are the cheapest flip-flop
Each one divides the clock by two, so chaining them produces the doubling pattern of binary counting
They do not need a clock
They can store more than one bit each
items.explanation: With T=1, each flip-flop toggles at half the rate of the one before it — exactly the 1, 2, 4, 8... place-value pattern of binary numbers, which is what a counter produces.

lessons.title: Registers and Shift Registers
lessons.content:
One flip-flop stores one bit. Real systems handle whole numbers, so we group flip-flops together.

Line up several flip-flops side by side, all sharing the same clock, and you get a **register** — a small box that stores a multi-bit number. With 8 flip-flops you store an 8-bit number; on each clock edge they all capture their inputs at once. This is a **parallel-load** register: a whole number goes in (or stays held) in a single tick. Registers are the scratchpad memory inside processors.

Now wire them differently. Connect each flip-flop's output to the *next* flip-flop's input, like people passing a bucket down a line. On each clock edge, every stored bit slides one step to the right. That is a **shift register** — it slides bits along, one stage per clock. The diagram shows a 4-bit shift register: feed a bit in at the left, and four clocks later it appears at the right.

Shift registers come in flavors named by how data enters and leaves (S = serial, one bit at a time; P = parallel, all at once):

- **SISO** (serial-in, serial-out): a delay line — a bit goes in, comes out a few clocks later.
- **SIPO** (serial-in, parallel-out): bits arrive one at a time, then you read the whole word at once. This *de-serializes* a stream.
- **PISO** (parallel-in, serial-out): load a whole word, then send it out one bit at a time. This *serializes* a word — how a number gets sent over a single wire.
- **PIPO** (parallel-in, parallel-out): that is just an ordinary register.

One neat variant: feed some outputs back through an XOR gate into the input, and the shifting bits cycle through a long, scrambled-looking sequence. That is a **linear-feedback shift register (LFSR)**, a cheap way to make pseudo-random-looking bit patterns — useful for test patterns and error checks.

**Recap:** flip-flops in parallel = a register (stores a number); flip-flops in a chain = a shift register (slides bits along), used to serialize, de-serialize, delay, and (with feedback) make pseudo-random sequences.

items.prompt: What is a register, in the simplest terms?
choices: A single flip-flop
Several flip-flops sharing one clock, used to store a multi-bit number
A type of logic gate
A clock generator
items.explanation: A register is a group of flip-flops on a common clock; together they store a whole number (e.g. 8 flip-flops hold 8 bits). A single flip-flop stores only one bit.

items.prompt: What does a shift register do on each clock edge?
choices: Loads an entire new number in parallel
Slides every stored bit one stage along the chain
Adds one to the stored number
Clears all bits to zero
items.explanation: In a shift register, each flip-flop's output feeds the next one's input, so on every clock the bits march one stage along — like passing a bucket down a line.

items.prompt: A 4-bit shift register currently holds the bits 1 0 1 1 (leftmost first). A 0 is fed in at the left on the next clock edge. What does it hold after that edge?
choices: 1 0 1 1 (unchanged)
items.explanation: Everything slides one place to the right and the new 0 enters at the left: 1011 becomes 0101 (the rightmost 1 falls off the end). The contents always move, so 'unchanged' is wrong.

items.prompt: Which shift register turns a serial stream of bits arriving one at a time into a whole parallel word you can read at once?
choices: PISO (parallel-in, serial-out)
SIPO (serial-in, parallel-out)
SISO (serial-in, serial-out)
A single D flip-flop
items.explanation: SIPO clocks bits in one at a time, then presents all stage outputs together — a de-serializer. PISO does the reverse (word in, bits out).

items.prompt: You want to send an 8-bit number over a single wire, one bit at a time. Which structure do you use?
choices: PISO (parallel-in, serial-out)
SIPO (serial-in, parallel-out)
A parallel-load register only
An AND gate
items.explanation: PISO loads the whole word at once, then shifts it out bit by bit — a serializer, exactly what sending over one wire requires. SIPO does the opposite.

items.prompt: A 'parallel-load' register captures its input when?
choices: One bit per clock over several clocks
All bits together on a single clock edge
Only when power is applied
Continuously, with no clock
items.explanation: Parallel load means the entire multi-bit word is captured in one clock edge — all flip-flops grab their inputs simultaneously. Loading one bit per clock is shift-register behavior.

items.prompt: What makes an LFSR (linear-feedback shift register) different from a plain shift register?
choices: It has no clock
It feeds some outputs back through an XOR gate into the input, so it cycles through a long pseudo-random-looking sequence
It can only store one bit
It counts upward in plain binary
items.explanation: An LFSR adds XOR feedback from selected stages back to the input. That feedback makes the bit pattern cycle through a long, scrambled-looking sequence — useful for test patterns and error checks. A plain shift register just slides bits with no feedback.

items.prompt: How many bits does a SISO (serial-in, serial-out) shift register effectively act as?
choices: A parallel adder
A delay line — a bit goes in and comes out a fixed number of clocks later
A clock divider only
A register with no output
items.explanation: Serial in, serial out means a bit enters at one end and emerges at the other after as many clocks as there are stages — a simple delay line.

lessons.title: Counters and the One-Clock Discipline
lessons.content:
A **counter** counts clock ticks. Think of a digital clock ticking off seconds, or a tally that goes up by one each beat. Internally it is just flip-flops arranged so their stored number increases (or decreases) by one on every clock edge. The diagram shows a 3-bit up counter stepping 0, 1, 2, ... 7 and then wrapping back to 0.

There are two ways to wire a counter, and the difference matters.

- An **asynchronous (ripple) counter** chains the flip-flops so each one's output clocks the next, like a row of dominoes. It is simple, but the change has to *ripple* down the chain, so the flip-flops do not all update at the same instant. During that ripple the output briefly shows wrong values (glitches), and the more bits you add, the slower it settles.
- A **synchronous counter** clocks *every* flip-flop from the same clock at once, with a little logic deciding the next value. All bits change together, cleanly, no matter how many there are. **Always prefer synchronous counters.**

Counters can count **up** or **down**, and a **modulo-N** (mod-N) counter counts 0 up to N−1 and then wraps back to 0. A counter that counts 0–9 (mod-10) is how each digit of a decimal display works.

Now the big-picture rule that ties this whole sub-topic together: the **synchronous design discipline**. Use **one clock**, have **every flip-flop update on the same edge**, and put only ordinary combinational logic between them. This keeps everything in step and makes the design predictable.

There is one tempting mistake to avoid. Suppose you want a counter to pause. The wrong fix is to *gate the clock* — ANDing the clock with an on/off signal to stop the ticks. That corrupts the carefully balanced clock and causes glitches. The right fix is a **clock enable**: let the clock keep ticking freely, but add a little logic on the data input so that when 'enable' is off, each flip-flop simply re-loads its own current value (no visible change). The slogan to remember: **never gate the clock — gate the data instead.**

**Recap:** counters count ticks; synchronous (all flip-flops on one clock) beats ripple. Mod-N counters wrap at N. Keep one clock, register everything on the same edge, and pause with a clock enable — never by chopping the clock.

items.prompt: What does a counter do?
choices: Stores a single bit forever
Counts clock ticks, changing its stored number by one on each clock edge
Converts serial data to parallel
Generates the clock signal
items.explanation: A counter is flip-flops arranged so their stored number steps up (or down) by one per clock edge — like a digital clock ticking off seconds.

items.prompt: A 3-bit up counter currently shows 011 (decimal 3). What does it show after the next clock edge?
choices: 011 (unchanged)
items.explanation: An up counter adds one each edge: 3 + 1 = 4, which is 100 in binary. It only wraps to 000 after reaching its top value (111 = 7).

items.prompt: A 3-bit up counter shows 111 (decimal 7). What does it show after the next clock edge?
choices: 111 (it stops)
000 (it wraps back to 0)
Unknown
items.explanation: With only 3 bits the highest value is 7 (111). One more tick wraps it back to 000 — this 0..7 cycle is a mod-8 counter. It cannot show 8 (that would need a 4th bit).

items.prompt: Why does a wide ripple (asynchronous) counter briefly show wrong values?
choices: Its flip-flops have no clock
Each stage clocks the next, so the change ripples down the chain and the bits do not all update at the same instant
It uses too much power
It has no reset
items.explanation: In a ripple counter the bits change one after another as the carry ripples through, so for a moment the output passes through incorrect intermediate values (glitches). A synchronous counter avoids this by updating all bits together.

items.prompt: What is the main advantage of a synchronous counter over a ripple counter?
choices: It needs fewer flip-flops
All bits update together on the same clock edge, so the output is clean and it stays fast as it gets wider
It does not need a clock
It can count higher with the same number of bits
items.explanation: Synchronous means every flip-flop shares one clock and updates simultaneously — no rippling, no glitch parade, and the speed does not degrade with width. This is why synchronous is always preferred.

items.prompt: A 'modulo-10' (mod-10) counter counts through which values before wrapping?
choices: 0 to 10
0 to 9, then back to 0
1 to 10
0 to 15
items.explanation: A mod-N counter runs 0 up to N−1 then wraps. Mod-10 counts 0..9 then back to 0 — exactly what one digit of a decimal display needs.

items.prompt: You need to pause a counter for a while. What is the correct way to do it?
choices: AND the clock with an on/off signal so the clock stops ticking
Use a clock enable: let the clock run freely, but feed each flip-flop its own value back when paused
Disconnect the power
Lower the clock voltage
items.explanation: Gating (chopping) the clock injects glitches and skew and breaks the timing. The clean fix is a clock enable: the clock keeps running and, when disabled, each flip-flop reloads its current value so nothing visibly changes.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the synchronous design discipline?
choices: Use many separate clocks and gate each one as needed
Use one clock, have every flip-flop update on the same edge, and put only ordinary combinational logic between them
Prefer ripple counters everywhere because they are simpler
Avoid flip-flops and rely on transparent latches
items.explanation: One clock, everything registered on the same edge, plain combinational logic between registers, and never a gated clock — that discipline keeps the whole design in step and predictable.

lessons.title: What an FSM Is: States, Inputs, and Transitions
lessons.content:
Think about a traffic light. At any moment it is showing exactly one color: green, yellow, or red. It never shows two at once, and it never invents a fourth. When a timer ticks, it moves from one color to the next in a fixed order. That simple idea is a **finite state machine (FSM)**.

An FSM is a system that is always in exactly one of a small, fixed set of situations called **states**. It moves from one state to another based on **inputs** (things that happen, like a timer ticking or a button being pressed). The move from one state to another is called a **transition**. While in a state, the machine can also produce **outputs** (things it does, like turning on a light).

You already know that flip-flops and registers can *remember* a value. An FSM is what you build *with* that memory: the remembered value is the current state, and a bit of logic decides which state to go to next. It is the standard way to design anything that follows a sequence of steps.

Everyday FSMs are everywhere. A turnstile is either locked or unlocked. A vending machine tracks how many coins you have inserted. A microwave moves through its menu screens. Each is always in one state and changes state on an input.

Recap: an FSM = a small set of states + inputs that trigger transitions + outputs. Get those three things straight and you can describe almost any step-by-step system.

items.prompt: In a finite state machine, what is a "state"?
choices: One of the small, fixed set of situations the machine can be in at a given moment
An input signal coming from outside the machine
The wire that carries the clock signal
A list of every possible input the machine accepts
items.explanation: A state is one of the limited situations the machine can be in, like 'green' for a traffic light. The machine is always in exactly one state. Inputs and clocks are separate things that cause the machine to move between states.

items.prompt: How many states is a finite state machine in at any single moment?
choices: As many as it wants, at the same time
Exactly one
Always two: a current and a backup
Zero until an input arrives
items.explanation: By definition the machine sits in exactly one state at a time, just as a traffic light shows one color. That is what makes its behavior easy to reason about.

items.prompt: What is a "transition" in an FSM?
choices: The output the machine shows while idle
The move from one state to another, triggered by an input
The number of flip-flops the machine uses
The moment the power is first switched on
items.explanation: A transition is the change from one state to another. It is triggered by an input (or a clock tick). The other choices describe outputs, hardware count, and power-up, not transitions.

items.prompt: For a traffic light that changes when a timer ticks, what is the "input"?
choices: The color currently showing
The timer tick
The bulb that lights up
The number of states
items.explanation: An input is something from outside that can trigger a transition. Here the timer tick is the input. The current color is the state, and the lit bulb is the output.

items.prompt: In a vending machine FSM, which of these is best described as an OUTPUT?
choices: A coin being inserted
The button the user presses
Dispensing the snack
The amount of money still owed
items.explanation: An output is something the machine does, like dispensing the snack. Inserting a coin and pressing a button are inputs; the amount owed is part of the state.

items.prompt: You learned that flip-flops and registers can remember a value. How does that connect to an FSM?
choices: It does not; FSMs never use memory
The remembered value IS the machine's current state
Flip-flops replace the need for any inputs
Memory is only used to store the outputs
items.explanation: An FSM uses memory (flip-flops/registers) to hold its current state. Logic then reads that state plus the inputs to decide the next state. Without memory there would be nothing to remember which state you are in.

items.prompt: Why is it called a "finite" state machine?
choices: Because it eventually stops running
Because it has only a fixed, limited number of states
Because it can only accept one input ever
Because it runs for a finite number of seconds
items.explanation: "Finite" refers to the number of states: there is a fixed, countable set of them. The machine can keep running forever; it just keeps moving among that limited set of states.

items.prompt: Which everyday device is the clearest example of a finite state machine?
choices: A plain resistor
A turnstile that is either locked or unlocked and changes on a coin or a push
A bag of marbles
A length of copper wire
items.explanation: A turnstile has a small set of states (locked, unlocked) and moves between them on inputs (coin, push) - a textbook FSM. A resistor, marbles, and wire have no states or transitions.

items.prompt: Which behavior is NOT naturally described as a finite state machine?
choices: An elevator deciding which floor to visit next
A washing machine stepping through wash, rinse, spin
An output that depends only on the current inputs, with nothing remembered
A traffic light cycling green to yellow to red
items.explanation: If an output depends only on the present inputs and nothing is remembered, that is plain combinational logic, not an FSM. An FSM needs states (remembered situations) that affect behavior. The other three all track a state over time.

lessons.title: State Diagrams and State-Transition Tables
lessons.content:
Once you know an FSM is states plus transitions, you need a way to *draw* it and *write it down*. There are two standard tools, and they describe the exact same machine.

The first is the **state diagram**. Picture each state as a bubble (a circle). Draw an arrow from one bubble to another to show a transition, and label that arrow with the input that triggers it. An arrow that loops back to the same bubble (a **self-loop**) means "on this input, stay where you are." The traffic light becomes three bubbles - GREEN, YELLOW, RED - with arrows looping around. A turnstile is two bubbles, LOCKED and UNLOCKED, with a coin arrow one way and a push arrow the other.

The second tool is the **state-transition table**. This is just a chart with one row for each combination of (current state, input). Each row says: if you are in this state and you see this input, here is the next state (and any output). For the turnstile:

| Current state | Input | Next state |
|---|---|---|
| LOCKED | coin | UNLOCKED |
| LOCKED | push | LOCKED |
| UNLOCKED | push | LOCKED |
| UNLOCKED | coin | UNLOCKED |

The diagram is great for *seeing* and reasoning about the machine at a glance. The table is great for being *complete and precise* - every state-and-input combination has exactly one row, so nothing is forgotten. Engineers usually sketch the diagram first, then write the table to make sure no case is missing.

Recap: the diagram (bubbles and arrows) and the table (a row per state-and-input) are two views of one machine. Use the diagram to think, the table to be thorough.

items.prompt: In a state diagram, what do the bubbles (circles) represent?
choices: The inputs
The states
The clock edges
The outputs only
items.explanation: Bubbles are states. The arrows between bubbles are the transitions, and the labels on the arrows are the inputs that trigger them.

items.prompt: What does an arrow between two bubbles in a state diagram show, and what is its label?
choices: It shows a transition, labeled with the input that triggers it
It shows a wire, labeled with its resistance
It shows a clock, labeled with its frequency
It shows an output, labeled with a state number
items.explanation: An arrow is a transition from one state to another, and its label is the input that causes that move. This is how the diagram captures 'on input X, go from state A to state B.'

items.prompt: An arrow that leaves a bubble and loops right back to the same bubble means:
choices: The machine resets to its starting state
On that input, the machine stays in the same state
The machine has an error
Two states have merged into one
items.explanation: A self-loop means 'on this input, stay put.' For example, a locked turnstile that is pushed stays LOCKED, so a 'push' arrow loops back to LOCKED.

items.prompt: In a state-transition table, what does a single row tell you?
choices: The total number of states in the machine
Given a current state and an input, what the next state (and output) is
The voltage on each flip-flop
How long the machine has been running
items.explanation: Each row pairs a current state with an input and lists the resulting next state (and any output). One row per (state, input) combination makes the table complete.

items.prompt: How are a state diagram and a state-transition table related?
choices: They describe two different machines that happen to look alike
They are two different views of the exact same machine
The diagram is for inputs and the table is for outputs
The table can only describe machines with two states
items.explanation: Both describe the same FSM. The diagram is the visual view (bubbles and arrows); the table is the written view (rows). You can build either one from the other.

items.prompt: Why do engineers often write a state-transition table after sketching the diagram?
choices: The table makes the machine run faster
The table forces them to cover every state-and-input combination, so no case is forgotten
Diagrams cannot show transitions
The table removes the need for any states
items.explanation: A diagram is easy to read but easy to leave incomplete. The table has a row for every (state, input) pair, which is a built-in checklist that nothing was missed.

items.prompt: Given the traffic-light diagram (GREEN to YELLOW to RED, each on a timer tick), if the machine is in YELLOW and a timer tick arrives, what is the next state?
choices: GREEN
YELLOW (it stays)
RED
It turns off
items.explanation: Following the arrows: GREEN to YELLOW to RED to GREEN. From YELLOW, a timer tick takes the machine to RED. Tracing arrows from the current state is exactly how you read a state diagram.

items.prompt: Given the turnstile diagram, the machine is UNLOCKED and the input is "push." What is the next state?
choices: UNLOCKED (it stays)
LOCKED
Both at once
It depends on the clock frequency
items.explanation: From UNLOCKED, a push closes the gate behind you, so the machine transitions to LOCKED. A coin while UNLOCKED would be a self-loop (stay UNLOCKED).

items.prompt: A machine has 3 states and accepts 2 possible inputs. To be complete, how many rows should its state-transition table have?
items.explanation: One row per (state, input) pair: 3 states times 2 inputs = 6 rows. Listing all of them guarantees every situation has a defined next state.

lessons.title: Moore vs Mealy and a Sequence Detector
lessons.content:
So far our outputs have come from the state itself: in GREEN the green bulb is on. But there is a second, subtly different way to produce outputs, and the two styles have names.

In a **Moore machine**, the output depends on the **state only**. You write the output on the bubble: "in state B, output = 1." The output changes only when the state changes.

In a **Mealy machine**, the output depends on the **state AND the current input**. You write the output on the arrow: "on input 1 from state A, output = 1 during that move." The output can change the instant the input changes, even before the state settles.

Why have both? It is a trade-off. **Moore** outputs come straight from the stored state, so they are steady and clean (they only change at a clock tick) - great when something downstream needs a glitch-free signal. The cost: a Moore machine reacts one clock tick later, and often needs a few extra states. **Mealy** outputs react in the same cycle as the input, so they are faster and the machine usually needs fewer states - but the output can briefly glitch because it follows the input directly.

A classic example is a **sequence detector**: watch a stream of bits and raise the output to 1 whenever the recent bits spell a target pattern, such as 1011. A Moore version uses one state per amount of progress (saw 1, saw 10, saw 101, matched) and lights the output in the 'matched' state. A Mealy version can raise the output on the final transition itself, saving that dedicated 'matched' state.

Recap: Moore = output from state (clean but a tick slower); Mealy = output from state plus input (fast but can glitch). Same problem, two valid styles.

items.prompt: In a Moore machine, the output depends on:
choices: The current state only
The current input only
Both the state and the current input
The clock frequency
items.explanation: Moore outputs are a function of the state alone, so they are drawn on the state bubbles. The output changes only when the state changes.

items.prompt: In a Mealy machine, the output depends on:
choices: The current state only
Both the current state and the current input
Nothing; the output is constant
Only how many states exist
items.explanation: Mealy outputs depend on the state AND the current input, so they are drawn on the transition arrows. That lets the output respond the moment the input changes.

items.prompt: What is the key difference between a Moore machine and a Mealy machine?
choices: Moore uses inputs; Mealy does not
Moore output depends on state only, while Mealy output depends on state plus the current input
Moore can have transitions; Mealy cannot
There is no real difference; the names are interchangeable
items.explanation: The single defining difference is where the output comes from: state only (Moore) versus state plus current input (Mealy). Both have states, inputs, and transitions.

items.prompt: If you see an output value written on a state bubble (not on an arrow), the machine is:
choices: A Mealy machine
A Moore machine
Neither; that is illegal
Both at once
items.explanation: Output on the bubble means the output depends on the state alone - that is the Moore style. Mealy machines write the output on the arrows instead.

items.prompt: You need a clean, steady output signal that only changes on clock ticks to drive another synchronous block. Which style is the safer choice?
choices: Mealy, because it is always faster
Moore, because its output comes from the stored state and is glitch-free
Neither can produce a clean signal
Whichever uses fewer inputs
items.explanation: A Moore output comes directly from the state register, so it only changes at clock ticks and does not glitch - ideal for feeding another synchronous block. A Mealy output follows the input and can briefly glitch.

items.prompt: Compared with a Moore machine, a Mealy machine's response to an input change is typically:
choices: Slower by one clock tick
Faster, because the output can change in the same cycle as the input
Exactly the same in timing
Completely unrelated to the input
items.explanation: Because the Mealy output depends directly on the current input, it can change in the same cycle. A Moore output must wait until the input first changes the state at a clock tick, so it lags by about one cycle.

items.prompt: For the same job, a Mealy machine often needs fewer states than a Moore machine. Why?
choices: Mealy machines delete their reset state
Mealy can produce the output on a transition itself, so it does not need a dedicated state just to hold that output
Mealy machines ignore some of the inputs
Moore machines are not allowed to share states
items.explanation: A Moore machine often needs an extra state whose only job is to carry an output value. Mealy can attach that output to the transition arrow instead, eliminating the dedicated state.

items.prompt: A "sequence detector" for the pattern 1011 does what?
choices: Counts how many bits have arrived in total
Raises its output to 1 whenever the most recent bits in the stream spell 1011
Converts the input to its two's-complement
Stops the clock when it sees a 0
items.explanation: A sequence detector watches a bit stream and asserts its output when the recent bits match the target pattern (here 1011). It does not simply count bits or do arithmetic.

items.prompt: In a Moore-style 1011 detector, why are there states like "saw 1", "saw 10", and "saw 101"?
choices: To count the total length of the input
Each state remembers how much of the target pattern has been matched so far
Because Moore machines must always have exactly four states
To store the output for later use
items.explanation: Each state records the progress toward the pattern: how many leading bits of 1011 have been seen. When the last needed bit arrives, the machine reaches the 'matched' state and asserts the output.

items.prompt: Which statement best summarizes the Moore-versus-Mealy trade-off?
choices: Moore is always better than Mealy in every way
Moore gives clean, registered outputs but reacts a tick later; Mealy reacts faster with fewer states but can glitch
Mealy uses more flip-flops and is always slower
They have identical timing and identical state counts
items.explanation: Moore trades a tick of latency (and sometimes extra states) for clean, glitch-free outputs. Mealy trades possible glitches for faster response and usually fewer states. Neither is universally best; you pick based on what the design needs.

lessons.title: Naming States in Binary and the Three-Block Pattern
lessons.content:
A diagram uses friendly names like GREEN or LOCKED, but hardware only stores bits. So every state gets a bit pattern - this is called the **state encoding**. There are a few common ways to do it.

**Binary encoding** numbers the states 0, 1, 2, 3, ... in ordinary binary. With this scheme, $N$ states need $\\lceil \\log_2 N \\rceil$ flip-flops - that is, just enough bits to count up to $N$. For example, 4 states fit in 2 bits (00, 01, 10, 11), and 5 states need 3 bits. Binary uses the fewest flip-flops.

**Gray encoding** is like binary but arranged so that only one bit changes between neighboring states. That can reduce electrical noise and is handy in special cases, but the idea to remember is just: one bit flips at a time.

**One-hot encoding** gives each state its own flip-flop and turns exactly one bit on at a time (0001, 0010, 0100, 1000). It uses the most flip-flops, but the logic to figure out the next state becomes very simple and fast. This is why one-hot is a popular choice on FPGAs, which have flip-flops to spare. (You do not need the deep reasons now - just the idea.)

However you encode the states, a clean FSM is built from **three blocks** that match the three parts you already know. (1) **Next-state logic** looks at the current state and the inputs and decides where to go next. (2) The **state register** is the flip-flops that remember the current state, updating on each clock tick. (3) The **output logic** turns the current state (Moore) or state plus input (Mealy) into outputs. Block 1 is the transitions, block 2 is the memory, block 3 is the outputs.

Recap: encoding assigns bits to states (binary = fewest bits, one-hot = simplest logic), and the three-block pattern - next-state logic, state register, output logic - is the standard, tidy way to organize any FSM.

items.prompt: What does "state encoding" mean?
choices: Hiding the FSM so others cannot read it
Assigning each named state a specific bit pattern that the hardware stores
Choosing how many inputs the machine has
Locking the FSM so it cannot change states
items.explanation: Encoding maps each friendly state name to a pattern of bits (like 00, 01, 10) that the flip-flops actually hold. The hardware works in bits, not names.

items.prompt: Using binary encoding, how many flip-flops (bits) does a machine with 4 states need?
items.explanation: Binary encoding needs just enough bits to count to N. For 4 states, 2 bits give the patterns 00, 01, 10, 11 - exactly four. The formula is ceil(log2 N) = ceil(log2 4) = 2.

items.prompt: With binary encoding, how many flip-flops does a 5-state machine need?
items.explanation: 2 bits only cover 4 states (00 to 11), so 5 states need 3 bits (which cover up to 8). That is ceil(log2 5) = 3. The 3 leftover codes are unused.

items.prompt: What is one-hot encoding?
choices: Using the fewest possible bits to number the states
Giving each state its own flip-flop, with exactly one bit on at a time
Changing only one bit between neighboring states
Storing the output instead of the state
items.explanation: One-hot uses one flip-flop per state and keeps exactly one bit high (e.g., 0001, 0010, 0100). It uses more flip-flops but makes the next-state logic simple. Changing one bit between neighbors describes Gray code instead.

items.prompt: A 6-state machine uses one-hot encoding. How many flip-flops does it use?
items.explanation: One-hot uses one flip-flop per state, so 6 states need 6 flip-flops. (Binary would need only 3, but its next-state logic is more involved.)

items.prompt: Which statement captures the binary-versus-one-hot trade-off?
choices: Binary uses fewer flip-flops; one-hot uses simpler, faster next-state logic
One-hot always uses fewer flip-flops
Binary is always faster on every chip
They use identical resources
items.explanation: Binary minimizes the number of flip-flops; one-hot spends extra flip-flops to make the next-state logic simpler and faster. That simpler logic is why one-hot is common on FPGAs, which have flip-flops to spare.

items.prompt: What is the defining feature of Gray encoding for states?
choices: Every state uses all bits set to 1
Only one bit changes between neighboring states
It uses the most flip-flops of any scheme
It stores the inputs instead of the state
items.explanation: Gray encoding is arranged so neighboring states differ by exactly one bit. That single-bit change reduces switching noise and is useful in certain situations.

items.prompt: What are the three blocks of the standard three-block FSM structure?
choices: Input filter, amplifier, output filter
Next-state logic, state register, output logic
Clock, reset, power supply
Adder, multiplier, comparator
items.explanation: The three blocks are next-state logic (decides where to go), the state register (remembers where you are), and output logic (turns the state into outputs). They map onto transitions, memory, and outputs.

items.prompt: In the three-block structure, what is the job of the state register?
choices: To decide which state to go to next
To remember the current state, updating it on each clock tick
To generate the output signals
To count the inputs
items.explanation: The state register is the flip-flops that hold the current state and load the next state on each clock tick. Deciding the next state is the next-state logic's job; producing outputs is the output logic's job.

items.prompt: Which block looks at the current state and the inputs to decide where the machine goes next?
choices: The state register
The output logic
The next-state logic
The clock
items.explanation: The next-state logic combines the current state with the inputs to compute the next state. It is the hardware version of the arrows in the state diagram.

lessons.title: Illegal States, Safe Reset, and FSM-with-Datapath
lessons.content:
Suppose a machine has 5 useful states. With binary encoding you need 3 bits, which can hold 8 different patterns - so 3 of those patterns are not assigned to any real state. These extras are **illegal states** (also called unused or undefined states). They are not supposed to happen, but a power glitch or a stray noise spike could flip a flip-flop and drop the machine into one of them.

If nothing handles that case, the machine can get stuck or behave randomly. The fix is **illegal-state recovery**: design the next-state logic so that from any unexpected pattern, the machine goes to a known **safe state** - usually the reset or idle state. In practice this means having a catch-all "otherwise, go to reset" rule that covers every pattern you did not explicitly list.

Closely related is a **safe reset**. Every well-behaved FSM has a defined reset state it starts in (for example, IDLE), so when power comes up or you press reset, the machine begins from a known place rather than a random one. Reset plus illegal-state recovery together mean the machine can never be stuck somewhere undefined.

Finally, real designs rarely use an FSM alone. They pair a control FSM with a **datapath** - the registers, adders, counters, and memory that do the actual work. This pairing is called an **FSM with datapath (FSMD)**. The FSM sends **control signals** telling the datapath what to do this step (load, add, shift), and the datapath sends back **status flags** (done, zero, overflow) that the FSM reads to decide its next move. The FSM is the manager; the datapath is the crew. This control-plus-datapath split is the core idea inside every processor.

Recap: unused encodings create illegal states, so route any unexpected pattern back to a safe reset/idle state. And the big picture - an FSM controlling a datapath via control signals and status flags - is how real machines get built.

items.prompt: Why do illegal (unused) states exist in a binary-encoded FSM with 5 states?
choices: Because the designer made a mistake
Because 3 bits hold 8 patterns but only 5 are assigned, leaving 3 unused
Because every FSM must have exactly 8 states
Because the clock creates extra states
items.explanation: 3 bits can represent 8 patterns. If only 5 are mapped to real states, the other 3 are unused/illegal. It is a natural consequence of the encoding, not a mistake.

items.prompt: How might a working machine actually land in an illegal state?
choices: It is impossible to ever reach one
A power glitch or noise spike flips a flip-flop into an unused pattern
Only by adding more inputs
Whenever the output is 1
items.explanation: Normal transitions never go to illegal states, but a glitch, noise spike, or power transient can corrupt the stored bits and leave the register in an unused pattern. That is exactly why recovery is needed.

items.prompt: What is the standard way to handle illegal states (illegal-state recovery)?
choices: Ignore them and hope they never happen
Add logic so that from any unexpected pattern the machine goes to a known safe state
Add more illegal states
Turn off the clock permanently
items.explanation: Illegal-state recovery means routing any unlisted pattern back to a safe state (usually reset/idle) with a catch-all rule. Ignoring them risks the machine getting stuck or acting randomly.

items.prompt: When recovering from an illegal state, which state is the usual target?
choices: A random state
The reset or idle state, a known safe place to restart from
The state with the highest bit pattern
Any illegal state will do
items.explanation: Recovery sends the machine to a known safe state, typically reset/idle, so it restarts from a defined point. Going to a random or another illegal state would not fix anything.

items.prompt: Why does a well-designed FSM have a defined reset state?
choices: So that on power-up or reset it starts from a known state instead of a random one
To make the machine run faster
To remove the need for inputs
Reset states are optional decoration
items.explanation: A reset state guarantees the machine begins from a known, defined place when power comes up or reset is pressed. Without it, the starting state would be unpredictable.

items.prompt: What is an FSM with datapath (FSMD)?
choices: An FSM with no inputs
A control FSM paired with a datapath (registers, adders, counters, memory) that does the actual work
Two identical FSMs running in parallel
An FSM that has been minimized
items.explanation: An FSMD couples a control FSM with a datapath. The FSM directs the steps; the datapath holds and processes the data. This pairing is the foundation of real processing systems.

items.prompt: In an FSMD, what does the control FSM send to the datapath?
choices: Status flags like done and overflow
Control signals such as load, add, and shift that tell the datapath what to do
The clock crystal
Nothing; they do not communicate
items.explanation: The FSM issues control signals (load, add, shift, select) that command the datapath each step. The flags travel the other way, from the datapath back to the FSM.

items.prompt: How does the datapath communicate back to the control FSM so the FSM can decide its next move?
choices: By changing the clock frequency
By sending status flags such as done, zero, or overflow
It cannot communicate back at all
By resetting the FSM each cycle
items.explanation: The datapath returns status flags (done, zero, overflow, comparison results). The FSM reads these as conditions on its transitions - for example, loop until a 'done' flag is set.

items.prompt: In the "manager and crew" analogy for an FSMD, which part is the manager?
choices: The datapath, because it does the work
The control FSM, because it decides the steps and directs the datapath
The clock, because it sets the pace
There is no manager
items.explanation: The control FSM is the manager: it decides what happens each step and tells the datapath. The datapath is the crew that does the actual computing and reports status back.

items.prompt: Why does the FSM-with-datapath idea matter for understanding real computers?
choices: It does not; processors do not use FSMs
The control-plus-datapath split is the core structure inside every processor
It only applies to traffic lights
It replaces the need for memory
items.explanation: Splitting a system into a controller (FSM) and a datapath (the registers and arithmetic units it directs) is exactly how processors are organized. The toy examples scale up to real machines using this same idea.

lessons.title: Delays & Flip-Flop Timing Parameters
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Nothing in electronics happens instantly. When a signal changes, it takes a tiny but real amount of time to travel through a wire and a logic gate before the output settles. And a flip-flop (the one-bit memory you met earlier, which copies its input D to its output Q on the clock edge) only copies its input correctly if that input sits still for a moment around the clock edge. Get the timing wrong and your circuit misbehaves.

**Build.** First, the delay through plain logic. **Propagation delay** $t_{pd}$ is the *longest* time you must wait, after an input changes, before the output is guaranteed to be at its final, steady value. (There is also a *shortest* time before the output even begins to move, called the **contamination delay** $t_{cd}$; it is always smaller, $t_{cd} \le t_{pd}$. We will use it later.)

Now the flip-flop. Think of jumping onto a train just as the doors close. You must be standing in the doorway *before* the doors start to shut, and you must stay put *until* they finish. A flip-flop has the same two requirements around its clock edge:

- **Setup time** $t_{su}$ — the data input D must already be steady for at least $t_{su}$ *before* the clock edge.
- **Hold time** $t_{h}$ — D must stay steady for at least $t_{h}$ *after* the clock edge.

After the edge, the output isn't ready instantly either. **Clock-to-Q delay** $t_{cq}$ is the time from the clock edge until the new value appears at Q.

**Recap.** Plain logic has a delay $t_{pd}$; a flip-flop needs its data steady for a window ($t_{su}$ before, $t_{h}$ after) and then produces its output after $t_{cq}$. These few numbers control everything that follows.

items.prompt: What does propagation delay $t_{pd}$ measure?
choices: The longest time after an input changes until the output is guaranteed steady at its new value
The voltage of the output once it settles
How often the clock ticks
The time the data must be held after the clock edge
items.explanation: Propagation delay is the worst-case (longest) wait for the output to finish settling after an input change. It is a time, not a voltage, and it has nothing to do with the clock rate or with the flip-flop's hold requirement.

items.prompt: A flip-flop's data input D must be steady for a short time BEFORE the clock edge. What is that requirement called?
choices: Hold time $t_{h}$
Clock-to-Q delay $t_{cq}$
Setup time $t_{su}$
Contamination delay $t_{cd}$
items.explanation: Setup time is the 'be in position before the doors close' requirement: D must be steady for at least $t_{su}$ before the edge. Hold time is the matching 'stay put after' requirement, which comes after the edge.

items.prompt: What does hold time $t_{h}$ require?
choices: That the clock stays high for $t_{h}$
That the data input D stays steady for $t_{h}$ AFTER the clock edge
That the output Q appears within $t_{h}$
That the data changes exactly at the clock edge
items.explanation: Hold time is about staying steady after the edge, so the flip-flop finishes capturing the value. It says nothing about the clock's shape or when Q appears, and the data must NOT change right at the edge.

items.prompt: After the clock edge, how long until the new value shows up at the output Q?
choices: Instantly, with no delay
After the setup time $t_{su}$
After the clock-to-Q delay $t_{cq}$
After the propagation delay of the next gate only
items.explanation: The clock-to-Q delay $t_{cq}$ is the flip-flop's own internal delay from edge to valid output. Output is never instant. Setup time is a requirement on the input before the edge, not the output delay.

items.prompt: Which statement about contamination delay $t_{cd}$ and propagation delay $t_{pd}$ is always true?
choices: $t_{cd}$ is always larger than $t_{pd}$
$t_{cd}$ is always smaller than or equal to $t_{pd}$
They are always exactly equal
They are completely unrelated numbers
items.explanation: The output cannot finish settling before it has even started to move, so the earliest-change time $t_{cd}$ is always less than or equal to the fully-settled time $t_{pd}$.

items.prompt: Why is the 'jumping onto a train as the doors close' a good picture of setup and hold time?
choices: Because trains run on a fixed schedule like a clock
Because you must be in position before the doors start to close (setup) and stay put until they finish (hold)
Because the train is faster than walking
Because doors are made of metal like a chip
items.explanation: The analogy maps directly: the closing doors are the clock edge, being in position beforehand is setup time, and not moving until they finish is hold time. The schedule and materials are not the point.

items.prompt: Which of these is the correct order of events for one flip-flop capturing data?
choices: Output appears ($t_{cq}$), then data must be steady ($t_{su}$), then the clock edge
Data steady before edge ($t_{su}$), clock edge, data still steady after ($t_{h}$), then output appears ($t_{cq}$)
Clock edge, then data must be steady ($t_{su}$) for the next edge only
Hold ($t_{h}$) before the edge, setup ($t_{su}$) after the edge
items.explanation: Setup comes before the edge, hold comes just after the edge, and only then does the output emerge after $t_{cq}$. Setup is before the edge (not after) and hold is after (not before).

items.prompt: A friend says 'a flip-flop copies D to Q the instant the clock rises.' What is the small correction?
choices: The clock must fall, not rise
It copies only if D is steady through the setup/hold window, and Q appears after $t_{cq}$, not instantly
Flip-flops never copy D to Q
D and Q are always the same value at all times
items.explanation: Two refinements: the capture is reliable only if the setup and hold windows are respected, and the result appears after the clock-to-Q delay rather than instantly. The rising edge can indeed be the active edge, so that part isn't the issue.

items.prompt: Why do signals take time to travel through a gate at all?
choices: Because the clock is slow
Because real transistors and wires need a moment to charge and switch — nothing is instantaneous
Because the designer chose to add a delay
Because the data is too large
items.explanation: Delay is physical: transistors switch and wires charge up over a real, finite time. It is not something the designer adds on purpose, nor a side effect of the clock speed or data size.

lessons.title: Max Clock Frequency & the Critical Path
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Now we can answer the big practical question: how fast can a chip be clocked? The clock can't tick faster than the slowest journey a signal has to make in one tick.

**Build.** Picture two flip-flops with some logic between them: flip-flop A launches a value, the value crosses the logic, and flip-flop B must capture it on the *next* clock edge. For B to capture correctly, the value has to arrive and be steady before B's setup window. Add up the journey:

- $t_{cq}$ — time for A's output to appear after the edge,
- $t_{pd}$ — time to cross the logic,
- $t_{su}$ — the value must land this much before the next edge.

All of that must fit inside one clock period $T_{clk}$ (the time between edges):

$$T_{clk} \ge t_{cq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}$$

The fastest clock is just the reciprocal of the tightest period:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}}$$

A real chip has many such flip-flop-to-flip-flop paths. The slowest one — the one with the biggest $t_{cq}+t_{pd}+t_{su}$ — is the **critical path**, and it sets the speed limit for the whole chip. Speeding up any other path doesn't help; you must shorten the critical one.

**Recap.** The clock period must be at least clock-to-Q plus logic delay plus setup time, measured along the slowest (critical) path. That single slowest path caps the maximum clock frequency.

items.prompt: Which three delays must fit inside one clock period on a flip-flop-to-flip-flop path?
items.explanation: The journey is: output appears ($t_{cq}$), crosses the logic ($t_{pd}$), and must land before the next setup window ($t_{su}$). Hold time and contamination delay belong to a different check, covered in the next lesson.

items.prompt: A path has $t_{cq}=0.3\,\text{ns}$, $t_{pd}=4.2\,\text{ns}$, and $t_{su}=0.5\,\text{ns}$. What is the maximum clock frequency?
choices: About 100 MHz
About 200 MHz
About 238 MHz
About 500 MHz
items.explanation: Add the delays: $0.3+4.2+0.5 = 5.0\,\text{ns}$. The fastest clock is $1 / 5.0\,\text{ns} = 200\,\text{MHz}$. The trap answer 238 MHz comes from forgetting to add one of the terms.

items.prompt: What is the 'critical path' in a digital circuit?
choices: The path that uses the most power
The slowest flip-flop-to-flip-flop path, which sets the maximum clock speed
The shortest path between two gates
The path that carries the clock signal
items.explanation: The critical path is the one with the largest total delay; because the clock must wait for it, it caps the whole chip's speed. The shortest path matters for hold (next lesson), and the clock's own path is a separate idea.

items.prompt: You speed up a path that was NOT the critical path. What happens to the maximum clock frequency?
choices: It increases, because every speedup helps
It stays the same, because the critical path still sets the limit
It decreases
The clock stops working
items.explanation: The speed limit is set by the single slowest path. Improving a faster path leaves the bottleneck untouched, so $f_{max}$ doesn't change. You must shorten the critical path itself.

items.prompt: Why does the clock period have to be at least as long as the slowest path?
choices: Because the clock needs time to rise and fall
Because the launched value must travel through the logic and be steady at the next flip-flop before its setup window
Because slower clocks save power
Because the data is too large to move quickly
items.explanation: If the next edge arrives before the value has crossed the logic and settled, the capturing flip-flop won't see a steady input in time. The period must cover that whole journey plus setup.

items.prompt: Two paths exist: Path X totals 6 ns, Path Y totals 9 ns. What is the smallest clock period the chip can use?
choices: 6 ns
9 ns
3 ns
15 ns
items.explanation: The period must be at least as long as the slowest path, which is Path Y at 9 ns. Path Y is the critical path. You cannot use 6 ns or Path Y would fail; you do not add the two paths together.

items.prompt: If you make the combinational logic between two flip-flops simpler (smaller $t_{pd}$), what happens to $f_{max}$ for that path?
choices: $f_{max}$ goes up, because the total delay is smaller
$f_{max}$ goes down
$f_{max}$ is unaffected by logic delay
The flip-flops stop working
items.explanation: Since $f_{max} = 1 / (t_{cq}+t_{pd}+t_{su})$, reducing $t_{pd}$ shrinks the denominator and raises the maximum frequency. Less logic between flip-flops means a faster possible clock.

items.prompt: A path has $t_{cq}=0.4\,\text{ns}$, $t_{pd}=7.6\,\text{ns}$, $t_{su}=1.0\,\text{ns}$. What is the minimum clock period and roughly $f_{max}$?
choices: 9.0 ns, about 111 MHz
8.0 ns, about 125 MHz
7.6 ns, about 132 MHz
5.0 ns, about 200 MHz
items.explanation: Sum the delays: $0.4+7.6+1.0 = 9.0\,\text{ns}$, the minimum period. Then $1/9.0\,\text{ns} \approx 111\,\text{MHz}$. Leaving out a term gives the smaller, wrong periods.

items.prompt: What does $f_{max}$ stand for, and how is it related to the clock period?
choices: The maximum data value; unrelated to the period
The maximum clock frequency; it is 1 divided by the minimum clock period
The maximum flip-flop count; equal to the period
The fastest gate delay; the same as $t_{pd}$
items.explanation: Frequency and period are reciprocals: a shorter minimum period means a higher maximum frequency, $f_{max} = 1/T_{clk,min}$. It is a clock speed, not a data value or a gate delay.

lessons.title: Hold Violations, Clock Skew, Jitter & Pipelining
lessons.content:
**Hook.** There is a second, sneakier timing problem that slowing the clock will *never* fix. Understanding it separates people who can debug real hardware from those who can't.

**Build.** The setup check (last lesson) worries that data arrives too *late* for the next edge. The **hold check** worries about the opposite: data arriving too *early* and racing through to the capturing flip-flop while it is still trying to hold the *current* value from this same edge. The condition is that the fastest the new value can arrive ($t_{cq,min} + t_{cd}$) must still be no sooner than the hold time:

$$t_{cq,min} + t_{cd} \ge t_{h}$$

Notice the clock period $T_{clk}$ is nowhere in this. A hold violation is a race against the *same* edge, so making the clock slower changes nothing. The fix is to *add* a little delay on the too-fast path (extra buffers), never to slow the clock.

**Clock skew** is when the clock edge reaches one flip-flop slightly later than another (the clock wires aren't the same length). **Jitter** is the clock edge wobbling earlier or later from tick to tick. Both eat into your timing margin and can turn a healthy design marginal.

**Pipelining** is the standard trick to go faster: take one long combinational path and break it into shorter stages with flip-flops between them, like adding rest stops on a long road. Each shorter stage allows a faster clock. The cost is that a result now takes several clock cycles to come out the end (more latency), but the chip processes more work per second (more throughput).

**Recap.** Hold violations are frequency-independent — fix them with delay, not a slower clock. Skew and jitter shrink your margins. Pipelining breaks a long path into stages so the clock can run faster.

items.prompt: Your design has a hold-time violation. Which fix will work?
choices: Slow down the clock (use a longer period)
Add a little delay (buffers) on the too-fast path
Speed up the clock
Remove the setup-time requirement
items.explanation: A hold violation is data racing through too fast against the same edge, so adding delay on that short path fixes it. The clock period does not appear in the hold condition, so changing clock speed has no effect.

items.prompt: Why does slowing the clock NOT fix a hold violation, even though it fixes setup violations?
choices: Because hold violations are imaginary
Because the hold condition $t_{cq,min}+t_{cd} \ge t_{h}$ contains no clock period $T_{clk}$ — it is a race against the same edge
Because slowing the clock also slows the data by the same amount
Because hold time gets larger when the clock slows down
items.explanation: Setup is about fitting work before the NEXT edge, so a longer period helps. Hold is about the SAME edge and has no $T_{clk}$ term, so a longer period changes nothing. The hold time itself is fixed by the device.

items.prompt: What is clock skew?
choices: The clock edge arriving at one flip-flop slightly later than at another
The clock changing frequency over time
The data arriving before the clock
A flip-flop that has stopped working
items.explanation: Skew is a difference in clock arrival time between flip-flops, usually because the clock wires have different lengths. A clock changing frequency or wobbling tick-to-tick is jitter, a separate idea.

items.prompt: What is clock jitter?
choices: A permanent difference in wire length between two clock paths
The clock edge arriving a little early or late, varying from one tick to the next
The data signal being too large
The flip-flop output appearing instantly
items.explanation: Jitter is the tick-to-tick wobble in when the clock edge actually arrives. A fixed, permanent offset between two clock paths is skew instead. Both reduce timing margin.

items.prompt: What does pipelining do to a long combinational path?
choices: It deletes the logic to make it shorter
It breaks the path into shorter stages with flip-flops between them so a faster clock can be used
It slows the clock to be safe
It removes all the flip-flops
items.explanation: Pipelining inserts registers to chop one long path into several short stages, like rest stops on a road. Each short stage permits a faster clock; it adds flip-flops rather than removing them.

items.prompt: Pipelining raises throughput but has a cost. What is that cost?
choices: The chip uses no power
Higher latency — a result now takes several clock cycles to come out the end
The clock must slow down
The data becomes incorrect
items.explanation: Splitting into stages means a value passes through several registers before emerging, so the delay from input to first result (latency) grows. In exchange, more results stream out per second (throughput). It does not corrupt data or force a slower clock.

items.prompt: A 12 ns combinational block is pipelined into 3 equal stages of 4 ns each (ignore the small extra delays). What clock period can the pipeline now run at?
choices: 12 ns (unchanged)
36 ns
About 4 ns
1.3 ns
items.explanation: Each stage is now about 4 ns, so the slowest stage — and thus the clock period — drops to roughly 4 ns. The end-to-end latency is still about 12 ns (3 cycles), but the clock is three times faster.

items.prompt: Setup violations and hold violations differ in one key way. Which statement is correct?
choices: Both are fixed by slowing the clock
Setup can be fixed by slowing the clock; hold cannot, because hold has no clock-period term
Hold can be fixed by slowing the clock; setup cannot
Neither has anything to do with the clock
items.explanation: Setup is about reaching the next edge in time, so a longer period helps. Hold is a same-edge race with no period in its condition, so a slower clock does nothing for it — you add path delay instead.

items.prompt: Engineers warn against 'gating the clock' (running it through an AND gate with an enable). Why?
choices: It makes the clock too fast
It adds uncontrolled skew and can create glitches on the clock; use a synchronous clock-enable on the flip-flop instead
It is the only way to fix hold violations
It increases the setup time of every flip-flop
items.explanation: Putting logic in the clock path delays and can glitch the clock, hurting timing. The clean approach is a clock-enable input on the flip-flop, which keeps the clock itself untouched.

lessons.title: Metastability, Synchronizers & Crossing Clock Domains
lessons.content:
**Hook.** What actually happens if you break the setup/hold rule? The flip-flop can get stuck, briefly, unable to decide whether its output is a 0 or a 1. This in-between state is called **metastability**, and it is the cause of many baffling 'it works most of the time' hardware bugs.

**Build.** Imagine trying to grab a spinning coin the instant it lands — for a moment it teeters on its edge before falling heads or tails. A flip-flop whose input changed right inside its setup/hold window can teeter the same way: its output hovers between voltage levels for an unpredictable time before finally settling on a random 0 or 1. It always resolves eventually, but you can't say exactly when.

When does this risk arise? Whenever you sample a signal that isn't lined up with your clock — for example, an input from a button, a sensor, or another part of the chip running on a *different* clock. Because that signal can change at any instant, sooner or later it will change right at the bad moment.

You can't eliminate metastability, only make it astronomically unlikely. The measure of how rare failures are is **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) — literally the average time you'd wait between one slip-through and the next; bigger is better.

The standard fix is the **two-flop synchronizer**: two flip-flops in a row on the receiving clock. The first one samples the wild signal and is *allowed* to go metastable; it then has a full clock cycle to settle before the second flip-flop reads it, so the second one almost always sees a clean 0 or 1.

Moving signals between two different clocks is called **clock-domain crossing (CDC)**. A two-flop synchronizer handles one bit. A whole bus of bits can't just use parallel synchronizers (the bits would resolve on different cycles, giving garbage); instead engineers use tricks like Gray-code counters or an asynchronous FIFO (a small dual-clock memory). One related detail: a reset signal is usually applied immediately but *released* in step with the clock, so flip-flops don't leave reset right at an edge and go metastable.

**Recap.** Sampling a signal that isn't aligned to your clock risks metastability — a brief undecided output. You make it rare (high MTBF) with a two-flop synchronizer, the cornerstone of safely crossing between clock domains.

items.prompt: What is metastability in a flip-flop?
choices: The output settling instantly to the correct value
The output hovering between 0 and 1 for an unpredictable time before settling on a random value
The clock running too fast
The flip-flop permanently breaking
items.explanation: Metastability is the brief, undecided in-between state, like a coin teetering on its edge, that follows a setup/hold violation. It always resolves eventually and doesn't permanently damage the part, but the timing and outcome are unpredictable.

items.prompt: When is a flip-flop most at risk of going metastable?
choices: When its input is generated by the same clock and always meets setup/hold
When it samples a signal that is NOT aligned to its clock, such as a button or another clock domain
When the clock is perfectly clean
When the output is never read
items.explanation: An unrelated signal can change at any instant, so eventually it changes right inside the setup/hold window. A signal that always respects setup/hold (same clock) is safe.

items.prompt: Why is the spinning-coin image a good picture of metastability?
choices: Because coins are made of metal
Because a coin can teeter on its edge before randomly falling heads or tails, just as the output teeters before settling to a random 0 or 1
Because a coin flip is always fair
Because coins are round like a clock face
items.explanation: The teetering, undecided moment and the random final result are the point of the analogy. The shape or material of the coin, and the fairness of the flip, are not relevant.

items.prompt: Can metastability be completely eliminated by good design?
choices: Yes, a fast enough clock removes it entirely
No, but it can be made astronomically unlikely (very high MTBF)
Yes, by using more flip-flops you reach zero risk
No, and nothing can be done to reduce it
items.explanation: Because it is a physical effect, it can never be driven to exactly zero — but a synchronizer makes the failure rate so low that it effectively never happens. Saying it can't be reduced at all is also wrong.

items.prompt: What does MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) tell you about a synchronizer?
choices: How fast the clock runs
The average time you would wait between metastability slip-throughs — bigger is better
How many flip-flops are on the chip
The voltage of the output
items.explanation: MTBF is the average spacing between rare failures; a larger MTBF means failures are more spread out and the design is safer. It is not a clock speed, a count, or a voltage.

items.prompt: In a two-flop synchronizer, what is the role of the FIRST flip-flop?
choices: It guarantees a clean output instantly
It samples the unaligned signal and is allowed to go metastable, then has a full clock cycle to settle
It generates the clock
It converts the signal to Gray code
items.explanation: The first flop is the one permitted to teeter; the design counts on it settling within one clock so the second flop reads a clean level. It does not produce an instantly clean output — that's what the second flop is for.

items.prompt: Why does the SECOND flip-flop in a two-flop synchronizer usually see a clean 0 or 1?
choices: Because it samples a full clock cycle later, after the first flop has almost certainly settled
Because it ignores the clock
Because it is faster than the first flop
Because it adds the two values together
items.explanation: The whole clock period between the two flops gives the first flop time to resolve any metastability, so the second flop captures a settled value. The second flop is an ordinary flop, not faster or special.

items.prompt: What is 'clock-domain crossing' (CDC)?
choices: Two clocks merging into one
Moving a signal from a part of the chip on one clock to a part on a different clock
Slowing a single clock down
Removing the clock entirely
items.explanation: CDC is exactly the act of passing a signal between two regions running on different clocks — the situation that creates metastability risk and calls for a synchronizer. It is not about merging or removing clocks.

items.prompt: Why can't you safely cross a whole multi-bit bus by putting a separate two-flop synchronizer on each bit?
choices: It would use too little power
Different bits could resolve on different cycles, producing a garbled in-between value
Two-flop synchronizers only work on the clock signal
Buses never cross clock domains
items.explanation: Each bit might settle a cycle apart, so the receiving side could briefly read a mix of old and new bits — an illegal value. That's why buses use Gray-code counters or an asynchronous FIFO instead.

items.prompt: A common reset strategy applies reset immediately but RELEASES it in step with the clock. Why release it synchronously?
choices: To make reset slower for no reason
So flip-flops don't leave reset right at a clock edge and go metastable
Because reset must always be ignored
To save wiring on the chip
items.explanation: Releasing reset in sync with the clock keeps all flip-flops from exiting reset at a bad moment near an edge, which would risk metastability — the same synchronizer reasoning applied to the reset line.

lessons.title: What Is Memory? Volatile vs Non-Volatile and the Hierarchy
lessons.content:
**Memory** is where a system stores **bits** (the 0s and 1s it works with) so it can *remember* data. You already met one tiny memory: a flip-flop holds a single bit. Real systems need to remember billions of bits, so we build big memories out of many cells.

**The first big idea: two families.** Some memory is **volatile** — it forgets everything the moment power is removed. Other memory is **non-volatile** — it keeps its data even with the power off. Here is the everyday hook: your computer's **RAM** is volatile working memory, while its **SSD** (or a USB flash drive) is non-volatile storage. That is why an *unsaved* document vanishes during a power cut, but *saved* files are still there when you reboot. A phone is the same: a little fast **working memory** that empties when off, and a big **storage** that keeps your photos.

**The second big idea: a hierarchy.** No single memory is both huge and lightning-fast and cheap. So computers stack several layers, trading speed against size and cost. At the top sit tiny, blazing-fast **registers**; below them a small fast **cache**; then larger **main memory** (RAM); and at the bottom big, slower **storage**. As you go down, memory gets **bigger and cheaper per bit but slower**. The top (volatile) layers do the fast day-to-day work; the bottom (non-volatile) layer keeps everything safe long-term.

**Recap:** memory stores bits. Volatile memory is fast working memory that forgets when powered off; non-volatile memory is slower storage that remembers. Real systems layer them into a hierarchy: fast-and-small near the processor, slow-and-big for storage.

items.prompt: What is the single most important difference between volatile and non-volatile memory?
choices: Volatile memory loses its data when power is removed; non-volatile memory keeps its data without power
Volatile memory is always smaller than non-volatile memory
Volatile memory stores letters and non-volatile memory stores numbers
Volatile memory is non-volatile memory that has been turned off
items.explanation: The defining split is power behavior: volatile memory (like RAM) forgets its contents when power is cut, while non-volatile memory (like flash) retains data even with the power off. Size and cost differences exist but follow from this; both can store any kind of bits.

items.prompt: You are typing a document but have not saved it. A power cut hits. Why is your typing lost while your previously saved files survive?
choices: The unsaved text lived only in volatile working memory (RAM), which forgets on power loss; saved files were written to non-volatile storage
Saved files are smaller, so they are easier to keep
The power cut erased the storage but not the RAM
Unsaved text is stored in the cache, which is non-volatile
items.explanation: While you type, the document sits in fast volatile RAM. RAM loses everything when power drops, so the unsaved work is gone. Saving copies the data into non-volatile storage, which keeps it through a power cut. This is exactly why 'save often' matters.

items.prompt: In a computer, which pairing correctly matches a part to its memory family?
choices: RAM = volatile working memory; SSD/flash drive = non-volatile storage
RAM = non-volatile storage; SSD = volatile working memory
Both RAM and SSD are volatile
Both RAM and SSD are non-volatile
items.explanation: RAM is the fast working memory that empties when the machine is off (volatile). The SSD or USB flash drive is the long-term storage that keeps files without power (non-volatile). Swapping them is the common beginner mix-up.

items.prompt: As you move DOWN the memory hierarchy (from registers toward storage), what generally happens?
choices: Memory gets bigger and cheaper per bit, but slower
Memory gets smaller, faster, and cheaper
Memory gets faster and more expensive per bit
Nothing changes except the color of the chip
items.explanation: The hierarchy is a deliberate trade-off. Lower levels (main memory, storage) hold far more data at lower cost per bit but respond more slowly. Upper levels (registers, cache) are tiny and costly per bit but extremely fast. You cannot get huge, fast, and cheap all at once.

items.prompt: Why do computers bother with a hierarchy of several memory types instead of using just one kind?
choices: No single technology is simultaneously huge, very fast, and cheap, so layers trade speed against size and cost
Engineers like making things complicated
Each program legally requires four memory types
Volatile memory cannot be read without non-volatile memory present
items.explanation: A fast memory technology is expensive and small; a cheap, huge one is slow. By layering them — a little fast memory near the processor backed by lots of slow cheap memory — a system gets most of the speed of the fast layer with most of the capacity of the slow one.

items.prompt: A flip-flop from an earlier lesson is best described as:
