items.explanation: Flash wins on speed, sigma-delta wins on resolution (via oversampling), and SAR is the balanced middle-ground workhorse. Match the converter to what the job most needs.

lessons.title: The Clock: A Digital System's Heartbeat
lessons.content:
## Hook

Imagine a band playing together. If everyone keeps their own time, the music falls apart. A **drummer** sets a steady beat so the whole band stays in step. A digital system needs the same thing: a steady, regular **tick** called the **clock**.

## Build

In earlier lessons you saw that a **flip-flop** (a one-bit memory cell) only updates on a clock **edge** — the instant the clock signal jumps from low to high. The clock is just a signal that goes up, down, up, down, over and over, forever: **HIGH, LOW, HIGH, LOW**. Each full up-and-down is one **cycle** (one tick).

We measure how fast it ticks with **frequency**, in **hertz (Hz)** — ticks per second. One million ticks per second is a **megahertz (MHz)**; one billion is a **gigahertz (GHz)**. A typical small chip might run at 25 MHz (25 million ticks every second).

The time for one tick is the **period**. Period and frequency are opposites: $T = 1/f$. At 25 MHz, one tick lasts $1 / 25{,}000{,}000 = 40$ nanoseconds (40 billionths of a second).

Why does the clock need to be *steady*? Because every flip-flop in the chip acts on the same beat. If the beat speeds up and slows down randomly, parts of the chip fall out of step and data gets corrupted — just like a band losing the drummer.

## Recap

The clock is the drummer for a digital system: a steady on/off tick that keeps every part in step. Frequency (Hz) counts ticks per second; the period is the time for one tick.

items.prompt: Study this diagram of a clock signal.
choices: It is a steady square wave that repeats HIGH then LOW, over and over
It is a single pulse that happens once and stops
It is a random noise signal with no pattern
It is a slowly rising voltage that never comes back down
items.explanation: A clock is a repeating square wave: HIGH, LOW, HIGH, LOW, with equal-length periods. It is not a one-time pulse, not random, and not a slow ramp — its whole job is to give a regular, repeating beat.

items.prompt: In the band analogy, what does the clock play the role of?
choices: The singer who improvises freely
The drummer who sets a steady beat to keep everyone in step
The audience that listens
The lyrics of the song
items.explanation: The clock is like the drummer: it provides a steady, regular beat so every part of the system acts together. A singer who improvises (no fixed timing) is the opposite of what a clock does.

items.prompt: What does the frequency of a clock tell you?
choices: How many volts the signal swings between
How many ticks (cycles) happen per second
How long the wires are inside the chip
How much power the chip uses
items.explanation: Frequency, measured in hertz (Hz), counts ticks per second. 25 MHz means 25 million ticks every second. Voltage swing, wire length, and power are separate things.

items.prompt: A clock runs at 1 MHz. How many ticks is that per second?
choices: One thousand
One million
One billion
One hundred
items.explanation: The prefix 'mega' means one million, so 1 MHz = one million ticks per second. 'Kilo' is a thousand and 'giga' is a billion.

items.prompt: The clock period is the time for one tick. How is it related to frequency?
choices: Period equals frequency (they are the same number)
Period is one divided by frequency ($T = 1/f$)
Period is frequency times two
Period has nothing to do with frequency
items.explanation: Period and frequency are opposites: $T = 1/f$. A higher frequency means more ticks per second, so each tick (the period) is shorter.

items.prompt: A clock runs at 25 MHz. About how long is one tick (its period)?
choices: 40 nanoseconds
25 seconds
40 seconds
1 millisecond
items.explanation: Period $T = 1/f = 1 / 25{,}000{,}000 = 40$ nanoseconds (40 billionths of a second). Because the frequency is very high, each tick is extremely short.

items.prompt: Why is it important that the clock ticks at a STEADY, regular rate?
choices: So the chip looks nicer on an oscilloscope
So every flip-flop acts on the same beat and stays in step; an unsteady beat corrupts data
Steadiness lowers the cost of the chip
It is not important; the rate can wander freely
items.explanation: Every flip-flop updates on the shared clock edge. If the beat wanders, parts of the chip fall out of step and data is lost or corrupted — like a band losing its drummer. A steady beat keeps everything coordinated.

items.prompt: On which part of the clock signal does an edge-triggered flip-flop update its stored value?
choices: Whenever the clock is sitting at HIGH
On the clock edge, when the signal jumps from low to high
Only once when the chip is first powered on
Continuously, at every instant
items.explanation: An edge-triggered flip-flop captures its input on the rising edge — the instant the clock jumps from low to high. It does not respond to the steady level or update continuously; the edge is the 'beat' it acts on.

lessons.title: Where the Tick Comes From: Crystal Oscillators
lessons.content:
## Hook

The clock has to come from *somewhere*. A quartz watch keeps near-perfect time for years on a tiny battery. The secret is a **quartz crystal** — and the very same trick gives computers their steady clock.

## Build

A circuit that makes a repeating signal all by itself is an **oscillator**. The question is: how steady and accurate is it?

A **crystal oscillator** uses a tiny sliver of **quartz**. Quartz is **piezoelectric**: squeeze it and it makes a little voltage; apply a voltage and it physically flexes. A quartz crystal vibrates at one very precise frequency, like a tuning fork that always rings the same note. Because that mechanical vibration is so sharp and stable, the clock built from it is **extremely steady and accurate** — that is why quartz watches and computers keep good time.

We describe accuracy in **parts per million (ppm)** — how far the frequency can drift out of every million. A $\pm 20$ ppm crystal can be off by at most 20 parts in a million, which is excellent: at 25 MHz that is only about 500 Hz of wander.

Not every clock needs a crystal. Cheaper, looser options exist:

- **Ceramic resonator** — like a crystal but cheaper and less accurate (hundreds of ppm).
- **RC oscillator** — built from a resistor (R) and capacitor (C), often already inside a chip. It needs no extra parts but drifts a lot with temperature and voltage. Fine for blinking an LED; bad for precise timing.
- **MEMS oscillator** — a tiny vibrating beam carved out of silicon. Rugged and shock-resistant, programmable, but historically a bit noisier than quartz.

For extra-stable clocks, two upgraded crystals exist: a **TCXO** (temperature-compensated) corrects for temperature change, and an **OCXO** keeps the crystal in a tiny heated oven so its temperature never changes at all — the most stable of all.

## Recap

An oscillator makes the tick. A quartz crystal makes an exceptionally steady, accurate one (measured in ppm) — which is why it powers watches and computers. RC, ceramic, and MEMS sources trade accuracy for cost or ruggedness.

items.prompt: What is an oscillator?
choices: A circuit that stores one bit of data
A circuit that makes a repeating (oscillating) signal by itself
A circuit that only amplifies an incoming signal
A circuit that converts light into electricity
items.explanation: An oscillator generates a repeating signal on its own — that repeating signal becomes the clock tick. Storing a bit is a flip-flop's job; an amplifier only boosts an existing signal.

items.prompt: Why does a quartz crystal make such a steady, accurate clock?
choices: Quartz is the cheapest material available
Quartz vibrates at one very precise, stable frequency, like a tuning fork that always rings the same note
Quartz glows when current passes through it
Quartz can store a large amount of charge
items.explanation: Quartz is piezoelectric and vibrates at one sharp, stable mechanical frequency — like a tuning fork. That precise vibration sets the clock frequency very tightly, which is why quartz watches and computers keep accurate time.

items.prompt: An everyday device famous for keeping accurate time with a quartz crystal is:
choices: A wind-up mechanical clock with gears only
A quartz wristwatch
A sundial
An hourglass
items.explanation: A quartz wristwatch uses a vibrating quartz crystal as its timekeeper, which is why it stays accurate for years. Sundials, hourglasses, and gear-only clocks have no crystal and are far less precise.

items.prompt: What does 'parts per million (ppm)' describe for a clock?
choices: How many transistors the oscillator contains
How far the frequency may drift, measured in parts out of every million
How many millimeters wide the crystal is
How much current the oscillator draws
items.explanation: ppm measures accuracy as parts out of a million. A 20 ppm clock can be off by at most 20 parts in a million — a small fraction. It is a measure of frequency error, not size, current, or transistor count.

items.prompt: WHY would you pick a crystal oscillator over a simple RC oscillator for a clock that must keep accurate time?
choices: The RC oscillator uses too many external parts
The crystal is far more stable and accurate; an RC oscillator drifts a lot with temperature and voltage
The crystal is always cheaper than an RC oscillator
Only a crystal can produce a square wave
items.explanation: The key reason is accuracy: a crystal's mechanical resonance holds the frequency very tightly (tens of ppm), while an RC oscillator drifts a lot as temperature and voltage change. RC needs FEWER parts (often none external) and is usually cheaper — but that is exactly why it is less accurate. Both can make a square wave.

items.prompt: Which clock source needs no extra external parts but drifts the most with temperature and voltage?
choices: A quartz crystal
An RC oscillator built into the chip
An OCXO
A ceramic resonator
items.explanation: An RC (resistor-capacitor) oscillator is often built into the chip and needs no external parts, but its frequency wanders a lot with temperature and supply voltage. Crystals, ceramic resonators, and especially OCXOs are far more stable.

items.prompt: A MEMS oscillator's main advantage over a quartz crystal is that it is:
choices: Always perfectly noise-free
Made of a tiny vibrating silicon beam, making it rugged and shock-resistant
Powered without any electricity
Guaranteed to be more accurate than any crystal
items.explanation: A MEMS oscillator uses a tiny silicon beam, making it rugged against shock and vibration (and it is programmable). It is not noise-free — historically it is a bit noisier than quartz — and not automatically more accurate.

items.prompt: How does an OCXO achieve even better stability than a plain crystal?
choices: It uses two crystals wired in parallel
It keeps the crystal in a tiny heated oven so its temperature never changes
It replaces the quartz with plastic
It runs the crystal at a much lower frequency
items.explanation: An OCXO (oven-controlled crystal oscillator) holds the crystal at a constant temperature in a small oven, so temperature drift is nearly eliminated — giving the best stability. A TCXO instead compensates for temperature changes. Neither uses plastic or parallel crystals to do this.

items.prompt: Rank these from LEAST accurate to MOST accurate.
choices: OCXO, crystal, ceramic resonator, RC oscillator
RC oscillator, ceramic resonator, crystal, OCXO
Crystal, RC oscillator, OCXO, ceramic resonator
Ceramic resonator, OCXO, RC oscillator, crystal
items.explanation: From loosest to tightest: an RC oscillator drifts the most, a ceramic resonator is better (hundreds of ppm), a plain crystal is better still (tens of ppm), and an OCXO (oven-controlled crystal) is the most stable of all.

lessons.title: Delivering the Tick: Clock Distribution & Skew
lessons.content:
## Hook

A drummer in a huge stadium has a problem: sound takes time to travel, so musicians far away hear the beat slightly late and drift out of time. A chip has the exact same problem — the clock tick has to reach thousands of flip-flops, and it cannot arrive everywhere at once.

## Build

One oscillator makes the clock. But inside a chip that single tick must be copied and carried over wires to every flip-flop. Sending one signal out to many destinations is called **clock distribution**.

The trouble is that signals take a tiny amount of time to travel down a wire. If one flip-flop sits at the end of a short wire and another at the end of a long wire, they receive the same edge at slightly different moments. That arrival-time difference is called **clock skew**.

Skew is a problem because the whole point of a clock is to make everything act *together*. If flip-flop A gets the tick noticeably before flip-flop B, data passing between them can arrive too early or too late and be captured wrong. Earlier you saw that flip-flops have **setup** and **hold** timing rules; skew eats directly into the margin those rules give you.

The fix is to make every path the same length. Engineers use a balanced **clock tree** — often shaped like the letter **H** nested inside itself (an **H-tree**) — so the wire from the source to *every* flip-flop is equally long. Special **clock buffers** (drivers built just for clocks) boost the signal along the way so it stays sharp. Equal paths mean the edge reaches everyone at nearly the same instant: low skew.

## Recap

Distribution carries one clock to many flip-flops. Because wires have delay, edges can arrive at different times — that is skew, and it steals from the setup/hold budget. Balanced clock trees and dedicated buffers keep skew low so everything stays in step.

items.prompt: What does 'clock distribution' mean?
choices: Choosing what frequency the oscillator runs at
Carrying the one clock signal out to all the flip-flops that need it
Turning the clock off to save power
Converting the clock from analog to digital
items.explanation: Distribution is the job of copying and carrying one clock to the many flip-flops across a chip. Picking the frequency is the oscillator/PLL's job, not distribution.

items.prompt: What is clock skew?
choices: The difference in arrival time of the clock edge at different flip-flops
The number of flip-flops in the design
The voltage of the clock signal
The temperature of the crystal
items.explanation: Skew is the difference in WHEN the clock edge arrives at different points, caused by signals taking time to travel down wires of different lengths. It is not about voltage, temperature, or count.

items.prompt: Why does the clock edge NOT reach every flip-flop at the exact same instant?
choices: Flip-flops randomly ignore some edges
Signals take a tiny amount of time to travel down a wire, and wires have different lengths
The oscillator changes frequency for each flip-flop
Each flip-flop has its own separate crystal
items.explanation: A signal takes a small but real time to travel along a wire. Flip-flops at the ends of different-length wires get the edge at slightly different moments. One oscillator feeds them all; the delay comes from the wiring.

items.prompt: Why is clock skew a problem?
choices: It uses up the chip's memory
If the edge reaches flip-flops at different times, data between them can be captured at the wrong moment
It makes the clock frequency higher than intended
It is never a problem and can be ignored
items.explanation: The clock's purpose is to make everything act together. If one flip-flop ticks noticeably before another, data passing between them can land too early or too late and be captured incorrectly. Skew steals from the setup/hold timing margin.

items.prompt: Study this diagram of one crystal feeding many flip-flops. What is the balanced H-tree for?
choices: To make the clock run at a higher frequency
To make every path from the source to a flip-flop the same length, so the edge arrives at nearly the same time everywhere
To store data inside the clock tree
To convert the clock into a sine wave
items.explanation: An H-tree equalizes the wire length from the source to every flip-flop, so each one gets the edge at almost the same instant — keeping skew low. It does not change frequency or store data.

items.prompt: What is a clock buffer's job in a distribution network?
choices: To store the clock value for later
To drive and boost the clock signal so it stays strong and sharp as it travels to many destinations
To slow the clock down on purpose
To convert the clock to a different voltage standard for the outside world
items.explanation: A clock buffer is a driver that strengthens the clock so it can reach many flip-flops without weakening or smearing. Its job is to deliver a strong, clean edge, not to store or deliberately slow the clock.

items.prompt: The stadium-drummer analogy illustrates skew because:
choices: The drummer plays different songs for different people
Sound takes time to travel, so distant musicians hear the same beat slightly late and drift out of time
The drummer changes tempo constantly
The musicians cannot see the drummer
items.explanation: Sound travels at a finite speed, so a beat reaches far-off players later than near ones — they get the same beat at different times, just like clock edges arriving at flip-flops at different times. That arrival-time spread is skew.

items.prompt: Which approach keeps clock skew LOW across a chip?
choices: Use one short wire to the nearest flip-flop and long random wires to the rest
Use a balanced tree (like an H-tree) so every path is the same length, plus dedicated clock buffers
Use a different oscillator for every flip-flop
Send the clock through the slowest, longest possible path
items.explanation: Equal-length paths mean the edge reaches every flip-flop at nearly the same time, minimizing skew; a balanced H-tree with clock buffers does exactly this. Random or deliberately long paths make skew worse, and separate oscillators would not even be in step.

lessons.title: When the Tick Wobbles: Jitter & Phase Noise
lessons.content:
## Hook

Even a great drummer is not a perfect machine — each beat lands a hair early or late. A real clock is the same: its edges never arrive *exactly* on time. That tiny timing wobble has a name: **jitter**.

## Build

Imagine the ideal clock edges as evenly spaced marks on a ruler. A real clock's edges land *close* to those marks but each one is slightly off — a little early or a little late. **Jitter** is that timing error: how far the edges wander from where they should be.

There are a few ways to describe it:

- **Period jitter** — how much a single tick's length varies from the ideal.
- **Cycle-to-cycle jitter** — how much one tick differs from the very next tick.
- **Long-term jitter** — how far off the timing has drifted after many ticks.

Less jitter means a cleaner, more trustworthy clock. Why care? Some circuits use the clock edge to *measure* time. For example, an **analog-to-digital converter** (a circuit that samples a real-world signal) grabs a value at each clock edge. If the edge wobbles, it samples at the wrong instant and adds error — so a noisy clock directly hurts how cleanly that signal is captured.

There is a second way to describe the very same imperfection. Instead of looking at edges wobbling in *time*, you can look at the clock's *frequency*. A perfect clock would be one pure frequency; a real one has a little fuzzy spread of nearby frequencies around it. That spread is called **phase noise**. Jitter (time view) and phase noise (frequency view) are two descriptions of the same wobble — keep this conceptual for now: low phase noise means low jitter.

## Recap

Real clocks wobble. Jitter is how far the edges drift from their ideal times (period, cycle-to-cycle, long-term). Phase noise is the same wobble seen as a fuzzy spread in frequency. Less jitter and lower phase noise mean a cleaner clock and cleaner sampling.

items.prompt: Study this comparison of a clean clock and a jittery clock. What does the jittery one show?
choices: Edges that land exactly on the ideal time marks every cycle
Edges that arrive a little early or late, drifting off the ideal time marks
A signal that never changes from HIGH
A clock running at a completely different frequency
items.explanation: The jittery clock's edges wobble — arriving slightly before or after the dashed ideal-time marks. The clean clock's edges land on the marks. Jitter is exactly this drift of edges off their ideal positions.

items.prompt: What is clock jitter?
choices: The voltage level of the clock
The tiny timing error in when each clock edge arrives, compared to its ideal time
The number of flip-flops driven by the clock
The cost of the oscillator
items.explanation: Jitter is the small timing wobble — how far each edge arrives from where it ideally should. It is a timing imperfection, not a voltage, a count, or a price.

items.prompt: In the drummer analogy, jitter is like:
choices: The drummer playing a completely different song
Each beat landing a hair early or late instead of perfectly on time
The drummer stopping entirely
The drummer getting louder over time
items.explanation: Even a skilled drummer isn't a perfect metronome; beats land slightly early or late. That small timing scatter is exactly what jitter is for a clock.

items.prompt: Cycle-to-cycle jitter specifically measures:
choices: How much one tick differs from the very next tick
The total drift after a million ticks
The clock's average frequency
How many volts the clock swings
items.explanation: Cycle-to-cycle jitter compares one period to the immediately following period — the change from one tick to the next. Drift built up over many ticks is long-term jitter; average frequency and voltage are different measures.

items.prompt: Why does clock jitter hurt an analog-to-digital converter (a circuit that samples a signal at each clock edge)?
choices: It increases the converter's supply voltage
A wobbling edge samples the signal at the wrong instant, adding error to the captured value
Jitter makes the converter run cooler
Jitter has no effect on sampling
items.explanation: The converter grabs a value exactly at the clock edge. If the edge arrives early or late (jitter), it samples at the wrong moment and the captured value is off — so a noisy clock directly degrades how cleanly the signal is captured.

items.prompt: What is phase noise?
choices: The same timing wobble as jitter, but described as a fuzzy spread of frequencies around the clock instead of edges moving in time
Audible noise the chip makes
The number of phases in a multi-phase clock
A complete loss of the clock signal
items.explanation: Phase noise is the frequency-domain view of the very same imperfection that jitter describes in the time domain: a perfect clock would be one pure frequency, but a real one has a small fuzzy spread around it. Low phase noise means low jitter.

items.prompt: How are jitter and phase noise related?
choices: They are unrelated; one is about voltage and one is about current
They are two ways of describing the same clock imperfection — jitter in time, phase noise in frequency
Jitter only affects crystals; phase noise only affects RC oscillators
Phase noise is always good while jitter is always bad
items.explanation: Jitter (edges wobbling in time) and phase noise (a fuzzy spread of frequencies) are two views of the same underlying wobble. A clock with low phase noise also has low jitter.

items.prompt: Between two clocks, which is the cleaner, more trustworthy one?
choices: The one with MORE jitter, because its edges move around more
The one with LESS jitter, because its edges land closer to their ideal times
They are equally good regardless of jitter
The one with the larger voltage swing, regardless of jitter
items.explanation: Less jitter means edges arrive closer to where they should, giving cleaner, more reliable timing and cleaner sampling. More jitter is worse. Voltage swing is a separate matter from timing quality.

lessons.title: The Frequency Factory: PLLs
lessons.content:
## Hook

A chip often has just one crystal — say 25 MHz — but its different parts need different speeds: maybe 100 MHz for the processor and 48 MHz for the USB port. How do you get many frequencies from one crystal? With a clever circuit called a **PLL**.

## Build

**PLL** stands for **phase-locked loop**. Its job is to take one steady reference clock and produce a new clock at a different frequency, usually a multiple of the reference — and keep it perfectly in step (**locked**) with that reference.

Think of tuning an old radio. You turn the knob until the station comes in clearly, then it 'locks on' and stays there. A PLL does this automatically and electronically.

A PLL is a loop of four parts working together:

1. **Phase detector** — compares the reference clock with the PLL's own fed-back clock and asks: are we ahead or behind?
2. **Loop filter** — smooths that comparison into a steady control voltage (a gentle 'speed up' or 'slow down' command).
3. **VCO** (voltage-controlled oscillator) — an oscillator whose frequency is set by that control voltage. More voltage, faster; less voltage, slower.
4. **Divider** — divides the VCO's fast output down, then feeds it back to the phase detector for comparison.

Here is the trick. Suppose we want **100 MHz out of a 25 MHz reference** — a multiply by 4. We put a **divide-by-4** in the feedback path. The loop keeps adjusting the VCO until the divided-down feedback (VCO ÷ 4) exactly matches the 25 MHz reference. If VCO ÷ 4 = 25 MHz, then the VCO must be running at **100 MHz**. The loop has multiplied the frequency by 4. Change the divider, get a different multiple — that is **frequency synthesis**. Dividing the reference too lets you make non-integer ratios.

When the fed-back clock and the reference line up and stop drifting apart, the PLL is **locked**.

## Recap

A PLL turns one reference clock into another frequency and locks onto it, like tuning a radio. Phase detector, loop filter, VCO, and a feedback divider form a loop; the divider value sets the multiplication — a divide-by-4 in the feedback turns 25 MHz into 100 MHz.

items.prompt: What does a PLL (phase-locked loop) do?
choices: Stores many bits of data
Takes one reference clock and produces a new clock at a different frequency, locked in step with the reference
Converts a clock into a steady DC voltage
Removes the clock entirely to save power
items.explanation: A PLL generates a new frequency from a reference clock and keeps it locked (in step) with that reference. It does not store data, produce DC, or remove the clock.

items.prompt: Why is a PLL useful when a chip has only one crystal?
choices: It makes the crystal more accurate
It can create the several different frequencies the chip's parts need from that one crystal
It replaces the need for any flip-flops
It cools the crystal down
items.explanation: Different parts of a chip need different speeds. A PLL takes the one crystal's frequency and multiplies/divides it to make the various clocks needed, so you do not need a separate crystal for each.

items.prompt: Study this PLL block diagram. What is the role of the divide-by-4 block in the feedback path?
choices: It slows the final output clock down to 25 MHz before it leaves the chip
It divides the VCO output so the loop can compare it against the 25 MHz reference; matching them forces the VCO to 100 MHz
It stores the previous clock value
It converts the clock from digital to analog
items.explanation: The feedback divider takes the VCO's output and divides it (by 4 here) so it can be compared to the reference. The loop pushes VCO÷4 to equal 25 MHz, which forces the VCO itself to 100 MHz. The output stays at 100 MHz; only the feedback copy is divided.

items.prompt: A PLL has a 25 MHz reference and a divide-by-4 in its feedback path. What output frequency does it produce?
choices: 6.25 MHz
25 MHz
100 MHz
29 MHz
items.explanation: The loop forces (output ÷ 4) to equal the 25 MHz reference. So output = 25 × 4 = 100 MHz. The PLL multiplied the reference by 4. (6.25 MHz would be 25 ÷ 4, the wrong direction.)

items.prompt: Which part of the PLL is an oscillator whose frequency is set by a control voltage?
choices: The phase detector
The loop filter
The VCO (voltage-controlled oscillator)
The feedback divider
items.explanation: The VCO is the voltage-controlled oscillator: more control voltage makes it run faster, less makes it slower. The phase detector compares, the loop filter smooths, and the divider scales the feedback.

items.prompt: What does the phase detector in a PLL do?
choices: It compares the reference clock with the fed-back clock to see if the PLL is running ahead or behind
It sets the output voltage of the chip
It stores the clock frequency in memory
It generates the crystal's vibration
items.explanation: The phase detector compares the reference against the fed-back clock and reports whether the loop is ahead or behind, so the rest of the loop can correct. It does not store data or drive the crystal.

items.prompt: Generating a needed frequency by multiplying/dividing a reference with a PLL is called:
choices: Frequency synthesis
Clock gating
Quantization
Decoupling
items.explanation: Producing a desired frequency from a reference by choosing divider values is frequency synthesis. Clock gating (turning a clock on/off), quantization (rounding a sampled value), and decoupling (power-supply filtering) are unrelated ideas.

items.prompt: What does it mean for a PLL to be 'locked'?
choices: The PLL has been physically sealed shut
The fed-back clock and the reference line up and stop drifting apart, like a radio tuned exactly onto a station
The output clock has been switched off
The crystal has stopped vibrating
items.explanation: Lock means the loop has settled: the fed-back clock matches the reference and stays there, just like a radio that has locked onto a station. It is a state of the loop, not a physical seal or a powered-off clock.

items.prompt: Tuning an old radio until a station comes in clearly and 'locks on' is a good analogy for which part of a PLL's behavior?
choices: Storing data in flip-flops
Automatically adjusting until the output matches the target and then staying locked
Converting analog signals to digital
Distributing the clock to many flip-flops
items.explanation: Just as you turn the radio knob until the station is clear and it holds, a PLL adjusts the VCO until the feedback matches the reference and then stays locked. The other options describe different circuits entirely.

lessons.title: Putting It Together: On-Chip Clock Generation
lessons.content:
## Hook

Let's connect the whole story. A modern chip starts with a single crystal on the board and ends up with several clean, fast internal clocks reaching thousands of flip-flops — all generated on-chip. Here is how the pieces fit.

## Build

The journey of a clock inside a chip:

1. **One crystal** outside the chip provides a steady, accurate reference — say 25 MHz (Lesson 2).
2. That reference enters the chip and feeds one or more **PLLs**, which multiply and divide it into the various frequencies different blocks need — 100 MHz here, 48 MHz there (Lesson 5). This is **on-chip clock generation**: one crystal in, many internal clocks out.
3. Each generated clock is handed to a **clock distribution** network — a balanced tree with buffers — so it reaches every flip-flop with low **skew** (Lesson 3).
4. Along the way, every stage adds a little **jitter**, so designers pick low-noise PLLs and clean references where timing matters most, such as for a converter's sampling clock (Lesson 4).

In programmable chips called **FPGAs**, this clocking hardware is built in and ready to use: dedicated clock input pins, special low-skew distribution lines, and built-in PLL blocks you configure to synthesize the frequencies you want. You do not wire it from scratch — you set it up.

One more real-world trick: **spread-spectrum clocking**. A perfectly steady clock concentrates its energy at one frequency, which can radiate as **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** — radio-style noise that bothers other electronics. Spread-spectrum clocking deliberately wiggles the clock frequency by a tiny amount so its energy is smeared over a small band instead of one sharp spike, lowering the peak interference. The catch: that intentional wiggle is a form of jitter, so you avoid it on clocks that must be ultra-clean (like a converter's sampling clock).

## Recap

A chip turns one crystal into many internal clocks: crystal → PLL (synthesis) → distribution tree (low skew) → flip-flops, with jitter managed throughout. FPGAs provide this clocking hardware ready to configure, and spread-spectrum clocking trades a little jitter to reduce EMI.

items.prompt: In a modern chip, what is the usual order of the clock's journey?
choices: Distribution tree, then crystal, then PLL, then flip-flops
Crystal reference, then PLL to synthesize frequencies, then distribution tree, then flip-flops
Flip-flops, then PLL, then crystal
PLL first, then crystal, then distribution
items.explanation: The clock starts at the crystal (reference), gets multiplied/divided by a PLL into the needed frequencies, is carried by a distribution tree with low skew, and finally reaches the flip-flops.

items.prompt: What does 'on-chip clock generation' mean?
choices: Using a separate crystal for every clock the chip needs
Taking one crystal reference and generating the several internal clocks the chip needs, on the chip itself
Generating clocks only outside the chip on the circuit board
Turning all clocks off to save power
items.explanation: On-chip clock generation means one crystal comes in and the chip's internal circuitry (PLLs and dividers) produces the various internal clocks. It avoids needing a separate crystal for each frequency.

items.prompt: On an FPGA (a programmable chip), the clocking hardware (clock pins, low-skew lines, PLL blocks) is:
choices: Something you must build yourself from individual transistors
Built into the chip and ready for you to configure
Not available at all; FPGAs have no clocks
Located only on a separate companion chip
items.explanation: FPGAs include dedicated clock input pins, special low-skew distribution lines, and built-in PLL blocks. You configure them rather than wiring them from scratch.

items.prompt: What is spread-spectrum clocking?
choices: Running the clock at the highest possible frequency
Deliberately wiggling the clock frequency by a tiny amount to smear its energy over a small band and reduce peak EMI
Splitting the clock into many separate crystals
Removing all jitter from the clock
items.explanation: Spread-spectrum clocking intentionally varies the frequency slightly so the clock's energy is spread over a band instead of one sharp spike, lowering peak electromagnetic interference (EMI).

items.prompt: What is the downside of spread-spectrum clocking?
choices: It uses far more power than a normal clock
The intentional frequency wiggle is a form of jitter, so it should be avoided on clocks that must be ultra-clean
It makes the chip physically larger
It permanently lowers the clock frequency by half
items.explanation: The deliberate wiggling adds jitter. That is fine for reducing EMI on ordinary clocks, but you avoid spread-spectrum on clocks that must be very clean, such as a converter's sampling clock.

items.prompt: EMI (electromagnetic interference) that spread-spectrum clocking helps reduce is best described as:
choices: Heat produced by the chip
Radio-style noise radiated by the chip that can disturb other electronics
Errors stored in the chip's memory
The cost of the crystal
items.explanation: EMI is unwanted electromagnetic (radio-style) energy a circuit radiates, which can interfere with nearby electronics. A sharp, single-frequency clock radiates a strong spike; spreading it lowers that peak.

items.prompt: A chip has one 25 MHz crystal but needs both a 100 MHz processor clock and a 48 MHz USB clock. The cleanest way to get both is to:
choices: Add two more crystals, one per frequency
Feed the 25 MHz reference into PLL(s) on the chip to synthesize both 100 MHz and 48 MHz
Run everything at 25 MHz and ignore the other frequencies
Use spread-spectrum clocking to create the frequencies
items.explanation: PLLs multiply and divide the single reference into whatever frequencies the blocks need, so one crystal can serve many clocks. Adding crystals is wasteful, running everything at 25 MHz ignores the requirement, and spread-spectrum does not synthesize new target frequencies.

items.prompt: Why do designers prefer a low-noise PLL and a clean reference for a converter's sampling clock specifically?
choices: Because the converter draws less power that way
Because jitter on the sampling clock directly adds error to the sampled signal, so a cleaner clock means cleaner sampling
Because the converter cannot work above 25 MHz
Because clean clocks are always cheaper
items.explanation: A converter samples at each clock edge, so any jitter makes it sample at the wrong instant and adds error (Lesson 4). A low-noise PLL and clean reference minimize jitter, giving the cleanest capture. This is unrelated to power or cost.

lessons.title: What an HDL Is (and Isn't): Hardware, Not Software
lessons.content:
## HDL describes hardware, not a program

The single biggest mental reset for a software developer: **HDL describes structure, not steps**. When you write Verilog, you are drawing a *parallel* schematic in text — every statement at module scope is "alive" simultaneously, like wires soldered on a board. There is no program counter, no top-to-bottom execution. A C `for` loop iterates over time; an HDL construct *unrolls into hardware* that all exists at once.

Two HDLs dominate. **Verilog** (and its modern superset **SystemVerilog**) is C-flavored and is the default in commercial chip and FPGA work. **VHDL** is Ada-flavored, verbose, and strongly typed; it is entrenched in defense, aerospace, and radar shops, so reading literacy pays off **[->08]**. We focus on Verilog and read VHDL.

A design is a tree of **modules**. Each module has a name, a port list (its pins), and a body that connects sub-modules and logic:

```verilog
module and_gate(input a, input b, output y);
  assign y = a & b;   // continuous: y tracks a&b forever
endmodule
```

That `assign` is not "run once." It is a permanent connection — change `a` or `b` and `y` updates after a propagation delay, exactly like a real gate. The whole flow mirrors test-driven software you already know: **simulate, then synthesize**. Simulation runs your HDL as an event model to check behavior; synthesis maps the *synthesizable subset* onto real LUTs and flip-flops. Keeping those two worlds straight is the theme of this entire sub-topic.

items.front: How does HDL execution differ from a C program?
items.back: It does not execute step-by-step. HDL describes parallel hardware: every module-scope statement is concurrently 'live,' like wires on a board. There is no program counter.

items.front: What are the two dominant HDLs and where does each prevail?
items.back: Verilog/SystemVerilog: C-flavored, the commercial chip/FPGA default. VHDL: Ada-flavored, strongly typed, entrenched in defense/aerospace/radar.
items.hint: One is C-like, one is Ada-like.

items.front: What is a Verilog `module`?
items.back: The fundamental design unit: a named block with a port list (its pins) and a body that connects sub-modules and logic. Designs are trees of modules.

items.front: What does a continuous `assign` statement do over time?
items.back: It is a permanent connection, not a one-time run. The LHS continuously tracks the RHS expression, updating after propagation delay, exactly like a real gate.

items.front: What is the simulate-then-synthesize workflow?
items.back: Simulation runs the HDL as an event model to verify behavior; synthesis maps the synthesizable subset onto real LUTs/flip-flops. It mirrors test-driven development.
items.hint: Two separate tools, two purposes.

items.front: Why is 'an always block is a function call' a dangerous analogy?
items.back: Because it isn't a call. always blocks describe persistent hardware that reacts to events forever; treating HDL as sequential/procedural software leads to wrong designs.

items.prompt: What is the most accurate description of what Verilog code represents?
choices: A sequence of CPU instructions executed top-to-bottom
A structural description of parallel hardware
A scripting language for configuring a CPU
A purely mathematical proof of correctness
items.explanation: HDL describes hardware structure that all exists simultaneously; there is no program counter or sequential execution at module scope.

items.prompt: Why is VHDL literacy specifically valuable for this learner?
choices: It is faster to simulate than Verilog
It is the only synthesizable HDL
Defense, aerospace, and radar shops commonly use it
It compiles to smaller bitstreams
items.explanation: VHDL is entrenched in defense/aerospace/radar [->08], so reading it opens those doors even when you write Verilog day-to-day.

items.prompt: In `assign y = a & b;`, when does `y` change?
choices: Only once, when simulation starts
Only when the module is explicitly called
Continuously, whenever a or b changes (after delay)
Only on the next clock edge
items.explanation: A continuous assignment is a permanent connection; y tracks a&b at all times, like a physical AND gate, with no clock involved.

lessons.title: Modules, Ports, and wire vs reg
lessons.content:
## Modules, ports, and `wire` vs `reg`

A module's interface is its **port list**: `input`, `output`, or `inout`, each optionally a vector (bus). Vectors use a `[msb:lsb]` range:

```verilog
module adder #(parameter W = 8)
  (input  [W-1:0] a, b,
   input          cin,
   output [W-1:0] sum,
   output         cout);
  assign {cout, sum} = a + b + cin;
endmodule
```

`{cout, sum}` is **concatenation** — here it captures the carry-out into the extra top bit. `#(parameter W=8)` makes the module *sized at instantiation*, the HDL answer to generics/templates.

Now the classic confusion: **`wire` vs `reg`**. These are not "combinational vs sequential" — they are about *who drives the signal*. A `wire` is driven continuously, by an `assign` or by a module output. A `reg` is a variable assigned *inside a procedural* (`always`/`initial`) block. A `reg` does **not** imply a hardware register; an `always @(*)` block assigning a `reg` synthesizes to pure combinational logic. The name is a historical wart.

The rule of thumb:
- Left side of `assign`, or a module output port left unconnected procedurally -> `wire`.
- Anything assigned inside an `always`/`initial` -> `reg`.

SystemVerilog fixes the wart with **`logic`**, a single 4-state type usable in both contexts; you simply stop worrying about `wire`/`reg` and write `logic`. Ports default to `wire`/`logic` on input; outputs you assign procedurally must be `reg`/`logic`. Vectors slice naturally: `bus[3:0]`, `bus[i]`, and the literal width syntax `4'b1010` (4 bits, binary) or `8'hFF` (8 bits, hex) keeps sizes explicit — vital, because mismatched widths silently zero-extend or truncate.

items.front: What declares a module port and how are buses sized?
items.back: `input`/`output`/`inout`, optionally a vector with a `[msb:lsb]` range, e.g. `input [7:0] a`. The range gives the bus width.

items.front: What does `#(parameter W=8)` do?
items.back: It declares a parameter so the module is sized at instantiation, the HDL equivalent of a generic/template. Override it per instance to reuse the module at any width.
items.hint: Think C++ template / generic.

items.front: What does the concatenation `{cout, sum} = a + b + cin;` accomplish?
items.back: It joins signals into one wider bus; here the carry-out lands in the extra top bit while the lower bits hold the sum, capturing overflow cleanly.

items.front: What truly distinguishes `wire` from `reg` in Verilog?
items.back: Who drives it, NOT comb vs seq. A wire is continuously driven (assign/module output); a reg is assigned inside a procedural always/initial block.
items.hint: It is about the driver, not about clocking.

items.front: Does declaring something `reg` create a hardware register?
items.back: No. A reg assigned in `always @(*)` synthesizes to pure combinational logic. 'reg' is a historical naming wart, not a flip-flop guarantee.

items.front: What does SystemVerilog `logic` solve?
items.back: It is a single 4-state type usable in both continuous and procedural contexts, so you stop choosing between wire and reg and just write logic.

items.front: Why are sized literals like `4'b1010` and `8'hFF` important?
items.back: They make bit widths explicit. Mismatched widths silently zero-extend or truncate, a quiet source of bugs; explicit sizing prevents it.

items.prompt: Which statement about `wire` vs `reg` is correct?
choices: reg always means a flip-flop in hardware
wire is for combinational, reg is for sequential logic
The distinction is which one is procedurally assigned
wire can only be 1 bit wide
items.explanation: The split is about how a signal is driven: continuous (wire) vs assigned inside a procedural block (reg). A reg in always @(*) is combinational.

items.prompt: A signal assigned inside an `always @(*)` block must be declared as:
choices: wire
reg (or logic)
input
parameter
items.explanation: Anything assigned in a procedural block must be reg (or SystemVerilog logic); wire is only for continuous assignment / port connections.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of `parameter` in a module header?
choices: To declare a clock signal
To make the module sized/configurable at instantiation
To force latch inference
To mark the module as simulation-only
items.explanation: Parameters (Verilog) / generics (VHDL) let one module be reused at different widths or constants, set when the module is instantiated.

lessons.title: Combinational vs Sequential: = , <= , and Why It Matters
lessons.content:
## Combinational vs sequential: `=`, `<=`, and the rules

This lesson is the heart of the sub-topic and the source of the field's #1 trap. There are two procedural block styles.

**Combinational** logic uses `always @(*)` (or a continuous `assign`). The `(*)` is an *automatic complete sensitivity list* — it re-evaluates whenever any input changes. Inside, use **blocking** assignments (`=`), which execute in order like software, so intermediate temporaries read back immediately:

```verilog
always @(*) begin          // pure combinational
  if (sel) y = a;
  else     y = b;          // every branch assigns y
end
```

**Sequential** logic uses `always @(posedge clk)` and must use **non-blocking** assignments (`<=`):

```verilog
always @(posedge clk) begin
  q1 <= d;                 // all RHS sampled, THEN
  q2 <= q1;                // all LHS updated together
end
```

**Why `<=` matters:** non-blocking assignments evaluate *all right-hand sides* using the values *at the clock edge*, then update *all left-hand sides* simultaneously. That is exactly what a bank of flip-flops does — every FF samples its input at the edge at once. The code above is a 2-stage shift register. Rewrite it with blocking `=` and `q2 = q1` reads the *already-updated* `q1`, collapsing two flip-flops into one: it may simulate "fine" yet synthesize wrong, or create a simulation race versus synthesis mismatch.

**The discipline, memorized:**
- Clocked (`posedge clk`) block -> **non-blocking `<=`**.
- Combinational (`always @(*)`) block -> **blocking `=`**.
- Never assign the same signal from two different `always` blocks.

This rule comes from Cliff Cummings' SNUG papers and is non-negotiable. Drill it until it is reflex; it is *the* HDL rust-knocker.

items.front: What does `always @(*)` mean and what assignment goes in it?
items.back: It is a combinational block with an automatic complete sensitivity list (re-evaluates on any input change). Use blocking `=` assignments inside.
items.hint: Combinational; the (*) builds the list for you.

items.front: What block and assignment operator model a flip-flop?
items.back: `always @(posedge clk)` with non-blocking `<=` assignments. This is the only correct way to infer edge-triggered registers.

items.front: WHY must clocked blocks use non-blocking `<=`?
items.back: Non-blocking samples ALL right-hand sides at the clock edge, then updates ALL left-hand sides together, exactly mirroring a bank of flip-flops sampling at once.
items.hint: Real flip-flops all sample simultaneously.

items.front: What happens if you use blocking `=` in `q1=d; q2=q1;` inside a clocked block?
items.back: q2 reads the already-updated q1, collapsing a 2-stage shift register into a single flip-flop, and can cause sim-vs-synthesis mismatch / races.

items.front: Why do blocking assignments behave 'like software' in a comb block?
items.back: They execute in order, so an intermediate temporary is read back immediately by later lines, which is what you want for combinational data flow.

items.front: State the memorized blocking/non-blocking discipline.
items.back: Clocked posedge block -> non-blocking <=. Combinational always @(*) -> blocking =. Never drive the same signal from two always blocks.
items.hint: Two lines plus one prohibition.

items.front: Why can the wrong `=`/`<=` choice be especially insidious?
items.back: It can simulate 'fine' while synthesizing to different hardware (or create a simulation race), so the bug hides until hardware behaves unexpectedly.

items.front: Whose practitioner notes codified the non-blocking rule?
items.back: Cliff Cummings' SNUG papers on non-blocking assignments; they are the definitive reference for the discipline.
items.hint: SNUG conference papers.

items.prompt: Inside `always @(posedge clk)`, which assignment operator is correct?
choices: = (blocking)
<= (non-blocking)
assign
items.explanation: Clocked blocks model flip-flops; non-blocking <= samples all RHS at the edge then updates all LHS together, matching simultaneous FF behavior.

items.prompt: Why does `q1<=d; q2<=q1;` correctly build a 2-stage shift register?
choices: Because <= updates q1 before q2 reads it
Because both RHS use the OLD values, sampled at the edge, then update together
Because = would be even more correct here
Because @(*) rebuilds the sensitivity list
items.explanation: Non-blocking evaluates q1 and q2's RHS using pre-edge values, then updates both, so q2 gets the OLD q1 and two real flip-flops are inferred.

items.prompt: Using blocking `=` for `q1=d; q2=q1;` in a clocked block typically yields:
choices: A correct 2-stage shift register
A single flip-flop, because q2 reads the new q1
An inferred latch
A syntax error
items.explanation: Blocking executes in order, so q2 reads the already-updated q1, collapsing both stages into one register, often with sim/synth mismatch.

items.prompt: Which is the correct operator choice by block type?
choices: Blocking in clocked, non-blocking in combinational
Non-blocking everywhere
Non-blocking in clocked, blocking in combinational
Blocking everywhere
items.explanation: The discipline: non-blocking <= in posedge clk blocks, blocking = in always @(*) combinational blocks. This is the #1 HDL rust-knocker.

items.prompt: Mixing up `=` and `<=` is dangerous mainly because:
choices: It always produces a compile error caught immediately
It can simulate fine yet synthesize to different hardware
It changes the FPGA pinout
It only affects VHDL, not Verilog
items.explanation: The failure is silent: behavior may match in simulation but diverge in synthesized hardware (or create a race), hiding the bug until late.

lessons.title: FSMs, Inferred Latches, Testbenches, and SystemVerilog
lessons.content:
## FSMs, latches, testbenches, and SystemVerilog

**Inferred latches** are the second great trap. In a combinational `always @(*)`, if a signal is *not* assigned on every path, the synthesizer must "remember" its old value — it infers a transparent **latch**, which wrecks timing and is almost never intended. The fix: assign every output a **default first**, and complete every `if` with an `else` and every `case` with `default`.

```verilog
always @(*) begin
  y = 1'b0;          // default kills latch inference
  if (sel) y = a;    // no else needed now
end
```

The **three-block FSM template** (from Module 7) is the canonical sequential pattern:

```verilog
// 1) state register (clocked, non-blocking)
always @(posedge clk)
  if (rst) state <= IDLE;
  else     state <= next;
// 2) next-state logic (combinational, default + case)
always @(*) begin
  next = state;            // default avoids a latch
  case (state)
    IDLE: if (go) next = RUN;
    RUN:  next = IDLE;
  endcase
end
// 3) output logic (combinational)
assign busy = (state == RUN);
```

**Testbenches** are simulation-only and may use non-synthesizable constructs freely: `initial` blocks, `#10` delays, `$display`/`$monitor`, and self-checking `if (got !== exp) $error(...)`. Drive stimulus, compare against expected, and view waveforms in GTKWave. Never put `#delays` or `initial` for state in *synthesizable* RTL — that is the **synthesizable vs sim-only** line.

**SystemVerilog** makes intent explicit and lets the tool *enforce* it: `always_ff @(posedge clk)` flags accidental combinational use, `always_comb` auto-builds the sensitivity list *and warns on inferred latches*, and `logic` replaces `wire`/`reg`. Use them on every new design.

items.front: What is an inferred latch and why is it bad?
items.back: In a comb always @(*), if a signal isn't assigned on every path, the tool keeps its old value by inferring a transparent latch, wrecking timing; almost always unintended.
items.hint: Happens with incomplete if/case.

items.front: How do you prevent inferred latches?
items.back: Assign every output a default value first, and complete every `if` with `else` and every `case` with `default` so every path drives the signal.

items.front: Name the three blocks of the canonical HDL FSM template.
items.back: 1) clocked state register (state <= next), 2) combinational next-state logic (default + case), 3) combinational/registered output logic.
items.hint: Register, next-state, output.

items.front: Why is `next = state;` placed first in the next-state block?
items.back: It is a default assignment so that any unlisted case path still drives `next`, preventing an inferred latch on the next-state signal.

items.front: What constructs are legal in a testbench but NOT in synthesizable RTL?
items.back: `initial` blocks for state, `#delays`, `$display`/`$monitor`, and `$error`. They are simulation-only; using them as hardware state crosses the synth/sim line.

items.front: What makes a testbench 'self-checking'?
items.back: It compares the DUT output against an expected value and flags mismatches automatically, e.g. `if (got !== exp) $error(...)`, instead of relying on eyeballing waveforms.

items.front: What does SystemVerilog `always_comb` add over `always @(*)`?
items.back: It auto-builds the sensitivity list AND makes the tool warn/error on inferred latches and accidental sequential use, enforcing combinational intent.
items.hint: It enforces the intent the name states.

items.front: What does `always_ff @(posedge clk)` enforce?
items.back: It declares intent to model flip-flops; the tool flags any accidental combinational coding, catching mistakes the plain `always` keyword would silently allow.

items.prompt: An inferred latch most commonly results from:
choices: Using <= in a clocked block
An incomplete if/case in a combinational block (a path leaves a signal unassigned)
Declaring a port as inout
Using a parameter
items.explanation: When a comb always @(*) leaves a signal unassigned on some path, the synthesizer must remember its old value, inferring an unintended transparent latch.

items.prompt: Which technique most reliably avoids inferred latches?
choices: Adding more clocks
Assigning every signal a default value at the top of the comb block
Using blocking assignments in clocked blocks
Removing the sensitivity list
items.explanation: A leading default (plus full else/default) guarantees every path drives the signal, so no latch is inferred. always_comb also warns when you slip.

items.prompt: In the three-block FSM template, the state register block should use:
choices: assign
blocking = in always @(*)
non-blocking <= in always @(posedge clk)
an initial block
items.explanation: The state register is sequential: clocked always @(posedge clk) with non-blocking <=. Next-state and output logic are the combinational blocks.

items.prompt: Which construct belongs ONLY in a testbench, never in synthesizable RTL?
choices: assign
always @(posedge clk)
`#10` time delays and `$display`
case statements
items.explanation: `#delays`, $display/$monitor, and initial-for-state are simulation-only. Synthesizable RTL must avoid them; that is the synth-vs-sim boundary.

items.prompt: What is the chief advantage of SystemVerilog `always_comb` / `always_ff`?
choices: They run faster in simulation
They let the tool enforce your combinational/sequential intent and catch inferred latches
They eliminate the need for clocks
They make wire and reg interchangeable in all contexts
items.explanation: always_comb auto-builds the sensitivity list and warns on inferred latches; always_ff flags accidental combinational coding. They encode and enforce intent.

lessons.title: The FPGA Fabric: What's Actually on the Die
lessons.content:
An **FPGA** (Field-Programmable Gate Array) is not a fixed chip — it is a sea of **configurable resources** wired together by a programmable interconnect, all defined at power-up by a loaded **bitstream**. Where a CPU executes instructions sequentially, an FPGA *becomes* your circuit: every block runs in parallel, every clock cycle, deterministically.

The building blocks you must know:

- **Configurable logic blocks (CLBs / logic cells / slices / ALMs).** The atom of the fabric is a **LUT + flip-flop**. A $k$-input **lookup table (LUT)** is a tiny $2^k\times 1$ SRAM that stores the truth table of *any* Boolean function of $k$ inputs (typically $k=4$ to $6$). Its output optionally feeds a **flip-flop** so the cell can be combinational or registered. This is *LUT-based* logic, not gate-based — synthesis maps your design into LUT contents.
- **Block RAM (BRAM).** Dedicated dual-port SRAM blocks (tens of kbits each) for buffers, FIFOs, and sample memory — far denser than building RAM out of flip-flops.
- **DSP slices.** Hardened **multiply-accumulate (MAC)** units (e.g., $18\times 18$ or $27\times 18$ multipliers with an accumulator). These are the workhorses for filters and FFTs.
- **Clock management (PLL/MMCM).** Synthesize, phase-shift, and deskew clocks from a board crystal.
- **Configurable I/O.** Banked I/O supporting many standards (LVCMOS, LVDS, etc.) with per-pin drive and termination.

Knowing the fabric is knowing your budget: every design is a placement of LUTs, FFs, BRAMs, and DSPs onto fixed silicon.

items.front: What is the fundamental logic element of an FPGA fabric, and what are its two parts?
items.back: The configurable logic cell, built from a **LUT + flip-flop**. The $k$-input lookup table (a $2^k\times1$ SRAM) computes any Boolean function of $k$ inputs; the optional flip-flop registers the output, making the cell combinational or sequential. Thousands of these plus a programmable interconnect form the fabric.
items.hint: Lookup table feeds an optional register.

items.front: How does a LUT implement an arbitrary logic function?
items.back: A $k$-input LUT is a $2^k$-entry SRAM addressed by the $k$ inputs; each entry holds the function's output for that input combination — literally the truth table. The bitstream loads the SRAM contents, so the same physical LUT can be AND, XOR, a 3-input majority, etc. This is why FPGA logic is 'LUT-based,' not fixed gates.
items.hint: It stores the truth table in SRAM addressed by the inputs.

items.front: What are DSP slices and why do they exist as hardened blocks?
items.back: DSP slices are dedicated, hardened **multiply-accumulate (MAC)** units (e.g., an $18\times18$ or $27\times18$ multiplier with a wide accumulator and pre-adder). Building a fast multiplier out of LUTs is slow and area-hungry, so vendors harden them — giving high clock rates and low power for the multiply-heavy math (FIR, FFT, correlation) that dominates DSP.
items.hint: Hardened multipliers + accumulators for the MAC math.

items.front: What is block RAM (BRAM) and when do you use it instead of flip-flops or distributed RAM?
items.back: BRAM is dedicated dual-port SRAM (typically tens of kbits per block) built into the fabric. Use it for anything sizeable: sample buffers, line/frame stores, FIFOs, coefficient tables, and CDC async FIFOs. Building the same storage from flip-flops or LUT-based 'distributed RAM' wastes huge fabric resources and won't reach the density or speed.
items.hint: Dedicated dual-port SRAM for buffers and FIFOs.

items.prompt: A 6-input LUT can implement how many distinct entries (and thus any function of 6 inputs)?
choices: 64 entries ($2^6$)
6 entries
12 entries
36 entries
items.explanation: A $k$-input LUT is a $2^k\times1$ SRAM, so a 6-LUT has $2^6=64$ entries — the full truth table of any 6-input Boolean function. Picking $k=6$ balances logic density against the SRAM cost of $2^k$ bits per LUT.

items.prompt: Which on-chip resource is the right choice to buffer a 16k-sample block of ADC data inside the FPGA?
choices: Block RAM (BRAM)
A bank of individual flip-flops
DSP slices
The configurable I/O ring
items.explanation: BRAM is dedicated dual-port SRAM sized exactly for buffers and FIFOs. Sixteen thousand samples in flip-flops would consume an enormous share of the fabric; DSP slices do arithmetic, not bulk storage; I/O is for pins.

items.prompt: What configures an FPGA's logic the moment it powers up?
choices: A bitstream loaded into the fabric's configuration SRAM
A compiled C program in flash executed by a CPU
Permanent metal mask layers set at the foundry
The board's crystal oscillator frequency
items.explanation: SRAM-based FPGAs hold their LUT contents, routing, and block settings in configuration SRAM, loaded from a bitstream at power-up (from flash or JTAG). It's volatile — remove power and the configuration is gone, which is why a config flash sits next to most FPGAs.

items.prompt: The clearest architectural difference between an FPGA and a CPU is that the FPGA:
choices: Physically instantiates spatial, parallel hardware that all runs every clock, rather than time-multiplexing one datapath across an instruction stream
Runs software faster because it has a higher clock frequency
Stores its program in DRAM instead of cache
Can only implement combinational logic, never sequential
items.explanation: An FPGA spends area to gain parallelism: you lay down as many MACs, FIFOs, and state machines as fit, and they all operate concurrently and deterministically each cycle. A CPU reuses one datapath sequentially. FPGAs usually clock *slower* than CPUs but win on throughput-per-clock and latency determinism.

items.front: A logic cell: LUT + flip-flop
items.back: A k-input LUT (an SRAM truth table) computes any Boolean function of its inputs; an output mux selects either the raw combinational LUT output or a registered version from the flip-flop (clocked by clk). Thousands of these cells plus a programmable interconnect make up the FPGA fabric.

lessons.title: The Implementation Flow: HDL to Bitstream
lessons.content:
Getting your HDL onto silicon is a pipeline of distinct tool stages. Treat it like a compiler-and-linker chain for hardware.

1. **Design entry (HDL).** Write synthesizable Verilog/VHDL (RTL) plus the IP you instantiate.
2. **Simulation.** Before touching the fabric, verify behavior with a **testbench** (Icarus/Verilator/vendor sim). Catching bugs here is orders of magnitude cheaper than on hardware.
3. **Synthesis.** The tool elaborates RTL and maps it to fabric primitives — LUTs, FFs, BRAM, DSP — and does logic optimization (the K-map/minimization work, automated). Output: a technology-mapped netlist.
4. **Place & route (P&R, 'implementation').** Assign each primitive to a physical site (place) and connect them through the routing fabric (route). This is the hard, slow, NP-ish step; routing congestion and physical distance set your achievable speed.
5. **Static timing analysis (STA).** Against your **timing constraints**, the tool checks every path's setup and hold using post-route delays and reports **slack**. This ties straight back to the setup/hold and $f_{max}$ math from the timing module — now with real numbers.
6. **Bitstream generation.** Serialize the placed-and-routed configuration into the binary the chip loads.
7. **Configuration.** Download the bitstream to the board (JTAG for bring-up, SPI flash for boot).

Along the way you read two report families constantly: the **utilization report** (how much LUT/FF/BRAM/DSP you used) and the **timing report** (did every path close?). Both gate whether the design is shippable.

items.front: List the FPGA implementation flow in order, from HDL to a configured board.
items.back: HDL design entry, then simulation, then synthesis (map RTL to LUT/FF/BRAM/DSP), then place & route, then static timing analysis (STA), then bitstream generation, then configuration (download to the board). Reports of utilization and timing are read throughout.
items.hint: Entry, sim, synth, P&R, STA, bitstream, config.

items.front: What does synthesis actually produce, and what does it optimize?
items.back: Synthesis elaborates RTL and maps it to fabric primitives (LUTs, flip-flops, BRAM, DSP slices), producing a technology-mapped netlist. It performs logic optimization automatically — the Boolean minimization and state-encoding work you'd do by hand with K-maps — and infers blocks like RAMs and multipliers from your coding patterns.
items.hint: RTL becomes a netlist of fabric primitives, with logic minimized.

items.front: What happens during place & route (P&R), and why is it the slow, hard step?
items.back: **Place** assigns each netlist primitive to a specific physical site on the die; **route** connects them through the fixed programmable interconnect. It's a large combinatorial optimization (NP-hard in general): the placer must minimize wire length and congestion while the router must find legal paths that also meet timing. Physical distance and routing delay ultimately cap your clock speed.
items.hint: Assign primitives to sites and wire them up — a huge optimization problem.

items.front: Why simulate before synthesizing, given you'll test on hardware anyway?
items.back: Simulation with a testbench gives full observability and fast iteration: you see every signal, can force inputs, and catch logic bugs in seconds. A full synth/P&R/bitstream cycle can take minutes to hours, and on-board debug has limited visibility (you only see what you route to a probe/ILA). Fixing a bug in sim is orders of magnitude cheaper than after implementation.
items.hint: Full observability and fast iteration vs slow builds and limited on-chip visibility.

items.prompt: In which stage are post-route, real interconnect delays used to verify that every path meets setup and hold?
choices: Static timing analysis (STA)
Synthesis
Behavioral simulation
Bitstream generation
items.explanation: STA runs after place & route, when actual cell and routing delays are known, and checks every timing path against the constraints, reporting slack. Behavioral simulation has no real delays; synthesis only estimates; bitstream generation just serializes the result.

items.prompt: Your design synthesizes and places fine but the timing report shows several paths failing. The most direct interpretation is:
choices: Those paths have negative slack — they can't meet the constraint at the requested clock, so the design is not guaranteed to work at speed
The design used too many LUTs
The bitstream is corrupt
The testbench is wrong
items.explanation: Failing timing paths have negative slack: required time minus arrival time is negative, meaning data doesn't settle before the capturing edge. The design may work intermittently or not at all at that clock. Fixes: pipeline the path, restructure logic, relax the clock, or fix constraints.

items.prompt: What is the role of a bitstream file in the flow?
choices: It is the serialized binary that loads LUT contents, routing, and block settings into the FPGA's configuration memory
It is the human-readable HDL source for the design
It is the timing report summarizing slack on each path
It is the testbench stimulus used in simulation
items.explanation: The bitstream encodes the entire placed-and-routed configuration — every LUT truth table, routing switch, and block setting. Loading it (via JTAG or from a config flash) is what turns the generic fabric into your specific circuit.

items.prompt: Two reports you consult on essentially every build are:
choices: The utilization report and the timing report
The schematic and the BOM
The datasheet and the errata
The power-on log and the crash dump
items.explanation: Utilization tells you how much LUT/FF/BRAM/DSP you've consumed (are you about to overflow the part?), and timing tells you whether every path closes (will it run at the target clock?). Together they decide whether the design fits and runs.

items.front: FPGA implementation flow
items.back: The flow runs HDL entry, then simulation against a testbench, then synthesis, then place & route, then static timing analysis (STA), then bitstream generation, then configuring the board. STA reports slack; negative slack fails timing and feeds back to fixing the RTL or the constraints.

lessons.title: Constraints, Slack, Utilization, and IP
lessons.content:
Two text artifacts and one library decide whether a design is real: **constraints**, **reports**, and **IP**.

**Timing constraints (.xdc for Vivado, .sdc for Quartus/SDC tools).** STA has no idea how fast you intend to run unless you tell it. At minimum you `create_clock` with a period; you also specify **input/output delays** (when external data is valid relative to a clock at the pins) and any false/multicycle paths. Garbage constraints give garbage timing sign-off — a design that 'meets timing' against no clock means nothing.

**Slack.** For each path, $\text{slack} = \text{required time} - \text{arrival time}$. **Positive slack** = margin (good). **Negative slack** = a violation. **Worst Negative Slack (WNS)** is the headline number; **Total Negative Slack (TNS)** sums all violations. Setup slack scales with the clock period; **hold slack is frequency-independent** — you can't slow the clock to fix a hold violation (a direct callback to the timing module).

**Utilization.** The report tallies LUTs, FFs, BRAM, and DSP used vs available. Hit ~80–90% and routing congestion spikes, P&R slows, and timing gets harder — leave headroom.

**Pin / physical constraints** map HDL top-level ports to physical package pins (`set_property PACKAGE_PIN`) and I/O standards — buttons, LEDs, PMOD, the clock input. Wrong pin assignment is the classic 'LED won't blink' bug.

**IP: hard vs soft.** **Hard IP** is fixed silicon (PCIe transceivers, DDR controllers, the PLL/MMCM, DSP) — fast and power-efficient, but fixed. **Soft IP** is synthesized into the fabric from HDL — flexible, costs LUTs. A **soft CPU** (RISC-V, MicroBlaze, Nios) is soft IP: a whole processor built in the fabric, letting you run C alongside custom accelerators.

items.front: What is a timing constraints file and what is the single most important thing it must declare?
items.back: It's a text file (.xdc for Vivado, .sdc for Quartus) telling STA the design's timing intent. The essential entry is the **clock**: `create_clock` with a period (and waveform). Without it STA has nothing to check against — 'meeting timing' against no clock is meaningless. It also sets input/output delays and false/multicycle paths.
items.hint: Defines clocks and I/O timing for STA; clock definition is mandatory.

items.front: Define slack, WNS, and TNS.
items.back: **Slack** = required time minus arrival time for a path; positive is margin, negative is a violation. **WNS** (Worst Negative Slack) is the single most-violating path's slack — the headline. **TNS** (Total Negative Slack) sums all negative slacks, indicating how many paths fail and how badly. You sign off timing when WNS $\ge 0$.
items.hint: Slack = required - arrival; WNS = worst path; TNS = sum of all violations.

items.front: Contrast hard IP and soft IP, with examples.
items.back: **Hard IP** is dedicated silicon: PCIe/SerDes transceivers, DDR memory controllers, PLL/MMCM, DSP slices — fast, power-efficient, but fixed in capability and location. **Soft IP** is HDL synthesized into the fabric (UARTs, AXI interconnect, FIR cores, a soft CPU) — fully flexible and portable across parts, but it consumes LUTs/FFs and runs at fabric speeds.
items.hint: Hard = fixed silicon block; soft = synthesized into LUTs.

items.front: What is a soft-core CPU, and why drop one into an FPGA?
items.back: A soft CPU (RISC-V, Xilinx MicroBlaze, Intel Nios) is a full processor described in HDL and synthesized into the fabric. You use it for control-plane and housekeeping tasks (config, slow protocols, decision logic) written in C, while the fabric does the heavy parallel DSP. It gives software flexibility without an external MCU, and RISC-V makes it open and license-friendly.
items.hint: A processor built from fabric logic; runs C for control while hardware does DSP.

items.prompt: STA reports a hold-time violation. Which action can fix it?
choices: Add delay on the short path or fix clock skew/routing — slowing the clock will NOT help
Lower the clock frequency
Increase the clock frequency
Reduce LUT utilization
items.explanation: Hold violations are frequency-independent: $t_{cq}+t_{pd,min} \ge t_h + \text{skew}$ has no clock period in it. Slowing the clock fixes setup, never hold. You fix hold by adding delay to the too-fast path or correcting skew — usually the tools do it automatically by inserting routing.

items.prompt: A path has required time 8.0 ns and arrival time 9.3 ns. Its slack is:
choices: -1.3 ns (a setup violation)
+1.3 ns (passing with margin)
0 ns exactly
items.explanation: Slack = required - arrival = 8.0 - 9.3 = -1.3 ns. Negative slack means data arrives after it was required (too late for the capturing edge): a setup violation. Pipeline the path, restructure logic, or relax the clock.

items.prompt: Your HDL is correct in simulation but the on-board LED never lights. The most likely cause is:
choices: A wrong or missing pin/physical constraint mapping the top-level port to the LED's package pin (or wrong I/O standard)
The synthesis tool minimized the LED logic away on purpose
BRAM is full
The clock constraint period is too long
items.explanation: Simulation doesn't know about physical pins; only the constraints file maps HDL ports to package pins and I/O standards. A wrong PACKAGE_PIN, missing constraint, or incorrect I/O standard/voltage is the classic reason hardware behavior diverges from a perfect simulation.

items.prompt: Pushing fabric utilization toward 90% typically causes:
choices: Routing congestion that slows place & route and makes timing closure harder
Lower power and faster timing automatically
The bitstream to shrink proportionally
Hold violations to disappear
items.explanation: As the device fills, the router has fewer free tracks and must take longer detours, increasing delay and congestion. P&R runtime balloons and timing closure becomes fragile. Good practice is to leave headroom (often target well under ~80% for timing-critical designs).

items.front: Why are PLL/MMCM blocks usually hard IP rather than soft?
items.back: Clock generation needs analog/mixed-signal precision (voltage-controlled oscillator, phase detector, low jitter) that can't be built from LUTs. So vendors harden the PLL/MMCM as dedicated silicon you configure via a clocking wizard. It multiplies/divides the board crystal, phase-shifts, and deskews clocks — feeding the global clock network with low jitter.
items.hint: Clock synthesis is analog/mixed-signal; LUTs can't do it, so it's hardened.

lessons.title: FPGA vs MCU/ASIC/GPU, Ecosystems, and Why Radar/SDR Lives on FPGAs
lessons.content:
**Choosing the right silicon.** Four contenders, four sweet spots:

- **MCU:** cheapest, lowest power, easiest (C firmware), but a single sequential core — fine for control and modest sampling, poor for sustained high-rate DSP. **[->04]**
- **GPU:** thousands of cores, huge throughput on batched/floating-point workloads, but high power and **high, variable latency** (batches, memory hierarchy, OS) — great offline, awkward for hard-real-time streaming.
- **ASIC:** ultimate performance/watt and unit cost at volume, but enormous **NRE** (mask/design cost) and zero flexibility once taped out — only sane at very high volume.
- **FPGA:** reconfigurable parallel hardware with **deterministic, low latency** and line-rate throughput, no per-design mask cost, reprogrammable in the field. Wins for low-to-mid volume, evolving standards, and hard-real-time streaming DSP.

**Vendor ecosystems.** AMD/Xilinx → **Vivado** (Artix/Kintex/Zynq; Arty, Basys 3 boards). Intel/Altera → **Quartus** (Cyclone/Arria/Stratix). Lattice and small parts → the **open-source flow**: **Yosys** (synthesis) + **nextpnr** (P&R) + project IceStorm/Trellis (iCE40/ECP5; iCEBreaker, ULX3S). Open tools mean no license wall and scriptable, reproducible builds.

**Why FPGAs dominate radar/SDR.** Modern receivers digitize at hundreds of MS/s to GS/s, producing a firehose no single CPU can keep up with at low latency. FPGAs map the math spatially: arrays of **DSP-slice MACs** compute **FIR filtering**, **pulse compression** (matched filtering / correlation), **FFTs**, and **digital beamforming** every clock, in parallel, at line rate with deterministic latency. You instantiate, say, 128 taps as 128 MACs that all fire each cycle — the literal building block of a radar signal chain. That parallel-MAC-at-line-rate property, plus reconfigurability for evolving waveforms, is exactly why FPGAs (often alongside a soft or hard CPU on a Zynq-class device) are the centerpiece of contemporary radar and SDR — and why the skill is in demand. **[->08]**

items.front: When does an FPGA beat an MCU, and when does the MCU win?
items.back: FPGA wins when you need sustained high-throughput parallel processing with deterministic low latency (streaming DSP, line-rate filtering, custom protocols). The MCU wins for cost, power, and developer-speed on control-plane and modest workloads — sequential C is far easier than HDL. Many systems use both: MCU/soft-CPU for control, fabric for the heavy parallel math.
items.hint: FPGA = parallel/deterministic/high-rate; MCU = cheap/low-power/easy/sequential.

items.front: Compare FPGA vs ASIC on the axes that actually drive the decision.
items.back: ASIC gives the best performance-per-watt and lowest unit cost — but only after huge **NRE** (mask and design cost) and with **zero post-tapeout flexibility**. FPGA has higher per-unit cost and power but **near-zero NRE**, field reprogrammability, and fast iteration. ASIC pays off only at very high volume or extreme perf/power needs; FPGA wins for low-to-mid volume and evolving requirements.
items.hint: ASIC: low unit cost/high NRE/fixed. FPGA: higher unit cost/no NRE/reprogrammable.
